weddings.text: A "secret" one of these events titles Domenico Cimarosa's ("chee-mah-ROH-zah's") most famous opera. During one of these events, a baritone character opens a fake love letter that arranges a tryst "under the pines in the grove"; that letter was written by two sopranos in the duet "Sull'aria." After one of these events, Lucia kills Arturo and enters the Great Hall soaked in blood, beginning her "mad scene." After learning the title character is their long-lost son, (*) Marcellina ("mar-cheh-LEE-nah") and Bartolo ("BAR-toh-loh") participate in a "double" one of these events. In an opera titled for this kind of event, the Countess disguises herself as her maid Susanna to trick the unfaithful Count Almaviva. For 10 points, name this kind of event that titles a Mozart opera about Figaro.

The opening of this opera's prelude was plagiarized by Smetana for the dance of the mermaids in "The Moldau." For 10 points each: The most famous excerpt from Lohengrin is a "Chorus" that's often used as a processional at these occasions. Mendelssohn's incidental music to A Midsummer Night's Dream includes a "March" celebrating one of these events.

nixon in china.text: The title character of this opera was James Maddalena's signature role. At the start of Act III of this opera, a heldentenor muses, "I am no one," before his coloratura soprano wife gets him to descend from a giant portrait of himself. The title baritone of this opera remarks how "The whole world was listening!" during a handshake in this opera's Act I aria (*) "News has a kind of mystery." An "out-take" from this opera is subtitled "Foxtrot for Orchestra." Act I of this opera begins with the landing of the aircraft Spirit of '76, and Act II ends with an interrupted performance of The Red Detachment of Women. For 10 points, name this 1987 John Adams opera about a US president's visit to a country led by Chairman Mao.

Answer the following about operas by John Adams, for 10 points each. This first opera by Adams begins with the arrival of a plane carrying the title president, whose wife receives a small glass elephant and sings the aria "This is prophetic" in the Summer Palace.

One character in this opera interrupts a performance to help a character she thinks is being whipped to death. The orchestra for this opera is strengthened by two pianos and four saxophones, among others. The title character of this opera sings "News has a kind of mystery" when contemplating the effects of his actions, and that character's wife sings (*) "This is prophetic" while imagining her future after visiting a children's school. A chorus sings "The Three Main Rules of Discipline" at the beginning of this opera in which the main characters watch a performance of The Red Detachment of Women. For 10 points, beginning with the landing of the plane the Spirit of '76, name this minimalist opera about a US president's international visit by John Adams.

isolde.text: In 2026, Lise Davidsen ("Lisa DAH-vid-sen") made her much-awaited debut in this role, who ends the opera as she "sinks gently, as if transfigured" onto her lover's dead body as the orchestra holds a B major chord. In her Act I "Narrative and Curse," this character enlists her maid Brangane ("bron-GAY-nuh") to prepare a poison. This woman remarks how "softly and gently" her dead lover shines at the start of a scene in which a chord spelled as (*) "F, B, D-sharp, and G-sharp" is finally resolved in her "love-death," or "Liebestod" ("LEE-bus-toht"). In a pioneering 1865 opera, this woman is engaged to King Marke but falls in love with a heldentenor after drinking a love potion. For 10 points, name this Irish princess in a Wagner opera who loves the Cornish knight Tristan.

The A-flat major tonality of this character's Act II duet recurs in her last aria, which resolves the earlier scene with a B major tonic chord bringing the opera to a close. Wilhelm Furtwangler conducted a famous 1952 recording of this role sung by Kirsten Flagstad, who sang the role seventy-three times at the Metropolitan Opera between 1935 and 1941. The composer paired an orchestral version of this character's final aria with the opera's prelude as a promotional concert piece, which is still popular today. She sings the aria "Mild und leise wie er lachelt" after the arrival of her fiance, King Marke, in a scene commonly referred to as the "Liebestod," meaning her "Love-Death." For 10 points, name this heroine of an Arthurian Wagner opera who falls in love with Tristan.

georges bizet.text: Francesca Zambello's 2006 Royal Opera House production of an opera by this composer starred Anna Caterina Antonacci and Jonas Kaufmann ("YO-nahss KOWF-mahn"). This composer wrote the aria "Je crois entendre encore" ("je CRWAHZ on-TON-dron-COR"), in which a tenor reminisces about meeting a veiled woman. In another tenor aria by this composer, an officer recalls cherishing a "withered" object while in prison. This composer wrote the friendship duet (*) "Au fond du temple saint" ("oh FON du TOM-pluh SAHN") for an opera set in Ceylon. The "Flower Song" is contained in an opera by this composer in which Escamillo's flirtation with the title woman makes Don Jose ("zho-ZAY") jealous. For 10 points, name this composer of The Pearl Fishers who wrote the "Toreador Song" and "Habanera" for Carmen.

Two characters in this opera sing the friendship duet "Dio, che nell'alma infondere" (dee-oh, kay nell-AHL-mah in-fon-DAY-ray). For 10 points each: This man composed The Pearl Fishers. The "Toreador Song" appears in his opera Carmen.

In one of his operas, two characters remember how they both witnessed the appearance of a beautiful veiled woman, and now they notice that their hands push each other apart. One of those characters later burns his village to allow his love interest and his rival to escape execution. This composer adapted material from the carol "March of the Kings" into the "Farandole (fa-run-dole)" that closes his (*) L'Arlesienne (lahr-lay-syen) Suite No. 2. Nadir and Zurga sing this composer's aria "Au fond du temple saint," better known as the "Friendship Duet." Another of his operas features Escamillo singing the "Toreador Song," and ends with Don Jose stabbing the title gypsy girl. For 10 points, name this French composer of The Pearl Fishers and Carmen.
The eternal friendship of two men is threatened when they fall in love with the same woman. For 10 points each: The Pearl Fishers is by this French composer of the incidental music to L'Arlesienne.

In the second act of this opera, a group of women sing the tavern song "Les tringles des sistres tintaient." For 10 points each: Carmen was composed by this Frenchman, whose other operas include The Pearl Fishers.

This character prays to God to protect her in her Act 3 aria "Je dis que rien ne m'epouvante." For 10 points each: This composer of The Pearl Fishers included Micaela's aria and the still more famous "Habanera" in his Carmen.

In Act Three of the opera in which he appears, this character has his hat shot off of his head by his rival, Don Jose. For 10 points each: Escamillo appears in Carmen, an opera by this French composer who wrote an opera about the love triangle between Leila, Nadir, and Zurga, The Pearl Fishers.

One of this composer's operas is based on Alfred de Musset's Namouna. He also completed Fromental Halevy's unfinished opera Noe. He imitated Donizetti's Don Pasquale in an opera which ends with Odoardo marrying Bettina. He wrote the aria "Comme autrefois," in which Leila recalls happier times, for an opera whose characters include a high priest of Brahma named Nourabad. In that opera, Nadir and Zurga sing the duet "Au fond du temple saint." The title character of his most famous opera compares love to a rebellious bird in an Act 1 habanera. In that opera, Escamillo sings an aria usually known as the Toreador Song. For 10 points, name this composer of Don Procopio, The Pearl Fishers and Carmen.

In one opera by this composer, a character sings that the anger inside him has abated like the storm outside in "L'orage s'est calme". A character in one of his operas sings "Comme autrefois dans la nuit sombre" after describing being given a necklace by a fugitive she saved. In another opera by this composer, a character sings a seguidilla about Lillas Pastia's inn. Two friends sing about falling in love with a Brahman priestess at the same moment in his duet, "Au fond du temple saint". In one of his operas, Leila's hand is sought by the title characters, Zurga and Nadir. The title character of one of his operas sings a habanera, and flirts with Escamillo the toreador and with Don Jose. For 10 points, name this French composer of The Pearl Fishers and Carmen.

carmen.text: In this opera, a chorus announces the arrival of a character with the words "Vivat! ("vee-vah") Vivat! Vivat!" during a torch-lit procession. Carrie Cracknell's 2024 Met Opera production of this opera sets it in an industrial town by the U.S.-Mexico border. An aria from this opera opens with the arpeggiated cello ostinato [read slowly] "dotted eighth note D, sixteenth note A, eighth notes F, down to A" and was adapted into a (*) fantasy by Pablo de Sarasate. The duet "C'est toi? C'est moi!" ("say TWAH say MWAH") ends this opera, which features an aria that opens "Votre toast." In this opera, Escamillo sings the Toreador Song and falls in love with a woman who compares love to a rebellious bird. For 10 points, a Habanera is sung by the title Romani woman of what opera by Georges Bizet?

A 2018 production of this opera had the main character enter in a gorilla suit. Maria Pages's ("pah-HESS's") dance adaptation of this opera adds the word "Yo" to the title. Near the end of this opera's prelude, a slow section states the following motif over tremolo D minor triads: [read slowly] "long D, [pause] C-sharp, B-flat, C-sharp, A," followed by two soft timpani strokes. This opera's music was arranged into a percussion-heavy one-act ballet by Rodion Shchedrin ("shed-REEN"). The Hammerstein song "Stan' Up an' Fight" is in an all-Black musical based on this opera, whose 1954 film version stars Harry Belafonte and Dorothy Dandridge. During this opera's closing duet "C'est toi? C'est moi!" ("seh twah? seh mwah!"), the title character returns a ring to her former lover, who then stabs her. For 10 points, name this French opera that ends at a bullfight.

In one duet, a character in this opera receives a letter encouraging him to marry a girl from his village. At the end of this opera's second act, that character is persuaded to follow a group led by Le Dancaire ("dawn-ka-EER") and Le Remendado. During the duet "C'est toi! C'est moi!," a woman in this opera enrages a suitor by tossing a ring back at him. A corporal in this opera defies the orders of his superior Zuniga when he frees a woman's hands after she sings him a seguidilla. While at Lillas Pastia's tavern, that character in this opera sings the aria "La fleur que tu m'avais jetee," or "The flower that you threw at me," declaring his love for a woman who had earlier sung her habanera. For 10 points, Don Jose ("zho-SAY") stabs the title character to death when she rejects him for Escamillo in what Georges Bizet opera?

After this opera's premiere, its composer's mentor Charles Gounod ("goo-NOH") supposedly exclaimed, "take mine out of the score and there remains nothing to [its composer's] credit but the sauce that masks the fish!" For 10 points each: Name this opera that features a mezzo-soprano aria that was actually inspired by a Sebastian Iradier ("say-bahss-T'YAHN ee-rahd-YAIR") song, though its composer believed he was adapting an old folk tune.

In an aria often titled for one of these objects, a pattern of descending two-note phrases played by woodwinds followed by a chord from the harp musically depicts the act of creating one of them. For 10 points each: Yet another operatic letter scene can be found in the first act of this Georges Bizet opera, when Micaela ("mee-ka-EL-ah") delivers a letter to Don Jose ("zho-SAY") from his mother.

In this opera, two characters vow to remain true to each other in the aria "Au fond du temple saint" ("oh FON doo TOM-pluh SAN"). For 10 points each: This other opera by Bizet is set in Spain and includes numbers like the "Toreador Song" and a "Habanera."

This opera was the subject of Susan McClary's only monograph on a single work. The leads of a film based on this opera were criticized for their "taffy-colored" appearance and acting in an essay subtitled "The Dark is Light Enough." After attending its Vienna premiere, Johannes Brahms went to see this opera over twenty times. Harry Belafonte and Dorothy Dandridge starred in the all-black cast of a film version of this opera, which gives its title character the surname (*) "Jones." In Act II, this opera's title woman is shown a now-dry flower she once tossed to her lover. The first aria for this opera's title woman begins with a midtempo chromatic descent from D. This opera begins with a choir of urchins outside a cigar factory. This opera's aria "Votre toast, je peux vous le rendre" [voh-truh "TOAST," zhuh puh luh RON-druh] includes the refrain "Toreador, Toreador!" For 10 points, name this opera about a gypsy woman by Georges Bizet.

This aria was based on a Sebastian Iradier piece that the composer believed to be a folk song. For 10 points each: That "Habanera" is sung by the title woman of this opera, which also includes the "Toreador Song."

Answer some questions about some recently premiered operas, for 10 points each: Director Dmitri Tcherniakov rewrote some of the text of this Bizet opera to make it about psychotherapy for a 2017 production. This opera relates Don Jose's love for the title gypsy and contains a notable "Habanera."

This duet opens with lyrics about the "jasmine white" under a "leafy dome." For 10 points each: In the first act of this Georges Bizet opera, the title gypsy throws a flower to the Corporal of the Dragoons, Don Jose ("zho-SAY").

Two men in this opera try to recruit three women for a shady business job in the Act II quintet "Nous avons en tete une affaire." A famous aria from this opera has a downwards moving melody that begins with D and C-sharp pickup eighths followed by C triplets. Legend has it that the creator of this opera's main role, Celestine Galli-Marie, fainted during a performance of it on the night that its composer died. The protagonist of this opera sings of the inevitability of fate in "En vain pour eviter" after drawing a fortune-telling card that predicts her own death. That title character had earlier compared love to a rebellious bird in her "Habanera." For 10 points, Don Jose vies with the toreador Escamillo for the hand of the title gypsy, in what opera by Georges Bizet?

In the second act of this opera, a group of women sing the tavern song "Les tringles des sistres tintaient." For 10 points each: Name this opera in which the title character sings "En vain pour eviter," and a village girl expresses her determination to save the protagonist in "Je dis que rien ne m'epouvante."

Philip Hensher wrote the libretto to this opera, which ends with an electrician and a maid destroying its setting, a lavish hotel room. 10 points each: In 1947, this George Bizet opera became the first full opera to be performed at the Covent Garden Opera House. Its musical numbers include the "Toreador Song."

In this opera's third act, its main character sings "En vain pour eviter" while turning over an ace of spades representing death. In addition to singing the Card aria, this opera's main character sings "L'amour est un oiseau rebelle" in its first act before throwing flowers from her bodice at a soldier. In its second act, that soldier's eventual rival sings "Votre toast" and flirts with the title character, a worker at a (*) cigarette factory in Seville. Its final act is set at one of Escamillo's bullfights, where the title character is fatally stabbed by Don Jose. For 10 points, name this opera about a seductive gypsy composed by George Bizet.

tristan and isolde.text: Gustav Mahler and Arturo Toscanini feuded over the right to conduct this opera at the Met shortly before Mahler left for the New York Philharmonic. During the long love duet in Act 2 of this opera, the lovers ignore when an offstage mezzo-soprano warns them that night will end soon. A shepherd says "bleak and empty is the sea" in Act 3 of this opera, which begins with a long English horn solo. In this opera's last scene, which was originally titled "Transfiguration," the lead soprano gazes upon her lover's dead body while addressing her servant Brangane ("bron-GAY-nuh"). A half-diminished chord spelled as "F, B, D-sharp, G-sharp" finally resolves in this opera's concluding "Liebestod" ("LEE-bus-tote"). For 10 points, name this pioneering Wagner opera about the romance between a Cornish knight and an Irish princess.

The heroine of this opera instructs her servant to extinguish a torch as a signal that it is safe for another character to approach. The prelude to this opera begins with a pianissimo passage in the cellos that is often called the "grief", "longing", or "suffering" motif. Kurwenal and Melot are both killed in its third act, which takes place at Kareol in (*) Brittany. The prelude to its first act introduces a chord made of the notes F, B, G sharp, and D-sharp, which is often named after its male protagonist. It closes with the singing of the "Liebestod" aria by the heroine, an Irish maid who was kidnapped as a bride for King Marke. For 10 points, name this opera whose two title characters fall in love after drinking a magic potion, a work of Richard Wagner.

Emanuel Chabrier's Souvenirs is a quadrille on themes from this opera. Joseph Keilberth, Felix Mottl, and Ludwig Schorr von Carolsfeld all died while singing the lead tenor role in this opera. In one aria, this opera's female lead recalls saving someone who was mortally wounded in a barge. As it opens, a sailor sings of a "wild Irish maid," which the female lead takes as a personal insult. "Golliwog's Cakewalk" mockingly distorts a motive from this opera which features a half-diminished chord named for the first title character. The Prelude and Liebestod are often excerpted from, for 10 points, what opera about a pair of lovers from Irish mythology, composed by Richard Wagner?

poet.text: An imprisoned character with this profession calls on the "ultima dea" in an Act IV aria, after which an offstage baritone sings the Marseillaise ("mar-say-YEZ"). That tenor with this profession criticizes people who don't understand love or care for the poor in the aria "Un di all'azzurro spazio" ("oon DEE ahl-LAHT-tsoor-oh SPAHTS-yo"), or "L'improvviso." In an Act I aria, a man with this profession asks, "E come vivo? Vivo" ("EH KOH-meh vivo? vivo") and calls himself a "millionaire in spirit." A man with this profession is executed during the Reign of Terror in Umberto Giordano's opera Andrea Chenier ("shen-YAY"). At the start of a verismo opera, a tenor with this profession burns a script to keep warm. A man with this profession sings the aria "Che gelida manina" ("keh JEH-lee-dah mah-NEE-nah") to a woman who leaves him for a viscount ("VYE-count") before dying of consumption. For 10 points, name this profession of Mimi's lover Rodolfo in La Boheme.

doctor atomic.text: A character in this opera asks, "Am I in your light?" in one aria before her partner responds by quoting a poem by Baudelaire. The opening line of a refrain in this opera changes whether it refers to north, west, or east each time it appears. This opera's composer was inspired by Sibelius's Seventh Symphony to create a symphony based on it whose second movement is titled "Panic." A 2018 production of this opera with Ryan McKinny in the lead role featured indigenous artists performing a Corn Dance. In this opera, the maid Pasqualita describes the blossoming of a "cloud-flower" while singing a lullaby adapted from a traditional Tewa melody. Act 1 of this opera ends with an aria based on Donne's "Batter My Heart, Three-Person'd God." For 10 points, name this opera by John Adams about J. Robert Oppenheimer.

This opera's libretto includes passages from the Bhagavad Gita and Charles Baudelaire. For 10 points each: Name this opera in which Pasqualita sings a Tewa lullaby about a "cloud flower" that blossoms in the north. This opera's opening chorus proclaims that "energy may become matter."

Name some 20th century operas, for 10 points each: This opera's first act ends with the title character's aria Batter my heart, adapted from a John Donne poem. Another aria borrows from the Bhagavad Gita ["BAH-gah-vod GEE-tuh"], possibly inspired by a famous quotation from the opera's main character in real life.

The documentary Wonders Are Necessary is about the creation of this opera. A symphony based on this opera includes a section based on the "panic music" found in this opera's second act. The world premier of this opera featured Kristine Jepson instead of Lorraine Hunt Lieberson, who died shortly after the premier. This opera features the repeated incantation of 'cloud-flower blossoms' from the maid Pascualita. A chorus in this opera concerns a "Shape stupendous, full of mouths and eyes" when "all the (*) worlds are fear-struck, even just as I am." The final aria of Act 1 of this opera sees the title character sing Donne's Holy Sonnet XIV. The opening chorus of this opera sings "Matter can not be created nor destroyed, but only altered in form," a line that was taken from the Smyth report. For 10 points, identify this opera by John Adams about John Oppenheimer.

united states.text: The vocal part in a soprano aria set in this country ends by descending from E-flat to F over the chords F minor and B-flat minor on the word "aita." An aria sung by a character in this country ends by calling the singer's beloved "mia vita mio solo fior" or "only flower of my life." The aria "Sola, perduta, abbandonata" ("SOH-luh pair-DOO-tuh ah-bahn-doh-NAH-tuh") has been mocked for falsely portraying a real-life city in this country as being on the outskirts of a desert. In another opera, two characters from this country conclude the aria "Dovunque al mondo" ("doh-VOON-kway al MON-doh") by singing "[this country] for ever!" over an excerpt from this country's national anthem. A man in this country sings "Ch'ella mi creda libero" ("KELL-uh mee CRAY-duh LEE-bair-oh") as he is about to be hanged for being the bandit Ramerrez. For 10 points, a Puccini opera titled for a fanciulla ("fahn-CHOO-luh")is set in what country, where Pinkerton hails from in Madama Butterfly?

thais.text: Oscar Hammerstein hired Mary Garden to star in this opera's leading role in 1907, at the height of his competition with the Met Opera. At this opera's premiere, a strap scandalously fell off the gown of star soprano Sibyl Sanderson. While lamenting the coming of old age, a character in this opera sings "tell me I am beautiful" to her mirror. The composer made an Ave Maria arrangement of this opera's most excerpted piece, which is marked Andante religioso and opens with soft, ascending (*) harp arpeggios accompanying the soloist. This opera's title character, a former lover of the hard-partying nobleman Nicias, agrees to go to a convent in the desert after a pensive D major entr'acte featuring a violin solo. The cenobite monk Athanael loves the title courtesan in, for 10 points, what opera by Jules Massenet with a famous "Meditation"?
Answer the following about "meditations" in music, for 10 points each. The "Meditation" theme appears in Act III of this opera by Jules Massenet as the White Nuns attend to the dying title character.

The female lead of this opera examines herself in a mirror and considers whether she will become ugly while singing "dis-moi que je suis belle." One character in this opera is dressed in new clothes by the servants Crobyle and Myrtale, and angrily smashes a statue of Eros on the ground after learning of its origins. This opera was written for Sybil Sanderson, and begins with Palemon attempting to discourage its male lead from going to Alexandria, where he is introduced to the title character by his old friend Nicias. In its second act, the monk Athanael tries to convince the title character to give up her decadent ways, a decision the title character contemplates in an orchestral "meditation." For 10 points, name this opera based on an Anatole France novel about a courtesan, composed by Jules Massenet.

pablo de sarasate.text: After being called a "genius", this musician quipped: "for 37 years I've practiced 14 hours a day, and now they call me a genius!" This musician, who performed in 600 concerts with Berthe Marx, left London critics gasping "miles behind" according to George Bernard Shaw. This musician said that he refused to "stand on the rostrum...and listen to the oboe" while complaining about the second movement of a piece by Johannes Brahms. This musician was the dedicatee of (*) Bruch's Scottish Fantasy and Saint-Saens's Introduction and Rondo Capriccioso. One of this musician's showpieces consists of several dances mis-identified as being of Romani origin, while another adapts the "Habanera" by Georges Bizet. For 10 points, name this composer of Zigeunerweisen ("zee-GOY-ner-VYE-zen") and the "Carmen" fantasy, a Romantic-era violinist from Spain.

franz schubert.text: The Andante movement of a symphony by this composer abruptly modulates from E-flat to C-flat major, then back, as the woodwinds and violins trade the melody. Though in B-flat major, that Mozart-inspired symphony by this composer has a G minor minuet whose melody comes from the opera The Devil's Pleasure Palace. A trio of unison trombones repeatedly plays "quarter-dotted quarter-eighth-eighth" rhythms in the first movement of another symphony by this composer which opens with two unison (*) horns playing the notes [read slowly] "long C, D, E, down to A, B, C." The research of Arthur Sullivan and George Grove helped popularize this composer's symphonies, the last of which was praised for its "heavenly length." Another symphony by this composer re-uses his incidental music to Rosamunde ("ROH-zah-MUN-duh"). For 10 points, name this composer of the "Great" C Major and "Unfinished" symphonies.

john dowland.text: Anthony Rooley argues that this composer's motif of a descending fourth represents the fallen state of humanity. Edin Karamazov collaborated on an album primarily consisting of this composer's music, subtitled "From the Labyrinth." Several pieces from this composer's best-known collection begin with the motif [read slowly] "long A, short G, short F, long E." Percy Grainger arranged this composer's piece "Now, Oh Now." This composer, who puns on his own name with the Latin for (*) "sorrowful" in his most famous collection of pieces, also composed the "Frog Galliard" and "In Darkness Let Me Dwell." This composer's Second Book of Songs contains a version of the same piece he made seven pavanne variations of in his collection Lachrimae. For 10 points, name this lutenist and composer of "Flow My Tears" who hailed from Renaissance England.

beethoven piano trios.text: After practicing a piece in this genre, Louis Spohr wrote that he was "moved by the tragedy" of the composer carelessly playing notes out of tune in his house. In the second movement of a Beethoven piece in this genre, tremolos accompany a recurring theme of [read slowly] "A, descending triplets B-flat-A-G, F, up to D, down to A." At his last-ever public performance, Beethoven was joined by Josef Linke ("LINK-uh") in premiering a piece in this genre. Although technically the Dressler Variations are older, Beethoven's (*) Opus 1 consists of pieces in this genre for Prince Lichnowsky. These pieces include an 1811 one nicknamed for Rudolph of Austria and one which inspired Carl Czerny to remark that its Largo second movement evoked Hamlet's father. For 10 points, Beethoven composed pieces nicknamed "Archduke" and "Ghost" in what genre featuring three performers?

san francisco symphony orchestra.text: This orchestra's Program Notes podcast dissects writings by its longtime program writer Michael Steinberg. A major donor to this orchestra, Cynthia Hersey, wrote a 2024 editorial lambasting changes to its SoundBox program. In 1999, Michael Kamen conducted this orchestra on an album co-recorded with Metallica. Before his long tenure as musical director in Boston, Seiji Ozawa led this orchestra. In 2024, this orchestra's director cited the need to find a new ensemble to premiere his horn concerto at the Lucerne Music Festival, plus budget cuts, as reasons for (*) ending his contract. Davies Hall is the main venue for this orchestra, whose longtime director was diagnosed with brain cancer in 2021; a year earlier, the formerly Los Angeles-based Finnish conductor Esa-Pekka Salonen took his post. For 10 points, name this West Coast orchestra led by Michael Tilson Thomas.

romeo and juliet.text: James Horner adapted music from this ballet into the track that plays after the destruction of the Enterprise in Star Trek III. The composer's Opus 75 Ten Pieces for Piano includes arrangements of this ballet's piece "The Street Awakens." This ballet calls for two harps and two mandolins whose plucking is joined by a flute, then a brass fanfare, in its "Morning Serenade." A piece from this ballet features a tuba playing the notes [read slowly] (*) "E, G, E, G" as part of four loud E minor chords at its beginning, interspersed with notes from the strings. This ballet's "Departure of the Guests" scene includes a re-used gavotte from the composer's "Classical" Symphony. A saxophone solo represents the protagonists seeing each other for the first time at a ball in this ballet's "Dance of the Knights." For 10 points, what ballet by Sergei Prokofiev features the piece "The Death of Tybalt?"

In one opera, a character laments her withered flowers in the aria "Ah! non credea mirarti" while performing this action. For 10 points each: Bellini depicted characters from this play as members of the Guelphs and Ghibellines in his opera The Capulets and the Montagues.

This composer insisted to librettist Carlo Pepoli that "Opera must make people weep, feel horrified, die through singing" while working on an opera about the English Civil War. For 10 points each: Bellini's I Capuleti e i Montecchi is based on two families from this play, which is also the basis for a ballet by Sergei Prokofiev that contains the "Dance of the Knights."

antonio salieri.text: An opera by this composer ends with a chorus of demons singing while snakes devour the entrails of a group of chained women inside a burning palace. This was the [emphasize] elder of the two collaborators on a cantata celebrating the recovery of Nancy Storace ("stoh-RAH-chay"), titled "Per la ricuperata salute di Ofelia." In an opera by this composer, the Genius of Fire and Genius of Nature choose two shades to be incarnated as the despot Atar and the title Persian general. When a stroke afflicted this composer's mentor (*) Christoph Gluck, he was asked to complete the opera Les Danaides. This teacher of Franz Liszt composed the opera Tarare, which was reworked into Axur, re d'Ormus. He won a 1786 opera competition at the court of Joseph II, but supposedly tried to poison the competition's loser five years later. For 10 points, name this Italian known for his rivalry with Mozart.

astor piazzolla.text: This composer formed an octet to play music by the Decarean school, featuring violinist Hugo Baralis, after studying under Nadia Boulanger. The "prelude" and "finale" of an album by this composer are used in the film Happy Together, including in a scene that juxtaposes crying with shots of a waterfall. This composer's pieces "Fugata" and "Mumuki" feature on a 1997 album in which Nestor Marconi accompanies Yo-Yo Ma. This composer commemorated his father's death in a bicycle accident with a version of his piece (*) "Nonino." For Marco Bellocchio's film version of Enrico IV, he wrote a mournful milonga titled "Oblivion." A bandoneon ("bahn-doh-nay-OHN") ostinato underlies a piece by this composer which infuses jazz into a classic dance style and hence is partly titled for the word libertad. For 10 points, name this Argentinian developer of Nuevo Tango.

egypt.text: A composer from this country collaborated with Georges Delerue on the piece "Here History Began," which has played during a nightly "Sound and Light Show" since 1961. After a record-setting five-hour performance at Paris's L'Olympia theater in 1967, a singer from this country donated her earnings to the military. An ethnomusicologist from this country composed music for the ballet Clytemnestra and pioneered musique concrete by manipulating sounds from an exorcism into tape music. The song (*) "Enta Omri" was a hit for an immensely popular singer from this country who was called the "Star of the Orient." An opera set in this present-day country features the arias "La fatal pietra" and "Ritorna vinctor," the latter of which plays during its "triumphal march" scene. For 10 points, name this home country of Umm Kulthum and setting of Aida.

goldberg variations.text: Ferrucio Busoni's edition of this piece cuts out several movements and replaces them with a single massive coda. A performer described a giddy "bit of Neo-Scarlattism" in a toccata in this work which begins with a mordent on low G, followed by a trill on a G three octaves higher. A movement of this work in six-eight time is generally argued to be a French-style gigue ("zheeg") imitating a dance from the Canary Islands. This work repeatedly features the bass line [read slowly] "G, F-sharp, (*) E, D, B, C, D, low G." This work, which may quote from folk songs such as "Cabbage and turnips have driven me away," includes a movement described as the "black pearl" by Wanda Landowska ("lahn-DOV-skah"). Every third movement is a canon in this work, which includes an unexpected quodlibet. For 10 points, Glenn Gould twice recorded what keyboard piece by J. S. Bach?

vixen.text: An opera titled for this character opens with a tenuto motif of four A-flats, G, G-flat, E-flat followed by 32nd staccato flits representing a blue dragonfly. After inciting a feminist revolution and faking her death, this operatic character is shot at and escapes to find love. This character's adventures title a serialized novella with cartoons by Lolek and Tesnohlidek. The "Forester's Farewell," played by request at the composer's funeral, ends a folksy 1921 operatic parable on the cycle of life and symbiosis named for this character, which Max Brod translated into German. A poacher turns this former pet into fur for Terynka as the elderly forester is haunted by her cubs. For 10 points, name this female character who titles Janacek's lightest opera.

silent night.text: Aside from Anna Sorensen singing "Dona Nobis Pacem" in Latin surrounded only by men, this trilingual opera shifts among three national musical styles. Minnesota Opera premiered this opera with a screenplay-based libretto by Mark Campbell. This 2012 Pulitzer Prize winner is set in trenches at the end of 1914 when Scottish, French, and German soldiers played bagpipes and sang during an informal truce. For 10 points, what first opera by Kevin Puts is titled for a Christmas carol by Franz Xaver Gruber?

new zealand.text: A diva from this country won a breach-of-contract suit after refusing to perform with a pop singer whose female fans threw underwear onstage. Malvina Major names a foundation for emerging singers run by this country's only opera company, which formed in 2000, a third of whose board resigned over staging The Unruly Tourists. Sisters Margaret and Helen Medlyn, baritone Christian Thurston, and crossover singer Hayley Westenra are from this country. A soprano who recently moved back to this country was a 1970s sensation as last-minute Ave-Maria-singing Desdemona and Countess Almaviva. For 10 points, Hohepa is an opera by Jenny McLeod from what home country of Kiri Te Kanawa?

la serva padrona.text: A bachelor who hangrily demands chocolate at this opera's start is later silently threatened with a 4,000-crown dowry. This opera's F minor aria "Son imbrogliato io gia" abruptly slows to a whole-note walking bass on the repeated words "Uberto, pensa a te." After being coupled with Lully's Acis et Galatee, progressive fans of this "divine" opera like Rousseau formed the "Queen's corner" attacking the merits of Rameau's tragedies when impresario Eustachio Bambini's comic troupe brought it to Paris. Only three characters, one silent, appear in this short intermezzo intended for Il prigionier superbo by a composer who died at age 26 having just finished his Stabat Mater. For 10 points, the wily maid Serpina conspires to marry her master in what Pergolesi opera that ignited a two-year pamphlet War of the Buffoons?

Both this opera and Giovanni Paisiello's 1781 setting of the same libretto use a similar deceptive cadence with a rising semitone in the bass before one character calls another's name in a failed attempt to summon her. One character in this opera insists, "You keep saying 'no' when you mean to say 'yes'" during the duet "Lo conosco a quegli'occhietti." Another character in this opera silently demands a 4,000 crown dowry while dressed as a soldier named Tempesta. Jean-Jacques Rousseau boosted this opera's reputation in his Letter on French Music after its 1752 performance in Paris sparked the Querelle des Buffons. It was originally performed as an intermezzo to the composer's Il Prigioniero Superbo. For 10 points, name this opera buffa in which the silent Vespone helps the servant Serpina marry her master Uberto, a work by Giovanni Battista Pergolesi.

tosca.text: Angela Gheorghiu ("g'YORR-g'yoo") interrupted a tenor encore in a 2024 performance of this opera to say, "It's a performance. It's not a recital. Respect me." A spinto tenor remembers "sweet kisses, languorous caresses" in an aria from Act III of this opera that begins with a wistful clarinet solo. In Act I of this opera, that tenor describes the "contrasting beauties" of his lover and of a blonde woman he (*) painted as Mary Magdalene. This opera's title soprano gives a safe-conduct pass to the singer of "Recondita armonia" and "E lucevan le stelle" ("ay loo-CHAY-von lay STELL-ay"). In this opera, Baron Scarpia orders a firing squad to execute Mario Cavaradossi. For 10 points, name this Puccini opera that ends with the title diva jumping off the Castel Sant'Angelo.

This opera was controversially referred to as a "shabby little shocker" by musicologist Joseph Kerman in his 1956 book Opera as Drama. For 10 points each: Name this opera. After Kerman's scathing remarks, the conductor Thomas Beecham responded by saying that anything Kerman says about this opera's composer "can safely be ignored."

During a performance of this opera, Maria Callas's ("KAH-lahss's") hair caught on fire, leading Tito Gobbi to leap onto her to put it out. This opera opens with the recurring motif of three loud, spooky, brassy major chords on B-flat, A-flat, and E. A character in this opera trades his last possession of a ring to deliver a letter to the title character, leading him to sing about how "the Earth was scented" in the aria (*) "E lucevan le stelle" ("ay loo-CHAY-von lay stell-ay"). This opera's title character sings the aria "Vissi d'arte" ("vee-see DAR-tay") after Baron Scarpia attempts to blackmail her, and in its finale, the title character kills herself by jumping off a parapet after the execution of her lover Mario Cavaradossi. For 10 points, name this opera about the title singer by Giacomo Puccini.

One aria in this opera begins in E-flat minor, starting with a descending Eb-Db-Bb-Ab phrase, before shifting to E-flat major. The naturalist Gerald Durrell is one of a number of people who refer to a 'bouncing' version of this opera, though none have ever been corroborated. At the start of this opera, a character looks for a key hidden in the feet of a statue of the Virgin Mary. This opera's most celebrated aria is often performed lying down; that aria, 'Vissi d'arte', is sung by the title character after she has been offered her lover's freedom in exchange for sex with Baron Scarpia. For 10 points, name this melodramatic opera by Giacomo Puccini which ends with its title character jumping off the Castel Sant'Angelo.

This singer made her 1944 debut as Elena in Mefistofele, an opera she later recorded under the baton of Tullio Serafin in 1958, singing the role of Margherita. For 10 points each: Tebaldi and her rival Maria Callas both gave significant performances of this Puccini role, the lover of the painter Mario Cavaradossi and killer of Baron Scarpia.

In Act II of this opera, a gavotte is heard from outside the palace during a victory celebration in honor of General Melas. The male lead contrasts the blue eyes of an "unknown beauty" he has seen in church with the black eyes of his lover in "Recondita Armonia." The villain uses a fan to arouse the title character's jealousy and convince her to betray (*) Angelotti in exchange for her lover's life. That title character asks God why she has suffered such misfortune in "Vissi d'arte, vissi d'amore," and jumps to her death after realizing that Cavaradossi's "fake" execution was real after all. For 10 points, name this opera in which the title singer stabs the wicked police chief Scarpia, by Giacomo Puccini.

The singer of this aria recalls "sweet kisses and languorous caresses" before sobbing, "I die in desperation / And I never before 6 loved life so much." For 10 points each: Cavaradossi sings "E lucevan le stele" in the third act of this Puccini opera that concludes with the title singer jumping to her death of the roof of the Castel Sant'Angelo.

In the first act of this opera, choir boys who mistakenly believe Michael von Melas has won a battle sing a "Te Deum" chorus; in that same act, "Recondita armonia" is sung comparing a lover to a painting. A character reassures his lover of his fidelity with the aria "Qual'occhio al mondo." That lover later sings an aria in which "I donated jewels to the Madonna's mantle" and "I never harmed a living soul," (*) "Vissi d'Arte," after she feels abandoned. That character later stabs and kills Scarpia, reneging on an agreed plan; Mario Cavardossi is then killed via firing squad. For 10 points, name this opera which ends with the title singer jumping off Castel Sant-Angelo following the death of her lover, a work composed by Giacomo Puccini.

parties.text: At one of these events, two operatic characters speak in mock French as they pretend to be a Chevalier and a Marquis. At that one of these events, a Russian man states his motto is "chacun a son gout" ("sha-CUHN a sohn GOO"). An opera's Act II ends at one of these events, where the lead tenor gambles with Baron Douphol ("doo-FOLE") and throws his winnings at his ex-lover's feet. The maid Adele ("ah-DAY-luh") sings her "Laughing Song" at one of these events at Prince (*) Orlofsky's house. The courtesan Violetta sings "Sempre libera" after one of these events. La Traviata begins at one of these events where characters sing a brindisi, a type of drinking song. For 10 points, Johann Strauss' operetta Die Fledermaus ("dee FLAY-der-mouse") is set at what sort of event held on New Year's Eve?

arnold schoenberg.text: Duke Bluebeard's Castle is sometimes performed on a double bill with a short opera by this composer about a woman finding her dead lover in the forest. This composer misspelled an opera's title for fear of it being thirteen letters long. The premiere of this composer's "monodrama" Erwartung was conducted by his teacher Alexander von Zemlinsky. This composer's opera Moses und Aron ("AH-ron") used a compositional technique he developed to build its source (*) row. This composer developed a half-singing, half-speaking technique called sprechstimme that his student employed in the operas Lulu and Wozzeck. For 10 points, name this teacher of Alban Berg, founder of the Second Viennese School, and developer of the twelve-tone technique.

A character with this name abandons her fiancee Stolzius for the dissolute nobleman Desportes during the first ricercari in Bernd Alois Zimmermann's opera Die Soldaten (dee zoll-DAH-tin), which is based on a play by Jakob Lenz (YAH-kop LENTS). For 10 points each: As a member of the Second Viennese School, Berg was a student of this composer, who applied his twelve-tone system to create operas like Moses und Aron.

sailor.text: Jean Cocteau wrote the libretto to a Darius Milhaud opera about a "Poor" person in this profession, which only has four unnamed characters. Despite having a stammer, a character with this profession is labeled "a find in a thousand." A man with this profession who is admired by Josephine is switched at birth by Mrs. Cripps, otherwise known as Little Buttercup, in an opera whose characters include Dick Deadeye. "The smartest lad" Ralph Rackstraw has this profession in a Gilbert and Sullivan opera. A character with this profession sings "Starry Vere, God Bless you!" before his execution in a Benjamin Britten opera that includes John Claggart. For 10 points, name this profession of Billy Budd.

rigoletto.text: In 1950, RCA Victor made a recording of this opera featuring Leonard Warren in the lead role alongside soprano Erna Berger. Tenor Raffaele Mirate was reprimanded for whistling the tune of a B major aria from this opera that opens with three accented notes on D-sharp. A coloratura soprano sings the line "Sweet name that made my heart" in this opera's aria "Caro nome che il mio cor," who believes a character is named Gualtier Malde. This opera was originally titled La maledizione ("MAL-uh-dits-YOH-neh") or "The Curse," in reference to an exclamation after Gilda is murdered by Sparafucile. The aria "La donna e mobile" is from, for 10 points, what opera about the Duke of Mantua and the title jester, composed by Giuseppe Verdi?

Michael Mayer's 2013 Met production of this opera is set at a casino in 1960s Las Vegas. A character in this opera sings "We are alike!," comparing his "tongue of malice" to another character's dagger in the aria "Pari siamo!" In the aria "Caro Nome," a character in this opera sings about the sweetness of her lover's fake name "Gualtier Malde" ("go-tee-AY mald"). Gilda is (*) slain by the assassin Sparafucile ("SPAR-uh-FOO-sil-AY") in this opera, in which the Duke of Mantua sings about the "flightiness" of women in the aria "La Donna e Mobile" For 10 points, a hunchbacked jester titles what Giuseppe Verdi opera?

A "scene and storm trio" from this opera, which Julian Budden described as being "without antecedent," includes an offstage male chorus that wordlessly hums to imitate the wind. A character introduces himself as Gualtier Malde to another character in this opera, prompting her to sing about his "dearest name" in the aria "Caro Nome." The elderly Count Monterone's warning at the start of this opera prompts the title character to cry "La maledizione!" as his last words. In this opera, Gilda sacrifices herself to save a character from the assassin Sparafucile, which the title character discovers upon hearing a distant reprise of the aria "La donna e mobile." For 10 points, name this Giuseppe Verdi opera titled for a vengeful court jester who works for the Duke of Mantua.

This opera ends with a choral setting of parts of Brecht's poem "To Posterity," and its 1961 premiere caused a neo-Fascist riot where demonstrators yelled "Viva la polizia!". For 10 points each: This other Italian opera premiered at La Fenice in 1851. In it, the title jester's daughter Gilda overhears the Duke of Mantua singing the aria "La donna e mobile."

This opera employs an offstage chorus humming in chromatic parallel thirds to simulate wind in the thunderstorm in Act Four, which precedes the stabbing of a character disguised as a traveller. One character in this opera notes the similarity between how he uses words and another character uses a dagger, in the aria "Pari siamo!" Another character in this opera listens to her beloved re-use the same words he used to seduce her, as he woos Maddalena, in the quartet "Bella figlia d'amore". The title character recalls Count Monterone's curse when he realizes that the body in the sack he's holding is his daughter Gilda's, and not that of his employer, who is singing "La donna e mobile" off in the distance. For 10 points, name this opera about a vengeful jester to the Duke of Mantua, by Giuseppe Verdi.

One character in this opera rejoices in the name of her beloved in "Caro Nome". For 10 points each: Name this opera, whose title character, a hunchbacked jester, fears that his daughter, Gilda, will become the conquest of the Duke of Mantua, who sings "La donna e mobile".

This opera's female lead declares her love for a man disguised as the student Gualtier Malde in the aria "Caro Nome." In this opera's third act, its antagonist attempts to seduce Maddalena at an inn while its main character vows revenge in the quartet "Bella figlia dell'amore." Based on Victor Hugo's play Le Roi s'amuse, this opera ends with its main character opening a sack next to a river, only to discover his dying daughter (*) Gilda, who sacrifices herself to the assassin Sparafucile. Its antagonist compares a woman to a feather in the wind in its aria "La donna e mobile." Focusing on the title character's failed attempt to kill the Duke of Mantua, for 10 points, name this opera about a cursed court jester, composed by Giuseppe Verdi.

In one aria, this opera's title character angrily rants that a group of courtiers are a vile and damned race. Another character in this opera claims to be a poor student named Gualtier Malde after giving Giovanna a hefty bribe. Marullo tells everyone about the title character's mistress, and later blindfolds him while forcing him to hold a ladder. The lead soprano announces her love in "Caro Nome," not knowing her would-be lover has been cursed by Count Monterone. The title character hires Maddalena and Sparafucile to kill the Duke of Mantua, who sings "La Donna e Mobile" and thereby reveals that Gilda has died instead. For 10 points, name this Verdi opera about a jester.

One character in this opera disguises himself as the student Gualtier Malde. That character in this opera sings of his beloved in "Parmi veder le lagrime" and of a life of pleasure in "Questa o quella". That character in this opera inspires another to contemplate his name in the aria "Caro nome" before she is kidnapped by a chorus of courtiers and the blindfolded protagonist. The title character of this opera fears the curse put upon him by Count Monterone, and hires the assassin Sparafucile to help him murder the rakish nobleman that seduced his daughter Gilda. For 10 points, the Duke of Mantua sings "La donne e mobile" in what Giuseppe Verdi opera about a title hunchbacked jester?

death.text: This word's adjectival form is the first in the title of an opera in which Joseph De Rocher is spiritually advised by Sister Helen Prejean in Louisiana. This is the first title word in the first opera by Jake Heggie. This word is the first in the title of an opera in which the line "There is so much to get through" is sung in the Chorus of Exiled Jews. Molqi shoots a man offstage in an opera titled for this noun, and his corpse sings the "Aria of the Falling Body." A Jewish passenger is murdered aboard the Achille Lauro in a John Adams opera whose title opens with this word. Klinghoffer experiences, for 10 points, what fate, which befalls Don Giovanni when he is dragged to hell?

butterfly.text: In a play titled for this animal, a man notes that he was once voted "least likely to be invited to a party," which is ironic because partygoers all across Europe at that moment discuss his stupidity. A man compares having power over women to a needle and this animal in a metaphor that leads to the man lying to his wife, Helga, and being promoted to Vice-Consul. This animal titles a play in which a French diplomat falls in love with a Chinese singer, unaware that the singer is actually a man. Rene Gallimard has an affair with Song Liling in a David Henry Hwang play partly titled for this animal. For 10 points, give this animal that symbolically names Song's role in a Giacomo Puccini opera.

A soprano traveling on the steamboat El Dorado transforms into one of these animals at the end of a 1996 opera in which the journalist Rosalba repeatedly loses her notebook. For 10 points each: An operatic character named after what sort of animal stabs herself after singing the aria "Con onor muore"? The soprano aria "Un bel di, vedremo" appears in that Puccini opera, whose title character is named for this animal.

Bao Tianxiao and Zhou Shoujuan were this kind of writer that discussed modern Shanghai. Stein went on expeditions to catch these animals in Lord Jim, while Van Veen wanted to study them in Nabokov's Ada. The Mirabal sisters confront the reality of life under the Trujillo regime in a work by Julia Alvarez set In the Time of these animals. This word also appears in the title of a David Henry Hwang play and a John Luther Long story about Pinkerton and Cio-Cio San. For 10 points-name this insect from a Puccini opera about a Madama one.

henry purcell.text: In 2013, Peter Sellars produced an unfinished semi-opera by this composer for which the artist Gronk creates Mayan-inspired art to blend with the Mesoamerican characters Montezuma and King Inca. This composer may have made fun of Elkanah Settle in an opera featuring a stuttering poet in a scene in which Titania argues with Oberon. This composer of The Indian Queen wrote a G minor aria that falls chromatically from G to D. The aria "What Power art thou" was composed by this man for the "Frost Scene" from his opera King Arthur. The line "Remember me, but ah! Forget my fate" concludes this composer's lamentful aria "When I am laid in earth." For 10 points, name this English composer of Dido and Aeneas.

In an opera by this composer, Osmond tries to woo Emmeline by putting on a show in which Cupid wakes up the Cold Genius. This composer wrote a G minor aria in chaconne form that uses a passus duriusculus, or a descending chromatic fourth bass line, in 3/2 time. An opera by this composer closes with the chorus "Ye drooping wings," which is modeled on a chorus by his mentor John (*) Blow. This composer included a celebrated "Frost Scene" in his opera King Arthur. The aria "Thy hand, Belinda" precedes an aria with ground bass accompaniment in an opera this composer wrote for Josias Priest's girls' school. For 10 points, name this English composer who included the lament "When I am laid in earth" in his opera Dido and Aeneas.

The recitativo secco that precedes this aria opens with the lines "Thy hand, Belinda, darkness shades me / On thy bosom let me rest." For 10 points each: "Dido's Lament" is from Dido and Aeneas, an opera by this English Baroque composer. He adapted William Shakespeare's play A Midsummer Night's Dream into his opera The Fairy-Queen.

vincenzo bellini.text: In an opera by this composer, Imogene falls in love with a Count of Montalto named Gualtiero, who survives a shipwreck. This composer reworked the Romeo and Juliet story into the opera I Capuleti e I Montecchi, one of his collaborations with Felice Romani. In an opera by this composer, the aria "Ah! non credea mirarti" ("ah nawn kray-DAY-uh mee-RAHR-tee") is sung by Amina after she fails to woo Elvino. This composer of Il pirata wrote an opera whose title character sings a cavatina in which she approaches an altar and sings about a "Chaste goddess," or "Casta diva." Polliane, a proconsul of Gaul, dies with the title Druid priestess in an opera by this bel canto composer. For 10 points, name this composer of La Sonnambula and Norma.

In one opera, a character laments her withered flowers in the aria "Ah! non credea mirarti" while performing this action. For 10 points each: Amina is the title "sleepwalker" of an opera by this composer. A druid priestess sings the aria "Casta diva" in this composer's opera Norma.

A 1972 Met performance of this opera garnered Luciano Pavarotti seventeen curtain calls. For 10 points each: Dessay played Amina in a 2009 Met production of this composer's La sonnambula that was controversially set in a modern rehearsal hall. This rival of Donizetti wrote the aria "Casta Diva" for his opera Norma.

One opera by this composer has the Romanza "Dopo l'oscura nembo," which is sung by Nelly as she finds out Lord Adelson has been forced to break his engagement with her. That aria is used in a later opera, in which Giulietta sings "O quante volte." Besides the operas "Adelson e Salvini" and "I Capuleti e i Montecchi," this composer also wrote an opera in which the phrase "Ah! Non credea mirarti" is sung by the titular character, Amina, in the aria "Ah! Non giunge uman pensiero" after she awakens from (*) sleepwalking atop a mill bridge. This composer's most famous opera contains the aria "Ah! Bello a me ritorna" as the titular character wishes for peace with Rome. Earlier in that scene, the same druid sings "Casta diva" after raising a mistletoe to the moon to protect Pollione. For 10 points, name this Italian composer of bel canto operas such as La Sonnambula and Norma.

The soprano aria 'Ah! non credea mirarti' is sung by Amina in the last act of this opera as she crosses an unsafe bridge holding some withered flowers. For 10 points each: The first lines of that aria from La sonnambula, which lament the early blooming of a flower, are written on the tomb of this bel canto composer of I puritani and Norma.

Answer the following about extreme notes in the operatic repertoire, for 10 points each. In this bel canto opera composer's I puritani, the tenor role Arturo sings up to an F5 during the aria "Credeasi, misera." This composer also wrote La sonnambula.

This composer insisted to librettist Carlo Pepoli that "Opera must make people weep, feel horrified, die through singing" while working on an opera about the English Civil War. For 10 points each: Name this bel canto composer, one of whose operas features the arias "In mia man" and "Casta diva."

A shipwrecked man named Gualtiero kills Ernesto in a duel, out of love for Imogene, in this composer's opera Il pirata. For 10 points each: Name this Italian bel canto composer who wrote the aria "Casta Diva" for the title druid priestess in his opera Norma.

sleepwalking.text: A 1911 recording of an aria depicting this action includes cello obbligato under Luisa Tetrazzini's cadenza. A mezzo-soprano aria depicting this action includes a high D-flat in its last phrase on the word "andiam" ("ahn-dee-YAHM"). This action is performed during the aria "Una macchia e qui tuttora" ("OO-nah MAHK-kyah eh kwee toot-TOH-rah") the final aria for female voice in an opera that has 1847 and 1865 versions. In a bel canto opera, this action is performed by a soprano singing to a bouquet of withered flowers on a rickety mill bridge. This action causes (*) Elvino to end his engagement to a woman who performs this action in the aria "Ah! non credea mirarti" ("Ah non kray-DAY-ah mee-RAHR-tee"). Amina is the title performer of this action in an opera by Vincenzo Bellini. For 10 points, in a Verdi opera, what action does Lady Macbeth perform while cleaning imaginary bloodstains?

In one opera, a character laments her withered flowers in the aria "Ah! non credea mirarti" while performing this action. For 10 points each: Name this action that causes Elvino to mistakenly believe that Amina is having an affair with Rodolfo. In a Verdi opera, a queen laments not being able to cleanse her hands of blood during a scene nicknamed for this action.

In one opera, the oboe occasionally interjects as a character performing this action laments her withered flowers and sings the aria "Ah! non credea mirarti / si presto estinto, o fiore." For 10 points each: Name this title action of an 1831 opera. Elvino calls off a wedding after mistakenly believing that a woman performing this action is having an affair with Rodolfo when she ends up in his room.

i pagliacci.text: The words "e van!" are repeated by a character in this opera to end an F-sharp major "Ballatella." A character in this opera exclaims, "Name of God! Those same words!" after hearing another character declare "I will always be yours!" In this opera's prologue, the audience is instructed to "mark well...our souls" by a character who asks "Si puo?" After stabbing (*) Nedda and Silvio, a character in this opera declares "La commedia e finita!" Canio sings "Vesti la giubba" in, for 10 points, what Ruggero Leoncavallo opera about a troupe of clowns?

In a scene from this opera, a choir sings the words "don, din, don" to mimic the sound of vesper bells. A character in this opera furiously repeats "il nome! il nome!" after discovering two lovers planning to run away at midnight. The audience is asked to "mark well our souls...for we are men...like you" by a single character on stage in a "prologue" to this opera. That character from this opera overhears a woman imitating birdsong as she sings the aria "Stridono lassu." Tonio exposes an affair in this opera to a character who shouts "La commedia e finita!" after stabbing his wife Nedda and her lover Silvio. Canio sings the aria "Vesti la giubba" in, for 10 points, what verismo opera by Ruggero Leoncavallo about a troupe of clowns?

At the beginning of an Act II aria from this opera, a nearly offstage singer tells a woman to "open your window to me [who is] close at hand, calling and sighing for you." Villagers in this opera sing an Act I "bell chorus" that precedes a "ballatella" inspired by the sight of screeching birds, "Stridono lassu." A man dressed as the servant Taddeo asks the question "Si puo?" at the beginning of this opera, in which (*) Arlecchino sings "O Colombina." In this opera, Nedda is stabbed by Canio, who sings the aria "Vesti la giubba" while putting on his costume. For 10 points, name this Ruggiero Leoncavallo opera typically paired with Cavalleria Rusticana and titled for a group of clowns.

Enrico Caruso's 1902 recording of this aria was the first record to sell over 1 million copies. For 10 points each: Canio sings "Vesti la giubba" in this Ruggero Leoncavallo-composed verismo opera about a commedia dell'arte troupe.

This suite was later adapted into an opera about the lovers Rosario and Fernando. For 10 points each: Granados's operatic version of Goyescas premiered on a double bill with this opera, which ends with Canio singing "Vesti la giubba" after stabbing his wife and her lover.

This aria is sung after one character discovers that his love agrees to run away with Silvio. For 10 points each: "Vesti la giubba" appears in this opera about a bunch of costumed performers by Ruggero Leoncavallo.

Stephen Barlow changed the setting of this opera to the 1970s in his current production of it. Foreboding ensues after one character offers to stay behind to groom a donkey. Another character in this opera, which takes place on the Feast of the Assumption, picks up a bullwhip to ward off the advances of a lovestruck man and joins in with a singing bird in the aria "Stridono lassu." That character is stabbed to death in this opera's second act, after which her killer declares that "the comedy is over." Canio laments having to act jovial despite being tormented by Nedda's infidelity in this opera's aria "Vesti la Giubba." For 10 points, name this opera that dramatizes the love affairs within a group of circus performers, by Ruggero Leoncavallo.

The title character of this opera dies after sniffing poisoned flowers sent by her rival, who also loves Maurizio. For 10 points each: A more famous example of verismo is this Ruggero Leoncavallo opera, in which Canio sings "Vesti la giubba" after discovering that his wife Nedda has been unfaithful.

A recording by Enrico Caruso of one aria from this opera was the first record in history to sell a million copies. The Intermezzo of this opera echoes the baritone solo that opened it. The lead soprano in this opera sings an aria in admiration of birds in the sky, "Stridono lassu". One character is angered by another saying "I am yours always" as Colombina to a character playing Arlecchino in a play within this opera. At the beginning of this opera, the protagonist warns Tonio not to pursue his wife Nedda. This opera's protagonist, Canio, sings the aria "Vesti la Giubba" as he puts on his costume at the end of Act I. For 10 points, name this Leoncavallo opera typically paired with Cavalleria Rusticana that is about a clown.

czechia.text: In an opera from this country, one of the title characters dances with a shepherd to sneak past the gatekeeper to hell. An opera from this country details the title character's two "excursions" arising from his drinking, both of which end with him awakening in a barrel. During a scene by a lake, a prince in an opera from this country kisses a will-o'-the-wisp and dies, causing another character to remark that "all sacrifices are futile". Earlier in that opera from this country, the title (*) water nymph sings the "Song to the Moon" and becomes human at the cost of her voice. In an opera from this country, the title character's stepmother drowns a baby in the icy river. This country's folklore inspired such operas as The Cunning Little Vixen and Rusalka. For 10 points, name this home country of Leos Janacek ("LAY-osh JAN-ah-check") and Antonin Dvorak ("d'VOR-zhack").

The 2023 La Scala production of an opera from this country set its first and third acts in a submerged gothic cathedral and introduces its title character in a wheelchair wearing a pink tentacled dress. A composer from this country used his technique of "speech-melodies" to evoke natural vocal patterns in a character's plea for forgiveness after drowning her stepdaughter's baby in an icy stream. In one opera from this country, ascending harp chords in 3/8 open the aria "Song to the Moon." In that opera from this country, a prince dies after kissing the title water nymph. Folk dances like the furiant and polka feature in an opera from this country, whose other operas include Rusalka and Jenufa ("yeh-NOO-fah"). For 10 points, name this country home to operas by composers Leos Janacek ("YA-na-check") and Antonin Dvorak ("DVOR-zhak").

tsars.text: A character who opposes two of these people quarrels with his son over the girl Emma in an opera that was left with unfinished orchestration. In an opera, one of these people chooses Marfa as his bride, but she goes insane after being poisoned at their engagement party. The orchestra imitates the sound of bells at the start of a scene in which a crowd chants "Glory!" in an opera about one of these people who hallucinates a murdered child. An 1836 opera titled for one of these people features a scene in which the Polish army is led into a blizzard and was followed by its composer's opera Ruslan and Lyudmila. That opera by Mikhail Glinka is titled A Life for [one of these people]. For 10 points, what kind of person is the main character of Modest Mussorgsky's opera Boris Godunov?

A man laments that he has no joy despite holding this title for six years due to his guilt over a child's death in a bass aria usually called that man's "monologue." In the epilogue of another opera titled for this position, people walk across a plaza singing a patriotic hymn beginning "Glory, glory!" The "holy fool's aria" and a drinking song by the vagabond Varlaam feature in an opera which ends with the title holder of this position (*) abdicating in favor of his son Theodore. A number of Poles are led into a blizzard by the hero Susanin, who thus gives his "life for" a man with this title, in the best-known opera of Mikhail Glinka ("MEE-hyle GLEEN-kah"). For 10 points, give this title held by the main character of Modest Mussorgsky's Boris Godunov ("bah-REES gah-doo-NOFF").

turandot.text: In 2024, the Washington National Opera commissioned Christopher Tin to write a new ending for this opera. For 10 points each: Name this opera that was originally completed by Franco Alfano. In this Puccini opera, Calaf answers three riddles to win the hand of the title Chinese princess.

The first notes in this opera consist of four octave doubles of [read slowly] A, E-sharp, low B, C-sharp. An aria in this opera begins by alternating between a G major and a complex D9 over E-flat chord accompanying four gentle repeated notes with dotted rhythm sung on D4 which is repeated an octave lower. A slave girl describes the title character of this opera as being encircled in ice in the aria "Tu che di gel sei cinta." The crowd of this opera sings "there on the Eastern mountains a stork sang," with a melody taken from the folksong "Jasmine Flower." Franco Alfano composed the most commonly performed ending of this opera, whose male lead solves three riddles and sings "Nessun Dorma." For 10 points, name this unfinished opera about a Chinese princess by Giacomo Puccini.

This question can be read to any team. A chapter on this opera titled for its composer's "Solution: Integrating the Conventional and Unconventional" appears in Andrew C. Davis's 2010 book on its composer's "Late Style," in which this is the latest opera discussed. A soprano aria in this opera's second act has an ostinato alternating between F-sharp sus2 sus4 and F-sharp minor add9 chords. That aria from this opera repeats the exclamation "No one will ever possess me!" and describes the fate of the protagonist's ancestor. During this opera's premiere, the words "at this point the maestro died" were reportedly spoken by Arturo Toscanini. The aria "In questa reggia" ("een KWEH-stah RED-jah") appears in this opera, whose most famous tenor aria concludes with three cries of the word "vincero!" ("veen-cheh-ROH"). For 10 points, the aria "Nessun Dorma" ("NESS-oon DOHR-mah") appears in what unfinished opera by Puccini, titled for a Chinese princess?

A gentle breeze lent an ethereal atmosphere to Montserrat Caballe's ("kah-bah-YAY's") 1974 outdoor performance of this aria at the Roman Theatre of Orange ("or-AHNJ"). For 10 points each: In separate recordings of this Puccini opera, Caballe sang the role of Liu and the title Chinese princess. Calaf sings "Nessun dorma" in this opera.

This opera was completed by Franco Alfano two years after the death of its composer; at its premiere, the conductor Toscanini ended the performance where that composer had left off, with a kiss between its two main characters. This opera features three (*) riddles that a suitor must answer for the titular character's hand in marriage, as well as a Rumpelstiltskin-like challenge posed by a successful suitor. A famous tenor aria from this opera has been performed at several World Cup finals; that aria, titled "Nessun dorma", ends with the singer repeating the word "vincero ("veen-CHAIR-oh")," or "I will win," three times. For ten points, name this Puccini opera set in China about Prince Calaf and his efforts to marry the titular princess.

Luciano Berio ("loo-CHAH-no BARY-oh") composed a new ending for this opera in 2002, attempting to correct the dramaturgical flaws of the standard ending written by Franco Alfano. For 10 points each: Name this opera in which Calaf successfully wins the hand of the title Chinese princess, the final opera of Giacomo Puccini.

Luciano Berio ("loo-CHAH-no BARY-oh") composed a new ending for this opera in 2002, attempting to correct the dramaturgical flaws of the standard ending written by Franco Alfano. For 10 points each: This is the

After the "chap" buys this character a "cacocalo," she tells him about testifying at the trial of Jeanne Lalochere, her mother, who had split open her alcoholic father's head to stop him from abusing this character. For 10 points each: Zazie in the Metro includes a character with this name who owns a parrot that repeats, "Talk, talk, that's all you can do." A Carlo Gozzi work about a Chinese princess with this name was made into an opera by Puccini.

In many performances of one aria in this opera, the penultimate note, a high B, is held for much longer than the semiquaver indicated on the score. The composer rather than the librettist wrote the words for one aria in this opera, "Tu che di gel sei cinta". The aria "In Questa Reggia" is sung by the title character of this opera and explains how Lo-U-Ling was raped and murdered. In this opera, a gong is struck three times, and the servants Ping, Pang and Pong torture the slave girl Liu to try to find out a man's name. "Vincero! Vincero!" are the final words of an aria sung by Calaf in this opera, "Nessun Dorma". For 10 points, name this opera about a Chinese princess, the final work of Giacomo Puccini.

A tenor aria from this opera begins with the note D sung in a double-dotted "LONG, short, LONG, LONG" rhythm, after which the same melody is repeated an octave below. A soprano aria from this opera describes a king that "unfurled" seven banners, conquered a rival kingdom, and "stifled" the voice of its ruler. A slave girl in this opera stabs herself to death after proclaiming the title character to be "encircled in ice" in the aria "Tu che di gel sei cinta." Its title character sings "In questa reggia" as she poses three riddles to the tenor lead, who in turn challenges her to discover his name by the next morning, but predicts her failure in the aria "Nessun dorma." For 10 points, name this Giacomo Puccini opera about a Chinese princess.

Shortly before stabbing herself, one character in this opera tells another that the ice she wraps herself in will be defeated by the fire of love. That character in this opera had previously pleaded with the hero in "Signore, ascoti!". At its premiere, Toscanini stopped the performance at the point in Act Three where he claimed the composer had (*) died writing it. It features "Tu che di gel sei cinta" and an aria that ends with the tenor declaring "Vincero!", because the title character doesn't know his name. A challenge in this opera is met by striking a gong three times, and invites the derision of Ping, Pang, and Pong. Liu aids Prince Calaf in, for 10 points, what opera about the title Chinese princess, the final work of Giacomo Puccini?

richard strauss.text: The opening of an opera by this composer uses wide horn arpeggios and sighing string figures to represent lovemaking between two characters, one of whom stops clocks in the night because of her fear of aging. An unseen character is represented by a broken D minor chord in an opera by this composer that uses a motif built from a polytonal E major and C-sharp major chord. Two lovers sing the duet "Mit ihren augen voll Tranen" ("mit EER-in OW-gun voll TRAIN-in") in act two of an opera by this composer before being interrupted by the lecherous Baron Ochs ("ox"). The title character of an opera by this composer performs the "Dance of the Seven Veils" before kissing the head of John the Baptist. For 10 points, name this German composer of Der Rosenkavalier and Salome.

In the prologue to this opera, Major-Domo declares that an opera seria and opera buffa will be performed simultaneously, causing a conflict between the Composer and the Music-Master. For 10 points each: This German composer of Ariadne auf Naxos described the brutish Baron Ochs's failure to marry Sophie after she falls in love with Count Octavian in his comic opera Der Rosenkavalier.

In the first scene of this work, the fifth maid is taken to be flogged after she shows sympathy for the wild-looking title character. For 10 points each: This composer collaborated with Hugo von Hoffmansthal on Elektra and Der Rosenkavalier, but wrote his own libretto after an Oscar Wilde play for his opera Salome.

An opera by this composer begins with a rehearsal of a string sextet by Flamand, who argues against Olivier that music is more powerful than words. This composer wrote a rondo beginning with the words "Als ein Gott" to conclude a 12-minute-long coloratura aria in which Zerbinetta advises another woman about getting over heartbreak; that aria is "Grossmachtige Prinzessin" (GROSS-maysh-tig-uh print-SESS-in). In another opera by him, Octavian and Sophie sing the love duet "Ist ein traum" after the Marschallin bids Octavian farewell. An opera seria and a commedia dell'arte play must be performed simultaneously in the frame story to his opera Ariadne auf Naxos. Hugo von Hofmannsthal often collaborated with, for 10 points, what composer of Der Rosenkavalier?

In the prologue to this opera, two pieces of entertainment are ordered to be performed simultaneously to leave time for fireworks. For 10 points each: Ariadne auf Naxos was created by this German composer, who also wrote Salome. He returned to the opera-within-an-opera format in Capriccio.

In an opera by this composer, a single high note on the double bass accompanies a scene where a woman awaits the executioner's sword before she is crushed by shields. A love affair first introduced in Act II with an oboe and horn duet in one of this composer's operas is later celebrated by a trio of female voices that sing "Ha mirs gelob't." This composer shocked audiences by having a woman demand that an executioner use a silver platter to bring her a dead man's (*) head before she performs the erotic "Dance of the Seven Veils"; that woman falls in love with John the Baptist against her father Herod's wishes. The title breeches-role character of another opera by this composer is allowed by the Marschallin to pursue the hand of Sophie. For 10 points, name this composer of Salome and Der Rosenkavalier.

In one work by this man, the title character's sister is seen giving a man the key to the room of an engaged woman by her fiance, leading to false accusations of infidelity. In another work, Zerbinetta asks the title character, "Are we not both women?" before proceeding to outshine her with a coloratura aria. This composer of Arabella also penned an opera in which the title character performs a striptease to her father-in-law, the Dance of the Seven Veils, and in return receives the head of John the Baptist on a silver platter. Ariadne auf Naxos and Salome are, for 10 points, operas by this German composer of Der Rosenkavalier and Elektra.

giuseppe verdi.text: An opera by this composer ends with a declaration that "he laughs well who laughs the final laugh," which is then repeated in a fugue. Leonie Rysanek substituted for Maria Callas in the 1959 Met premiere of an opera by this composer, performing as a character who was also one of Shirley Verrett's first soprano roles. The title character of an opera by this composer sings about the meaning of "honor" while drinking at a Windsor inn. In an opera by this composer, a character sings the "Ave Maria" before being strangled to death. Until 2015, performers at the Met wore blackface to play a character in an opera by this composer who stabs himself next to his wife Desdemona ("dez-DEM-oh-na"). For 10 points, name this Italian composer whose Shakespearan operas include Macbeth and Otello.

This character was played by Jerome Hines in a 1980 Metropolitan Opera production, where he sang opposite Paul Plishka. For 10 points each: The Grand Inquisitor is the chief villain in Don Carlo, a grand opera by this composer of Il trovatore ("eel tro-vah-TOH-reh").

In the overture to an opera by this composer, chromatically ascending strings follow a trombone chorale that is briefly interrupted by a loud tutti section.
