Name these 19th century opera heroines who commit suicide, for 10 points each: Having sung the Flowers Duet in the first act, this heroine of a Delibes [duh-LEEB] work eats a poisonous flower in the third act when she realises that the British officer Gerald has a military duty to abandon her.

alex ross.text: When a 1993 paper by Rascher et al found that listening to this composer's music temporarily improved spatial reasoning, the media exaggerated the result as a magical IQ booster. For 10 points each: In a New York Times column on the original study, this critic remarked "listening to Mozart actually makes you smarter." This music critic for the New Yorker wrote the book The Rest is Noise.

mozart s sonata for two pianos in d majo.text: When a 1993 paper by Rascher et al found that listening to this composer's music temporarily improved spatial reasoning, the media exaggerated the result as a magical IQ booster. For 10 points each: The piece used in Rascher et al's original "Mozart effect" study was this one, which has also featured in several studies claiming that listening to it helps epileptics. This D major piece was written for a collaborative performance with Josepha Auernhammer ("yoh-SEFF-ah AO-ern-HAH-mur").

pathetique symphony.text: The clarinets softly introduce the secondary theme of this symphony's third movement, which is an upbeat march in E major. For 10 points each: Name this symphony. Hans Richter began the tradition of having the second clarinet player complete the first clarinet's solo, which comes before an explosive Allegro vivo section in the middle of its first movement.

six p s.text: The clarinets softly introduce the secondary theme of this symphony's third movement, which is an upbeat march in E major. For 10 points each: Though Tchaikovsky called for a bassoon to complete that solo, a bass clarinet is preferred because it handles this unusual dynamic marking better. You may give your answer as a number of letters, e.g. "fifteen Ls."

scherzos.text: The clarinets softly introduce the secondary theme of this symphony's third movement, which is an upbeat march in E major. For 10 points each: Pathetique's third movement is one of these pieces, typical for the third movement of Romantic symphonies. The name of these pieces is Italian for "joke."

johann pachelbel.text: Several Baroque composers with large oeuvres are treated as one-hit wonders today. For 10 points each: Perhaps the most prominent example is this composer of Hexachordium Apollinis, who is today mostly known for composing a Canon in D.

te deum.text: Several Baroque composers with large oeuvres are treated as one-hit wonders today. For 10 points each: Marc-Antoine Charpentier is mainly known for setting this hymn, which is used as a fanfare during Eurovision. Jean-Baptiste Lully stabbed himself in the foot while conducting a setting of this hymn.

This character sings the aria "E lucevan le stelle" while standing atop the Castel Sant'Angelo, waiting to be executed by Scarpia's men. For 10 points each: The first act of Tosca ends with Scarpia profanely joining the chorus as they sing this hymn. A motet setting this prayer is the most famous work of Marc-Antoine Charpentier.

jean joseph mouret.text: Several Baroque composers with large oeuvres are treated as one-hit wonders today. For 10 points each: This composer is by far best known for a piece that begins with the notes [read slowly] "pickup F-sharp, E, quarter notes D, down to A, back up to D" and was popularized by PBS's program Masterpiece Theater.

partita for 8 voices.text: A New York magazine review of this piece described it as having "discovered a lode of the rarest commodity in contemporary music: joy." For 10 points each: Name this a cappella piece, for which Caroline Shaw became the then-youngest winner of the Pulitzer Prize for Music. Its four movements are named for Baroque period dances.

throat singing.text: A New York magazine review of this piece described it as having "discovered a lode of the rarest commodity in contemporary music: joy." For 10 points each: In 2019, Shaw acknowledged that elements of katajjaq, the Inuit variety of this singing technique, had inspired the Partita. The Mongol and Tuvan variety of this technique is called khoomei.

sol lewitt.text: A New York magazine review of this piece described it as having "discovered a lode of the rarest commodity in contemporary music: joy." For 10 points each: Shaw credited a painting by this artist as the inspiration for the Partita. This artist was an avid collector of classical music, with the Aldrich Museum displaying his collection of almost 4,000 cassette tapes of classical performances.

teatro di san carlo.text: Stendahl wrote of this opera house "those who wish to be stoned need merely try to find a defect" when it reopened in 1817, a year after a fire had destroyed it. For 10 points each: Name this oldest still-operating opera house in the world, built in 1737 and designed by Giovanni Antonio Medrano. It was the first to use the now-common U-shaped design for opera theaters.

opera buffa.text: Stendahl wrote of this opera house "those who wish to be stoned need merely try to find a defect" when it reopened in 1817, a year after a fire had destroyed it. For 10 points each: Most of the first premieres at Naples's Teatro di San Carlo were operas in this style, which dominated 18th century Neapolitan opera. This comic style is exemplified by Giovanni Pergolesi's La serva padrona.

This opera, which was written in about three weeks, reuses its composer's overture from the earlier opera Aureliano in Palmira. For 10 points each: The Barber of Seville exemplifies this genre of opera, for which Carlo Goldoni wrote some eighty libretti under various anagrammatic pseudonyms. Its name, which is Italian, translates to "comic opera."

scarlatti.text: Stendahl wrote of this opera house "those who wish to be stoned need merely try to find a defect" when it reopened in 1817, a year after a fire had destroyed it. For 10 points each: One of the first Neapolitan opera buffa composers was Alessandro, a man with this surname. Alessandro's son of this surname, also a Neapolitan composer, included a "cat fugue" among his 555 sonatas.

This opera's stagework is the best-known example of the azione teatrale ("ah-tzee-OH-nay tay-ah-TRAH-lay") genre of small-scale works on mythological subjects featuring dances. For 10 points each: Though none of his 114 operas survive, a Neapolitan composer of this surname is considered the father of "modern opera." His son, who also had this surname, wrote 555 keyboard sonatas, mostly in a single movement.

tenor.text: An unwritten ninth high C is often added to the eight high Cs sung in this opera's aria "Ah! mes amis!" ("ah may zah-MEE") For 10 points each: "Ah! mes amis!" is often called "the Mount Everest" for performers with this voice type. A noted performer of Tonio is Luciano Pavarotti, one of a famous group of "Three" singers with this voice type.

One of these two characters growls, "La pagherai! Brigante!" in anger after the other rebuffs him from helping a woman dismount a cart. For 10 points each: Enrico Caruso and Luciano Pavarotti are among the most celebrated singers with this voice type to have played Canio.

The tonality of the music for the scene set in this location is built around a dissonant diminished seventh chord, which the composer also used in his opera Euryanthe. For 10 points each: In Der Freischutz, the role of Max is sung by this intermediate male vocal range. Jose Carreras, Luciano Pavarotti, and Placido Domingo formed a group of three singers in this range.

dance of the blessed spirits.text: Yuja Wang played this excerpt as an encore at her 2023 Rachmaninoff marathon with the Philadelphia Orchestra. For 10 points each: Name this four-movement ballet sequence with a prominent flute solo from the Paris version of Christoph Gluck's opera Orfeo ed Euridice. This excerpt plays after the Furies let Orpheus into Elysium.

castrato.text: Yuja Wang played this excerpt as an encore at her 2023 Rachmaninoff marathon with the Philadelphia Orchestra. For 10 points each: In the Paris version of Orfeo ed Euridice, Orpheus is sung by a haute-contre, or French countertenor, instead of this type of singer. These male singers could sing soprano parts.

Jonathan Swift whipped up a scandal by amplifying Anastasia Robinson's accusations that this singer behaved overly sexually when singing opposite her in Attilio Ariosti's Vespasiano e Artaserse. For 10 points each: Like his contemporary Farinelli, Senesino was one of these male singers whose testicles were removed to preserve their prepubescent vocal range.

In an opera by this composer, a deposed King of Lombardy performs the aria "Dove sei, amato bene?" For 10 points each: Handel's role of Giulio Cesare ("JOOL-yo CHEH-zah-ray") is often performed today by women because it was premiered by Senesino, one of these singers whose testicles were removed to sing in the soprano or contralto range.

Some of this opera's recitatives employ awkward repeated notes and bizarre phrasing to depict the stuttering of the hunchback, Demo. For 10 points each: The lead role in Giasone was for this kind of male singer, who had their testicles removed as youths to preserve their high voices.

The aria "Delizie, contente" is sung by the title character of Francesco Cavalli's Giasone, who was this kind of singer. For 10 points each: Name this type of male singer popular in the Italian Baroque opera. It was sung by adult men who never underwent the usual vocal changes during puberty, allowing them to develop a powerful high-pitched voice.

Give these Italian operatic terms, for 10 points each: Carlo Broschi, also known as Farinielli, was among the most celebrated of these male singers, whose testes were cut off before puberty to preserve their high voices.

Answer some questions about the works that Handel composed whilst he was in the UK. For 10 points each: 8 arias in Giulio Cesare were written for Senesino, one of these singers, who later moved to the Opera of the Nobility to sing alongside another of these singers, Farinelli.

don juan.text: Yuja Wang played this excerpt as an encore at her 2023 Rachmaninoff marathon with the Philadelphia Orchestra. For 10 points each: Gluck originally wrote "Dance of the Blessed Spirits" for a ballet about this womanizer who is dragged to Hell at the end of a Mozart opera.

batter my heart.text: This opera's libretto includes passages from the Bhagavad Gita and Charles Baudelaire. For 10 points each: To close out Act I, J. Robert Oppenheimer performs this aria in which he repeats "knock, breathe" and "break, blow." The aria's common title or the poem it was drawn from is acceptable.

satyagraha.text: This opera was the only one made in collaboration between its composer and librettist Constance DeJong. For 10 points each: Name this opera about a lawyer and politician, whose first act is named after Leo Tolstoy. This opera's title roughly translates to "truth force."

portrait trilogy.text: This opera was the only one made in collaboration between its composer and librettist Constance DeJong. For 10 points each: Satyagraha is the second in this group of operas, which focus on leaders who changed history. In the first opera from this group, repetitive "knee plays" separate acts.

das rheingold.text: A leitmotiv from this opera, representing Alberich's Tarnhelm, is frequently used to illustrate an LP transformation on a minor triad. For 10 points each: Name this opera whose popular instrumental excerpts include the "Descent into Nibelheim" and "Entry of the Gods into Valhalla" and whose prelude is followed by the playful singing of three maidens.

This opera begins with a 136-bar drone that builds up to an E-flat chord, while the cellos play a "Nature" motif. For 10 points each: Name this Richard Wagner opera, which is the first in a cycle that ends with Gotterdammerung (GER-terDAHM-err-oong) and is played annually at the Bayreuth (bye-ROYT) festival.

In this opera, the orchestra plays the "death-curse" leitmotif when Fafner kills Fasolt. For 10 points each: Name this first opera of the Ring Cycle, in which the ring is forged from the title substance.

In this opera's third scene, Wotan and Loge travel to Nibelheim and kidnap Alberich in order to pay Fasolt and Fafnir for constructing Valhalla. For 10 points each: Name this Richard Wagner opera, in which Alberich forges a magic ring, but places a curse upon it after seeing it stolen by Wotan.

ring.text: A leitmotiv from this opera, representing Alberich's Tarnhelm, is frequently used to illustrate an LP transformation on a minor triad. For 10 points each: Das Rheingold ends with Alberich cursing this object, which Fafnir promptly murders Fasolt to obtain. It names the larger work that Das Rheingold is part of.

darth vader.text: A leitmotiv from this opera, representing Alberich's Tarnhelm, is frequently used to illustrate an LP transformation on a minor triad. For 10 points each: In another work, this character is represented by a melody built on the Tarnhelm progression. Most phrases in the theme representing this character end with a rising E-flat, B-flat over the E-flat minor chord resolving to a G over a G minor chord.

au fond du temple saint.text: Claims to "Let us swear to remain friends!" and "let nothing part us" are sung in this duet. For 10 points each: Name this duet. This act one duet is sung by Nadir and Zurga upon their reunion in Ceylon.

In Act Three of the opera in which he appears, this character has his hat shot off of his head by his rival, Don Jose. For 10 points each: Nadir and Zurga sing of how they agreed to renounce the woman they both fell in love with, in order to preserve their bond, in this celebrated "friendship duet" from The Pearl Fishers.

birgit nilsson.text: This singer had a rocky relationship with Herbert von Karajan ("CAH-rah-yahn"), who once asked if she paid for her pearl necklace with her La Scala fees. For 10 points each: Name this Swedish soprano whose portrayal of Turandot formed a partnership with Franco Corelli's Calaf at the Metropolitan Opera. Placido Domingo was the first winner of a prize founded by this soprano, which is the largest prize in classical music.

The second opera in this cycle ends with Wotan ("VO-tan") imprisoning Brunnhilde behind a curtain of magic fire. For 10 points each: This Swedish soprano, celebrated for her tremendous power and stamina, was considered the world's leading Brunnhilde during the 50s and 60s. She sang the role under Georg Solti in the first-ever complete recording of the Ring cycle.

der freischutz.text: This singer had a rocky relationship with Herbert von Karajan ("CAH-rah-yahn"), who once asked if she paid for her pearl necklace with her La Scala fees. For 10 points each: A young Nilsson allegedly considered suicide after falling out with conductor Leo Blech during her portrayal of Agathe in this opera. The "Huntsmen's Chorus" appears in this Carl Maria von Weber ("VAY-ber") opera about the marksman Max.

The tonality of the music for the scene set in this location is built around a dissonant diminished seventh chord, which the composer also used in his opera Euryanthe. For 10 points each: The Wolf's Glen scene occurs in this Carl Maria von Weber ("VAY-bur") opera, in which Kaspar goes there and summons the devil Samiel ("zah-MEEL").

english.text: In an opera in this language, the veils of a clairvoyant are removed to reveal a lotus flower underneath. For 10 points each: Name this language of a Jungian-influenced opera in which the Ancients perform the four "Ritual Dances." In another opera in this language, a boy keeps singing the word "Malo" during a Latin lesson.

possente spirto.text: An opera by this composer opens with a toccata before the personification of music delivers a prologue. For 10 points each: The vocal highlight of L'Orfeo is this aria that includes a call and response with the trumpet in one of its verses. Monteverdi wrote two alternative vocal lines for this aria in which Orpheus tries to convince Charon to ferry him across the Styx.

ancient rome.text: An opera by this composer opens with a toccata before the personification of music delivers a prologue. For 10 points each: Monteverdi's last opera is set in this civilization and ends with the duet "Pur ti miro, pur ti godo." The castrato Senesino ("say-nay-ZEE-no") premiered the role of a ruler of this civilization who sings "Va tacito e nascosto" ("vah TAH-chee-toh ay noss-COAST-oh") in an opera by Handel.

florencia.text: A soprano traveling on the steamboat El Dorado transforms into one of these animals at the end of a 1996 opera in which the journalist Rosalba repeatedly loses her notebook. For 10 points each: In a magical realist opera by Daniel Catan, this soprano transforms into a butterfly to reunite with her lover Cristobal. This character titles that opera, which was the first in Spanish to be commissioned by an American company.

star spangled banner.text: A soprano traveling on the steamboat El Dorado transforms into one of these animals at the end of a 1996 opera in which the journalist Rosalba repeatedly loses her notebook. For 10 points each: On several occasions, Puccini's score for Madama Butterfly represents Cio-Cio San's husband with a motif based on this song.

canio or tonio.text: One of these two characters growls, "La pagherai! Brigante!" in anger after the other rebuffs him from helping a woman dismount a cart. For 10 points each: Name either of these two characters who may sing the closing line "la commedia e finita!" after the stabbing of Nedda. These two characters appear in an opera often performed as a double bill with Cavalleria Rusticana.

si puo.text: One of these two characters growls, "La pagherai! Brigante!" in anger after the other rebuffs him from helping a woman dismount a cart. For 10 points each: Tonio, in character as Taddeo, sings these two Italian words twice to the audience's "ladies" and "gentlemen" to kick off the prologue of Pagliacci ("pall-YAH-chee"). These two words precede "morir" four times in the final stanza of "Una furtiva lagrima."

senesino.text: Jonathan Swift whipped up a scandal by amplifying Anastasia Robinson's accusations that this singer behaved overly sexually when singing opposite her in Attilio Ariosti's Vespasiano e Artaserse. For 10 points each: Name this singer who created the roles of Bertarido in Rodelinda, the title role in Orlando, and a title character who sings the aria "Va tacito e nascosto ("vah TAH-chee-toh ay nah-SCOHSS-toh")."

grand inquisitor.text: This character was played by Jerome Hines in a 1980 Metropolitan Opera production, where he sang opposite Paul Plishka. For 10 points each: Name this villainous opera character. Act One of the 1883 version of that opera ends with a king warning a nobleman to beware this character. This blind man sings with that king in a long bass duet that directly follows the aria "Ella giammai m'amo ("EL-luh JAHM-mye mah-MOH")."

jenufa.text: After hearing this opera during its 1916 run in Prague, a Czech author went on to translate most of the composer's operas into German, finally granting them international success. For 10 points each: Name this opera. The Kostelnicka ("KOSS-tell-NEECH-kuh") murders the baby of this opera's title character, who marries Laca ("LAH-tsuh") after he slashes her face with a knife to make her too ugly for Steva to marry.

max brod.text: After hearing this opera during its 1916 run in Prague, a Czech author went on to translate most of the composer's operas into German, finally granting them international success. For 10 points each: Jenufa was the first of many Janacek ("yuh-NAH-check") operas translated by this Czech author, Janacek's main advocate. He later wrote Die Musik Israels, the first comprehensive study of the music of Israel, where he settled after fleeing Prague in 1939.

bedrich smetana.text: After hearing this opera during its 1916 run in Prague, a Czech author went on to translate most of the composer's operas into German, finally granting them international success. For 10 points each: Brod praised this earlier Czech composer for mixing nationalism and cosmopolitanism. Brod translated this composer's opera The Bartered Bride.

A character in this opera claims to value love over beer, but later betrays his lover by accepting 300 florins. For 10 points each: This composer of The Bartered Bride wrote a string quartet that uses a sustained high E by the violin to represent his deafness. He wrote a set of six symphonic poems called Ma vlast.

In this opera, Milada brings a fiddle to the title character and falls in love with him, though she had earlier demanded his death. For 10 points: Dalibor and The Brandenburgers in Bohemia are two of the many nationalistic works by this Czech composer of The Bartered Bride.

vesti la giubba.text: This aria includes a tradition of letting the voice crack in a sob-note at its climax. For 10 points each: Name this aria in which Canio bemoans having to perform immediately after hearing of his wife's infidelity. This aria's title expresses the sentiment that "the show must go on."

This character's cavatina "Qual fiamma aveva nel guardo" is interrupted by high trills in the violins and flutes to transition to her cabaletta, "Stridono lassu." For 10 points each: At the end of Act 1, Nedda's husband Canio sings this tenor aria, in which he tells himself to "put on your costume" and "laugh clown, at your broken love!"

Enrico Caruso's 1902 recording of this aria was the first record to sell over 1 million copies. For 10 points each: Name this aria sung by a character who has just learned that his wife, Nedda, is cheating on him. Although the singer of this aria encourages himself to put on his make-up despite his sadness, he ends this aria in tears.

This aria is sung after one character discovers that his love agrees to run away with Silvio. For 10 points each: Name this aria that exhorts its singer to "Turn [his] distress and tears into jest." It is sung by Canio in Act I of the opera it appears in.

In this opera, Santuzza details her seduction in "Voi le sapete", shortly after the singing of the Easter hymn. For 10 points each: In this aria from Pagliacci, Canio tells himself to laugh, in spite of his misfortune, as he applies his make-up. Caruso's recording of it was the first record to sell a million copies.

clown.text: This aria includes a tradition of letting the voice crack in a sob-note at its climax. For 10 points each: Canio is a member of this profession, which titles the Leoncavallo opera in which he appears. In "Vesti la giubba," he puts on the makeup of this profession and tells himself to "turn your distress and tears into jokes."

enrico caruso.text: This aria includes a tradition of letting the voice crack in a sob-note at its climax. For 10 points each: This tenor's 1902 recording of "Vesti la giubba" was the first record to sell more than a million copies. This prolific Italian tenor was extorted by two Black Hand gangsters, whom he later helped apprehend.

This opera was controversially referred to as a "shabby little shocker" by musicologist Joseph Kerman in his 1956 book Opera as Drama. For 10 points each: Puccini considered casting this young operatic tenor as Cavaradossi for the premiere of Tosca, but selected the older Emilio De Marchi instead, which he later regretted. This Italian tenor sold over a million copies of his recordings of "Vesti la giubba" ("VESS-tee la JOOB-bah") from Pagliacci ("pahl-YAH-chee").

farms.text: Pa Zegner sings about how "Uncle Sam will give us all" one of these places in the prologue to Missy Mazzoli's opera Proving Up. For 10 points each: Name this type of location on which the Moss family sings about "the blessings of harvest." Carlisle Floyd's opera Of Mice and Men is set at one of these places in the Salinas Valley.

aaron copland.text: Pa Zegner sings about how "Uncle Sam will give us all" one of these places in the prologue to Missy Mazzoli's opera Proving Up. For 10 points each: The folk song "Zion's Walls" provides the melody for "The Promise of Living," the final quintet sung by the Moss family in this composer's only mature opera, The Tender Land.

treemonisha.text: Pa Zegner sings about how "Uncle Sam will give us all" one of these places in the prologue to Missy Mazzoli's opera Proving Up. For 10 points each: A former slave plantation provides the setting for this Scott Joplin opera, in which Remus saves the title freedwoman from being thrown into a wasps' nest by a group of conjurers.

The title character of this opera rejects the 'luck bags' sold by the conjurers Zodzetrick, Luddud, and Simon, arguing that it is such superstition which is holding her community back. For 10 points each: Name this opera, set in post-Civil-War Arkansas, which depicts the title woman rising to the leadership of her community of former slaves through her advocacy of literacy and education.

cinderella.text: The title character of this story is a composer who completes the tune to one of Theodore's love poems in the first full-length opera by Alma Deutscher. For 10 points each: Name this story that inspired the last chamber opera of Pauline Viardot. In another adaptation, the title character of this story is known as Angelina and marries Don Ramiro.

gioachino rossini.text: The title character of this story is a composer who completes the tune to one of Theodore's love poems in the first full-length opera by Alma Deutscher. For 10 points each: The most famous operatic adaptation of the Cinderella fairy tale is this composer's La Cenerentola. This bel canto composer is beloved for overtures to operas like The Thieving Magpie.

If you went to the theater in the late 1700s or early 1800s, there was a good chance you would see the story of Figaro. For ten points each, answer the following questions about the comic character. The first Beaumarchais play about Figaro, The Barber of Seville, was turned into an opera by multiple composers: first by Paisiello ("pie-see-ell-o") in 1782, and to more enduring success by this Italian composer in 1816.

This opera, which was written in about three weeks, reuses its composer's overture from the earlier opera Aureliano in Palmira. For 10 points each: The Barber of Seville is by this Italian composer, whose other operas include La Cenerentola ("chen-eh-RENtoh-lah").

Answer some questions about the eclectic set of symphonic and operatic works played at the inaugural performance of the New York Philharmonic in 1842, for 10 points each. The performance also included an aria from this composer's opera Armida. Richard Taruskin wrote that the "single most concentrated dose" of this composer can be found in the finale to the first act of his Italian Girl in Algiers.

At the end of Act I of an opera set in this city, the ghost of the murdered king Nino emerges from his tomb and terrifies the couple who conspired to murder him. For 10 points each: This man wrote the opera seriaSemiramide after composing a series of comic operas, such as La Cenerentola and The Barber of Seville.

Born in the wake of the French Revolution, this style of opera was characterized by politically-charged topics, five lengthy acts, and an elaborate ballet scene towards the middle. For 10 points each: Centering on the Austrian occupation of Switzerland, this Italian composer's four-act William Tell is considered a forerunner of grand opera.

The undercover spy Patrick Honore Begearss appears in this opera, which features a libretto by William Hoffman and was described by its composer as a "grand opera buffa". For 10 points each: Almaviva appears in both Mozart's The Marriage of Figaro and this Italian composer's The Barber of Seville. His other operas include The Thieving Magpie and William Tell.

The actions of this animal provoke the prayer Deh, tu reggi in tal momento. For 10 points each: The Thieving Magpie is an opera by this Italian composer. Count Almaviva tries to impress Rosina in his opera The Barber of Seville.

dreams.text: The title character of this story is a composer who completes the tune to one of Theodore's love poems in the first full-length opera by Alma Deutscher. For 10 points each: A short opera by Deutscher is titled for a "sweeper" of these events. Jules Massenet's Cendrillon suggests that the main plot might be one of these nocturnal events, which are defined as "a wish your heart makes" in Disney's adaptation.

leonora.text: This composer's fondness for adapting plays set in Spain resulted in his operas Don Carlo, Ernani, and La forza del destino, which all feature characters named Don Carlo. For 10 points each: This first name is shared by the soprano lead in Il trovatore and Don Carlo's sister in La forza del destino. Beethoven wrote three operatic overtures titled for this true name of Fidelio.
Name these 19th century opera heroines who commit suicide, for 10 points each: This character takes poison to avoid submitting herself to the Count di Luna in Verdi's Il Trovatore.

csardas.text: Hungarian composers Emmerich Kalman and Franz Lehar are best known for a style of operetta named for this city. For 10 points each: This dance and the word "princess" titles Kalman's best known Viennese operetta. In an operetta by Johann Strauss II, Rosalinde pretends to be a countess by describing the "Sounds of my homeland" while imitating this traditional Hungarian folk dance.

casta diva.text: A gentle breeze lent an ethereal atmosphere to Montserrat Caballe's ("kah-bah-YAY's") 1974 outdoor performance of this aria at the Roman Theatre of Orange ("or-AHNJ"). For 10 points each: Name this aria in which the title druid of Vincenzo Bellini's opera Norma prays to a moon goddess.

bel canto.text: A gentle breeze lent an ethereal atmosphere to Montserrat Caballe's ("kah-bah-YAY's") 1974 outdoor performance of this aria at the Roman Theatre of Orange ("or-AHNJ"). For 10 points each: Caballe and Maria Callas ("CAL-uss") led a mid-20th century revival of this style of opera, exemplified by Norma and Gaetano Donizetti's Anna Bolena. This Italian style of singing emphasizes the beauty of a legato vocal line rather than romantic expression.

blindness.text: The first opera by Neapolitan composer Leonardo Vinci is about a man who fakes having this condition. For 10 points each: Name this condition developed by Andrea Bocelli after being hit with a soccer ball. Audio descriptions help audience members with this condition enjoy opera.

pyotr ilyich tchaikovsky.text: The first opera by Neapolitan composer Leonardo Vinci is about a man who fakes having this condition. For 10 points each: In an opera by this composer, Princess Iolanta's ("ee-yah-LAN-tuh's") father tries to prevent her from learning she is blind, but she gains the ability to see after learning the truth. This composer also wrote The Queen of Spades.

barcarolles.text: "Santa Lucia," a traditional song in this genre, was translated from Neapolitan into Italian by Teodoro Cottrau. For 10 points each: Name this genre, which typically uses a 6/8 meter. In an opera, Nicklausse and Giulietta sing "Beautiful Night, Oh Night of Love," an example of this genre based on Venetian gondolier songs.

arthur sullivan.text: "Santa Lucia," a traditional song in this genre, was translated from Neapolitan into Italian by Teodoro Cottrau. For 10 points each: This composer used the barcarolle for "Of all the young ladies I know" in Iolanthe ("eye-oh-LANTH-ee"). Another barcarolle is found in H. M. S. Pinafore, a collaboration between this composer and W. S. Gilbert.

Venice, as one of Europe's leading cultural centres, has served as the setting for various operas. For 10 points each: Name this composer, the first act of whose 1889 opera The Gondoliers was set in Venice. They are also known for several other operas, as well as incidental music to The Tempest.

This character makes bad singers sing to Madame Tussaud's waxwork and pool sharks play billiards with bent cues. For 10 points each: The Mikado, as well as The Pirates of Penzance, was created by the playwright William Gilbert and this composer, who frequently collaborated with him.

metropolitan opera house.text: In 2018, Lucia Lucas became the first trans singer to perform at this venue, with a rendition of Angelotti from Tosca. For 10 points each: Name this Wallace K. Harrison-designed opera house, which is part of Lincoln Center in New York City.

anna netrebko.text: In 2018, Lucia Lucas became the first trans singer to perform at this venue, with a rendition of Angelotti from Tosca. For 10 points each: Despite the Met's promise to remove the use of ethnic makeup, in 2018 this singer used face-darkening makeup during a Met production of Aida. Angel Blue has repeatedly condemned this Russian soprano who was discovered by Valery Gergiev.

Her career broke out after she played Violetta in the 2005 Salzburg Festival production of La traviata alongside the curly-haired Mexican-French tenor Ronaldo Villazon. For 10 points each: Name this dark-haired Russian soprano, the world's most sought after diva. She was discovered and mentored by Mariinsky ("ma-RIN-skee") Theater director Valery Gergiev ("vuh-LAIR-ee GAIRG-yiff").

commedia dell arte.text: In the prologue to this opera, Major-Domo declares that an opera seria and opera buffa will be performed simultaneously, causing a conflict between the Composer and the Music-Master. For 10 points each: In Ariadne auf Naxos, Zerbinetta runs off with Harlequin, a stock character in this Italian theatrical form, whose other stock characters include the Doctor and Pantalone.

gardens.text: In the opera A Quiet Place, Dede protects one of these places previously owned by his mother Dinah before Old Sam appears. For 10 points each: Name these places, one of which titles the finale of an operetta whose title character sings "You've been a fool, and so have I" before resolving to marry a woman who earlier sings "Glitter and Be Gay."

nbc.text: In the opera A Quiet Place, Dede protects one of these places previously owned by his mother Dinah before Old Sam appears. For 10 points each: Trouble in Tahiti, in which Dinah sings the aria "There is a Garden," achieved success after being performed by an opera theater named for this company, which also commissioned Amahl and the Night Visitors.

william schwenck gilbert and arthur seym.text: The first work in this genre closes with the song "Oh, joy unbounded, with wealth surrounded." For 10 points each: The genre of Savoy Opera was pioneered by this librettist-composer duo. This duo created The Mikado.

patter songs.text: The first work in this genre closes with the song "Oh, joy unbounded, with wealth surrounded." For 10 points each: Savoy Operas often employed these fast-tempo songs with rapid rhythmic patterns and tongue-twisting lyrics, such as "I am the Very Model of a Modern Major-General."

der holle rache.text: According to legend, the composer of this aria imagined his sister-in-law, Josepha Hofer, singing its top note, F6, on his deathbed. For 10 points each: Name this extremely technical aria, whose performer sings a series of high staccato notes on the word "hort" after handing Pamina a knife.

hans guido von bulow.text: A prominent chord used in this opera consists of the notes (read slowly) F, B, D#, and G#. For 10 points each: This piano virtuoso conducted the premiere of Tristan Und Isolde. After Nikolai Rubinstein criticized Tchaikovsky's first piano concerto, this musician premiered the piece.

osmin.text: After being offered wine, this character sings the duet "Vivat Bacchus" with Pedrillo. For 10 points each: Name this bass character in Abduction from the Seraglio. This character's aria "O, wie will ich triumphieren," ("oh vee vill ish tree-oom-FEER-uhn") in which he gloats about hanging his captive, is famous for a long bottom D.

emanuel schikaneder.text: After being offered wine, this character sings the duet "Vivat Bacchus" with Pedrillo. For 10 points each: The libretto for The Magic Flute was written by this man, who portrayed the comic character Papageno ("pa-pa-GAY-no") at its premiere. This man also helped design and build the Theater an der Wien ("tay-AH-ter an der VEEN").

tragedie en musique.text: The works of Corneille and Racine serve as the foundation for this genre, which typically begin with an allegorical prelude extolling the king, such as depicting him as Apollo slaying the Python of Delphi. For 10 points each: Name this operatic genre pioneered by Jean-Baptiste Lully. This genre was invented with Lully's opera Cadmus et Hermione, and became the foundation for later operas like Charpentier's Medee and Rameau's Castor et Pollux.

louis xiv.text: The works of Corneille and Racine serve as the foundation for this genre, which typically begin with an allegorical prelude extolling the king, such as depicting him as Apollo slaying the Python of Delphi. For 10 points each: Lully developed tragedie en musique as court composer for this ruler. This king's role as Apollo in Ballet Royal de la Nuit ("nwee") supported his presentation as the "Sun King."

opera seria.text: The works of Corneille and Racine serve as the foundation for this genre, which typically begin with an allegorical prelude extolling the king, such as depicting him as Apollo slaying the Python of Delphi. For 10 points each: As French opera developed, it diverged from this Italian operatic style due to French preference for recitatives and dislike of castrati singers. This style is contrasted with opera buffa.

harpsichord.text: In an opera by this composer, a deposed King of Lombardy performs the aria "Dove sei, amato bene?" For 10 points each: While rehearsing, Handel told his tenor Alexander Gordon that audiences would rather hear him jump onto one of these instruments than sing. This instrument, which plays recitativo secco with cellos in opera, was the subject of a 1716 treatise by Francois Couperin.

antonio vivaldi.text: Answer the following about 18th-century opera in the Americas, for 10 points each. This composer's rival Domenico Zipoli wrote San Ignacio de Loyola as a Jesuit missionary in Paraguay. This composer's own works include the 1733 opera Motezuma, a D-major Gloria, and the collection L'estro armonico.

adonis.text: Answer the following about 18th-century opera in the Americas, for 10 points each. This character's death ends the opera La purpura de la rosa, which premiered in Lima in 1701. Henry Purcell's ("PUR-sull's") Dido and Aeneas is modeled on John Blow's opera about this character and his lover.

beggar s opera.text: Answer the following about 18th-century opera in the Americas, for 10 points each. Popular in the colonial United States was this satirical opera that features folk songs like "Over the Hills and Far Away." Near this opera's end, a condemned man laments his fate to the tune of "Greensleeves."

lady macbeth.text: Angela Gheorghiu ("gay-or-GHEE-oo") recorded an album of this composer's "heroines," including spinto roles often sung by Lianna Haroutounian ("hah-roo-TOON-ian") and Sonya Yoncheva. For 10 points each: Anna Netrebko broke into heavier Verdi roles with a 2014 performance as this character, opposite Zeljko Lucic ("ZHAYL-koh LOO-cheech") in the title role. Ekaterina Semenchuk has given mezzo-soprano takes on this role, who sings "Vieni t'affretta."

This opera's third act includes a chorus of slaves whose desire to return home, as expressed in "Va, pensiero," has been interpreted retroactively as a coded peal for Italian unification. For 10 points each: Anna Netrebko played this other Verdi soprano role at the Met in October 2014, including the later-added aria "La luce langue." She sings "Una macchia e qui tuttora!" in a non-Bellini sleepwalking scene.

anita rachvelishvili.text: Angela Gheorghiu ("gay-or-GHEE-oo") recorded an album of this composer's "heroines," including spinto roles often sung by Lianna Haroutounian ("hah-roo-TOON-ian") and Sonya Yoncheva. For 10 points each: The Met's 2014 HD broadcast of Macbeth was hosted by this Georgian mezzo known for dramatic Verdi roles like Amneris and Azucena ("ah-dzoo-CHAY-na"). After she lost her voice in 2021, she was unable to play Eboli ("AY-bo-lee") in Don Carlo.

harry belafonte.text: A popular touring production of this opera in 1952 had a cast including Cab Calloway and Maya Angelou. For 10 points each: This artist refused to play Porgy in Otto Preminger's film version of Porgy and Bess because he refused to spend the entire movie on his knees. This artist of "The Banana Boat Song" helped popularize an entire genre with his million-selling 1956 album Calypso.

virgil thomson.text: A popular touring production of this opera in 1952 had a cast including Cab Calloway and Maya Angelou. For 10 points each: This composer fumed, "Gershwin does not even know what an opera is" after seeing Porgy. This American composer's own operas include Four Saints in Three Acts, with libretto by Gertrude Stein.

A song in this opera that nonsensically repeats the name "Lucy" may be a reference to a stock minstrel character. For 10 points each: This composer collaborated with Gertrude Stein on Four Saints in Three Acts as well as on The Mother of Us All.

ruggero leoncavallo.text: The Intermezzo from this opera plays behind the opening and closing credits to Raging Bull. For 10 points each: Because they are both short Italian verismo operas, Pietro Mascagni's Cavalleria rusticana and this other composer's opera Pagliacci are commonly staged together as a "Cav/Pag" double bill.

clowns.text: The Intermezzo from this opera plays behind the opening and closing credits to Raging Bull. For 10 points each: Pagliacci is titled for this profession of the main character Canio and his troupe. In the aria "Vesti la Giubba," Canio puts on his makeup and costume after singing to "laugh, [this profession], at your broken love!"

siegfried idyll.text: Newly-invented gas lights helped this composer achieve dramatic lighting effects never-before-seen in an opera that premiered at the Semperoper in Dresden in 1843. For 10 points each: Wagner wrote this piece as a birthday gift to his wife Cosima. This piece is named for their son, who later served as artistic director for the Bayreuth Festival starting in 1908.

gioachino.text: For 10 points each, answer the following about the career of soprano Adelina Patti. This Italian composer was much chagrined when Patti sang for him an overly ornamented performance of "Una voce poco fa" ("OO-nuh VOH-chay POH-koh FA") from his The Barber of Seville.

This character laughs at the statue-like appearance of his shocked companions in the Act 1 finale "Fredda ed immobile" [FREH dehd ee-MOH-bee-lay]. For 10 points each: Figaro helps Count Almaviva and Rosina in this Italian composer's opera The Barber of Seville. He also wrote the grand opera William Tell and the opera seriaSemiramide[say-mee-RAH-mee-day].

henri meilhac.text: For 10 points each, answer the following about the career of soprano Adelina Patti. Patti is mentioned in a couplet from La vie parisienne, with music by Jacques Offenbach and a libretto by Ludovic Halevy ("loo-doh-VEEK ahh-lay-VEE") and this man, who also co-wrote the libretti for Bizet's Carmen and Massenet's Manon.

pierre beaumarchais.text: If you went to the theater in the late 1700s or early 1800s, there was a good chance you would see the story of Figaro. For ten points each, answer the following questions about the comic character. Figaro was created by this French playwright, who wrote three plays about the character. This man also worked as a spy and participated in the American and French revolutions.

merry.text: In this operetta, Rosalind convinces a party that she is Hungarian by singing a "Czardas." For 10 points each: A Strauss operetta involving the Genoese colonel Umberto Spinola is titled for this kind of "war." In another operetta partly titled for this adjective, Hanna sings the "Vilja ("VILL-yah") Song."

alceste.text: This opera was first premiered with a preface by Ranieri de' Calzabigi with a list of reformatory maxims, including a ban on both melismas and improvisation. For 10 points each: Name this 1767 opera, an adaptation of a Euripedes work. Mozart may have plagiarized this opera for the garden scene in Don Giovanni.

Berlioz suggested that Mozart plagiarized some of the Commendatore's music in Don Giovanni from this opera, in which the title character sings "Divinites du Styx." For 10 points each: Name this 1767 opera. It was the second "reform opera" its composer wrote with Ranieri de' Calzabigi, after Orfeo de Euridice, and was written before his French-language operas about Iphigenia.

pietro metastasio.text: This opera was first premiered with a preface by Ranieri de' Calzabigi with a list of reformatory maxims, including a ban on both melismas and improvisation. For 10 points each: This pseudonymous librettist embodied many of the Baroque traditions Gluck sought to escape in works such as Artasirse. His works are rarely performed today due to their abundance of difficult castrato roles.

nuns.text: In the second opera in Giacomo Puccini's Trittico, a character with this occupation begs for forgiveness before reuniting with her dead illegitimate son in heaven after committing suicide. For 10 points each: Name this occupation held by characters such as Blanche de la Force, who stop singing as they are executed at a scaffold one by one in an opera by Francis Poulenc ("frawn-SEES poo-LANK").

robert le diable.text: In the second opera in Giacomo Puccini's Trittico, a character with this occupation begs for forgiveness before reuniting with her dead illegitimate son in heaven after committing suicide. For 10 points each: Bertram summons nuns who dance in a ballet blanc to torment the title character in the aria "Nonnes qui reposez" ("nun kee ruh-po-ZAY") from this opera by Giacomo Meyerbeer. Frederic Chopin and Auguste Franchomme's Grand Duo Concertant incorporates many motifs from this opera.

richard nixon.text: A Puccini-inspired opera by this composer is based on letters written during the California Gold Rush. For 10 points each: The aria "This is prophetic" is sung in the Summer Palace by this president's wife Pat in another opera by Adams, which tells the story of his 1972 visit to China to visit Mao Zedong.

benjamin britten and peter pears.text: Two answers required. One of these two men premiered the roles of Robert Devereux in Gloriana and King Nebuchadnezzar in The Burning Fiery Furnace, two operas written by the other. For 10 points each: Name these two men who co-founded a festival that takes place in the Snape Maltings Concert Hall. One of them premiered many tenor roles in operas composed by the other, often with the soprano Joan Cross.

war requiem.text: Two answers required. One of these two men premiered the roles of Robert Devereux in Gloriana and King Nebuchadnezzar in The Burning Fiery Furnace, two operas written by the other. For 10 points each: Pears performed with Dietrich Fischer-Dieskau ("fisher-DEE-scow") in the premiere of this Britten vocal work based on a C-F-sharp tritone. Ten days before this work's 1962 premiere, Heather Harper replaced Galina Vishnevskaya as the solo soprano.

guns.text: A hit honky-tonk song about a "mama" with these objects indirectly influenced a Doris Day film that was adapted into a musical starring Carol Burnett. For 10 points each: Name these objects that make a woman from rural Ohio "sparkle like crystal." That character originated by Ethel Merman sings that "you can't get a man" with one of these title objects in an Irving Berlin musical.

moonshine.text: A hit honky-tonk song about a "mama" with these objects indirectly influenced a Doris Day film that was adapted into a musical starring Carol Burnett. For 10 points each: Specific term required. In Annie Get Your Gun, Annie Oakley sings a "lullaby" about her father bringing this stuff from "behind the hill." Grandpa Jones recorded a folk song with possible Irish roots about this stuff, which claims "them that refuse it are few."

bluegrass music.text: A hit honky-tonk song about a "mama" with these objects indirectly influenced a Doris Day film that was adapted into a musical starring Carol Burnett. For 10 points each: Moonshine Hollow artist Jimmy Martin pioneered this genre, which inflects the scores of Bright Star and The Robber Bridegroom. This Appalachian genre is named for a plant from northern Kentucky.

antony and cleopatra.text: Answer the following about operas by John Adams, for 10 points each. Two answers required. These two characters title the most recent opera by Adams and were performed by Gerard Finley and Amina Edris to open the San Francisco Opera's 100th season. Samuel Barber was commissioned to compose an opera about these two characters for the opening of the new Metropolitan Opera House.

la scala.text: This opera house's loggionisti ("load-joh-NEEST-ee") sit in the upper gallery and have a tendency to loudly talk over performers and sometimes boo them. For 10 points each: Name this renowned opera house in Milan.

In early 20th century England, the theater in this location was the only opera venue to perform operas that weren't in English. For 10 points each: This opera house, notorious for its unruly and talkative audiences, became one of the first non-English opera houses to use surtitles when it adopted them in 1985. The opera Falstaff premiered here in 1893.

don carlos.text: This opera house's loggionisti ("load-joh-NEEST-ee") sit in the upper gallery and have a tendency to loudly talk over performers and sometimes boo them. For 10 points each: The audience at La Scala booed during this opera after Luciano ("loo-CHA-no") Pavarotti's voice cracked during the auto-da-fe scene. This opera's "Milan version" cuts the first of its five acts, which contains a hunting scene at Fontainebleau.

Two characters in this opera sing the friendship duet "Dio, che nell'alma infondere" (dee-oh, kay nell-AHL-mah in-fon-DAY-ray). For 10 points each: Name this opera whose third act ends with a massive auto-da-fe. An eyepatch-wearing princess tries to seduce this opera's title character while singing the "Veil Song."

At the opening of this opera, its title character travels incognito to a forest in Fontainebleau, where he meets his future wife Elisabetta and determines that they both love each other. For 10 points each: Name this opera in which the title character, a son of Philip II, is seized by the inquisition while Charles V, from heaven, states that suffering is unavoidable and ceases only once one is dead.

11 o clock numbers.text: Famous examples of these songs include "Back to Before" from Ragtime, "Lot's Wife" from Caroline, or Change, and the title song from Cabaret. For 10 points each: Give this term for a showstopping musical number that gets its name from its placement near the end of the musical's second act, just before the finale.

cats.text: Famous examples of these songs include "Back to Before" from Ragtime, "Lot's Wife" from Caroline, or Change, and the title song from Cabaret. For 10 points each: In this musical's 11 o'clock number "Memory," Grizabella sings "I can smile at the old days."

rose s turn.text: Famous examples of these songs include "Back to Before" from Ragtime, "Lot's Wife" from Caroline, or Change, and the title song from Cabaret. For 10 points each: In this classic 11 o'clock number, a stage mother sings "Mama's talkin' loud / Mama's doin' fine," breaking down as she comes to the realization that she sacrificed her ambitions for her ungrateful daughters.

igor stravinsky.text: Answer the following about the use of Latin in 20th- and 21st-century opera, for 10 points each. Jean Cocteau wrote a libretto in French, which was then translated into Latin, for this composer's 1927 opera Oedipus Rex.

malo.text: Answer the following about the use of Latin in 20th- and 21st-century opera, for 10 points each. After his Latin lessons, the boy Miles sings an aria in which he repeats this word before phrases like "I would rather be" and "in the apple tree" in Benjamin Britten's The Turn of the Screw.

idomeneo.text: The patricians Annio and Sesto from this composer's opera La Clemenza di Tito are trouser roles, which are male characters performed by women. For 10 points each: A mezzo-soprano usually plays Idamante, the son of the title king of Crete, in this Mozart opera.

voi che sapete.text: The patricians Annio and Sesto from this composer's opera La Clemenza di Tito are trouser roles, which are male characters performed by women. For 10 points each: Likely the most famous of Mozart's trouser roles is the page Cherubino from The Marriage of Figaro, who sings this aria that describes his feelings of love for the Countess while Susanna accompanies him on guitar.

south pacific.text: In 1953, this musical's creators threatened to cancel a performance of it in a segregated Delaware theater unless the theater could be integrated. For 10 points each: Name this musical in which the nurse Nellie rejects the French plantation owner Emile because of his past interracial relationship. The popular standard "Some Enchanted Evening" was written for this musical.

The female protagonist of this work tries to forget her love interest while showering in "I'm Gonna Wash That Man Right Out of My Hair". For 10 points each: Name this 1949 musical in which Nellie and Emile sing "Some Enchanted Evening", and Lieutenant Cable explains the origins of prejudice in "You've Got To Be Carefully Taught".

oscar hammerstein ii.text: In 1953, this musical's creators threatened to cancel a performance of it in a segregated Delaware theater unless the theater could be integrated. For 10 points each: South Pacific was a collaboration between composer Richard Rodgers and this lyricist with whom Rodgers also created The King and I.

The protagonist of this operetta instills the spirit of the French Revolution into his comrades with the marching song "Stouthearted Men." For 10 points each: This lyricist co-wrote the libretto to The New Moon along with Frank Mandel and Laurence Schwab. He collaborated with Richard Rodgers on such shows as Oklahoma, Carousel, and The Sound of Music.

you ve got to be carefully taught.text: In 1953, this musical's creators threatened to cancel a performance of it in a segregated Delaware theater unless the theater could be integrated. For 10 points each: This short song was included in South Pacific despite warnings that it could risk the entire production given its hints of a Communist agenda. In this song, Joe Cable claims that prejudice is "drummed in your dear little ear."

persephone.text: After remarking, "There's a crack in the wall," this character interrupts her song to introduce each member of the orchestra by name. For 10 points each: Name this character, who is called "Our Lady of Ways" and "Our Lady of Means" in that song. In another song, this character pleads, "Give me morphine in a tin / Give me a crate of the fruit of the vine" after hearing the sound of an inbound train.

hadestown.text: After remarking, "There's a crack in the wall," this character interrupts her song to introduce each member of the orchestra by name. For 10 points each: Persephone appears as one of five named characters in this folk musical created by Anais Mitchell. Hermes introduces each character in "Road to Hell," this musical's opening number.

The main character in this musical is told to "not come on too strong" and responds by telling his love interest to "come home with [him]" and telling her about the song he's writing to fix the seasons. For 10 points each: Name this 2019 winner of the Tony Award for Best Musical, a post-apocalyptic retelling of the Orpheus and Eurydice myth.

orpheus and eurydice.text: After remarking, "There's a crack in the wall," this character interrupts her song to introduce each member of the orchestra by name. For 10 points each: Hadestown is a musical retelling of an ancient Greek myth about this couple that has been depicted on the stage countless times, including a Jacques Offenbach opera about one of them "in the underworld."

The aria "Delizie, contente" is sung by the title character of Francesco Cavalli's Giasone, who was this kind of singer. For 10 points each: The lead male role in this opera was sung by a castrato in its Viennese premiere, but not its reworked French premiere. This opera's extensive ballet sequences include the "Dance of the Blessed Spirits."

chevalier de saint georges.text: Answer the following about opera composers who crossed paths with Marie Antoinette, for 10 points each. After Marie Antoinette failed to advocate for his placement as director of the Paris Opera, this biracial Guadeloupean composer played in her private quarters instead. This composer's only surviving opera is L'Amant anonyme.

girl of the golden west.text: Answer the following about Giacomo Puccini's attempts to incorporate authentic local musical traditions into his operas, for 10 points each. Puccini used a Zuni melody collected by ethnomusicologist Carlos Troyer in Jake Wallace's song in this opera, which follows the title female protagonist during the California Gold Rush.

An opera by this composer includes a "Drug Ballad," where the title character's son sings a long list of drugs, while laying in a body bag. For 10 points each: Eva-Maria Westbroek, who premiered the title role of Anna Nicole, played Minnie in the Royal Opera's 2019 production of this 1910 Italian opera set in California.

In this opera, Minnie cheats against a sheriff in a poker game in order to win the life of her lover. For ten points each: Name this opera, in which Minnie is courted by Rance and Sonora, but falls in love with Dick Johnson, who is actually the bandit Ramirez. This opera was criticized by some for its faux-American tone.

mo li hua.text: Answer the following about Giacomo Puccini's attempts to incorporate authentic local musical traditions into his operas, for 10 points each. In Turandot, Puccini quoted this Chinese folk song, which a children's chorus sings after the Act I invocation to the moon. This song, whose title references a flower, begins with the pentatonic melody "E [pause], E, G, A, C, C, A, G."

mikhail glinka.text: In 1884, Mitrofan Belyayev set up an annual award of 500 rubles named for this composer, which was won by most of The Five. For 10 points each: Name this composer of the oldest Russian opera in the modern repertoire, in which Ivan Susanin dies in a noble sacrifice against the Polish army.

nikolai rimsky korsakov.text: In 1884, Mitrofan Belyayev set up an annual award of 500 rubles named for this composer, which was won by most of The Five. For 10 points each: This early winner of the Glinka Award described Gvidon's decision to marry the Princess-Swan in his own tsar-based opera, The Tale of Tsar Saltan. His other works include Capriccio espagnol ("cah-PRITCH-oh ess-panYOL").

bumblebees.text: In 1884, Mitrofan Belyayev set up an annual award of 500 rubles named for this composer, which was won by most of The Five. For 10 points each: By far the most famous part of Rimsky-Korsakov's opera The Tale of Tsar Saltan is an interlude titled for the "Flight of" one of these animals.

lulu.text: Desdemona's "Ave Maria" in Giuseppe Verdi's Otello inspired an Act III scene in this opera in which the title character's wife Marie reads from the Bible. For 10 points each: Marlis Petersen almost always used a voice double to perform the bloodcurdling scream that ends this other Alban Berg opera whose title femme fatale is killed by Jack the Ripper.

anvils.text: This opera begins with a 136-bar drone that builds up to an E-flat chord, while the cellos play a "Nature" motif. For 10 points each: At the end of Scene 2 of Wagner's Das Rheingold, eighteen of these instruments tuned to F play a rhythm in 9/8 as the rest of the orchestra drops out to depict Wotan and Loge descending into Nibelheim.

rome.text: This opera begins with a 136-bar drone that builds up to an E-flat chord, while the cellos play a "Nature" motif. For 10 points each: Another use of anvils in opera is in the "Anvil Chorus" that appears in Giuseppe Verdi's Il trovatore, which was premiered and is often performed in this Italian capital city.

Although he wrote such operas as The Sunken Bell, this composer is better known for his orchestral works such as Gli uccelli, or The Birds, as well as a work that contains such sections as "Circuses" and "October Festival." For 10 points each: Respighi's aforementioned trilogy is titled after this Italian city, which is the location of the Trevi and Triton Fountains as well as the Appian Way, all of which are depicted in the trilogy.

iris.text: In the "Octopus" aria, this opera's title character recounts seeing an erotic image with the same subject as Hokusai's Dream of the Fisherman's Wife in a temple during her childhood. For 10 points each: Name this Pietro Mascagni ("ma-SCAHN-yee") opera set in Japan, which features the celebrated tenor serenade "Apri la tua finestra" and begins with an offstage choir performing a "Hymn to the Sun."

giacomo.text: In the "Octopus" aria, this opera's title character recounts seeing an erotic image with the same subject as Hokusai's Dream of the Fisherman's Wife in a temple during her childhood. For 10 points each: Like Iris, this other Italian composer set his opera Madama Butterfly in Japan. His opera La Boheme was premiered with Arturo Toscanini as conductor.

This composer had a collaborative friendship with Arturo Toscanini, who conducted the premiere of his opera, La Boheme. For 10 points each: Name this Italian verismo composer whose Il trittico consists of the operas Il tabarro, Suor Angelica, and Gianni Schicchi.

humming.text: In the "Octopus" aria, this opera's title character recounts seeing an erotic image with the same subject as Hokusai's Dream of the Fisherman's Wife in a temple during her childhood. For 10 points each: Madama Butterfly features a notable chorus using this atypical vocal technique. Heitor Villa-Lobos instructs the soprano to use this technique in part of Bachianas Brasileiras no. 5.

mary.text: After this opera's premiere, its composer's mentor Charles Gounod ("goo-NOH") supposedly exclaimed, "take mine out of the score and there remains nothing to [its composer's] credit but the sauce that masks the fish!" For 10 points each: Gounod may have taken the high ground regarding his student Georges Bizet's plagiarism in Carmen since he cribbed J.S. Bach's melody for a setting of a Latin prayer titled "ave" this religious figure.

pearl fishers.text: After this opera's premiere, its composer's mentor Charles Gounod ("goo-NOH") supposedly exclaimed, "take mine out of the score and there remains nothing to [its composer's] credit but the sauce that masks the fish!" For 10 points each: In the last feuilleton ("fooy-TON") he ever wrote, Hector Berlioz took no issue with Bizet's inspirations in a glowing review of his opera about two Ceylonese people with this profession, who sing a duet of friendship near the ruins of a temple.

In this opera, two characters vow to remain true to each other in the aria "Au fond du temple saint" ("oh FON doo TOM-pluh SAN"). For 10 points each: Name this Georges Bizet ("ZHORZH bi-ZAY") opera, set in ancient Sri Lanka, in which the friendship between Zurga and Nadir is destroyed by the latter's love for Leila.

Two characters in this opera sing the friendship duet "Dio, che nell'alma infondere" (dee-oh, kay nell-AHL-mah in-fon-DAY-ray). For 10 points each: The friendship duet "Au fond du temple saint" ("oh fawn" du TAWM-pluh SAHNT) is sung by Nadir and Zurga in this opera, in which Nadir and Zurga both love a priestess of Brahma named Leila.

The eternal friendship of two men is threatened when they fall in love with the same woman. For 10 points each: In this opera set in Ceylon, the woman in question is torn between secular love and her sacred oath as a priestess.

This character prays to God to protect her in her Act 3 aria "Je dis que rien ne m'epouvante." For 10 points each: In this other opera by the same composer, Nadir and Zurga sing the friendship duet "Au fond du temple saint" in spite of their mutual love for the Ceylonese princess Leila.

mimi.text: Answer the following about the African-American soprano Angel Blue, for 10 points each. Blue's Met debut was as this seamstress, who sings the aria "Si, mi chiamano" ("see, mee k'YAH-mah-noh") to introduce herself to Rodolfo, a poet who had sung "Che gelida manina" ("kay JEH-lee-dah mah-NEE-nah") to her after hiding her key.

This character receives a pink bonnet, which she reminisces over while lamenting her lover's abandonment in the aria "Donde Lieta Usci." For 10 points each: Name this subject of the aria "Che Gelida Manina," which is sung by her lover Rodolfo upon noticing her cold hands. This seamstress dies of consumption at the end of the opera in which she appears.

i ve got a little list.text: At a 2011 Opera Australia performance of this song, the lyrics were changed to reference "that trendy thing in opera if the plot seems like a mess: that nice surtitlist" ("sur-TAI-till-ist"). For 10 points each: Name this song whose lyrics are often changed between performances to respond to current events. The singer of this song mentions "society offenders who might well be underground" before proclaiming, "May none of them be missed."

mikado.text: At a 2011 Opera Australia performance of this song, the lyrics were changed to reference "that trendy thing in opera if the plot seems like a mess: that nice surtitlist" ("sur-TAI-till-ist"). For 10 points each: "I've Got a Little List" is sung by Ko-Ko in this Gilbert and Sullivan opera about the son of the emperor of Japan.

This character makes bad singers sing to Madame Tussaud's waxwork and pool sharks play billiards with bent cues. For 10 points each: Name this character, who decreed flirting a capital crime. In a light opera named after him and featuring the song "Three Little Maids from School," Ko-Ko marries the elderly Katisha to escape a death sentence.

othello.text: At a 2011 Opera Australia performance of this song, the lyrics were changed to reference "that trendy thing in opera if the plot seems like a mess: that nice surtitlist" ("sur-TAI-till-ist"). For 10 points each: Another audience favorite song sung by Ko-Ko in The Mikado is "Tit Willow." (pause) "The Willow Song" is a popular aria in both Verdi's and Rossini's opera adaptation of this English play; in Verdi's adaptation of this Cyprus-set play, the lead soprano sings the aria "Ave maria piena di grazia."

Venice, as one of Europe's leading cultural centres, has served as the setting for various operas. For 10 points each: The title character enters to the chorus "Esultate!" ["e-sul-TA-te!"] in Verdi's opera based on this Shakespeare play in which the husband of Desdemona is sent from Venice to defend Cyprus.

licht.text: The complex staging of an opera titled for some of these people requires the frequent banging of chairs on the stage floor as percussion and uses three film projectors for visuals. For 10 points each: Zimmermann's The Soldiers is considered the most important German opera between Berg's Lulu and this later cycle of operas themed around the days of the week. Three-note leitmotifs ("LIGHT-moh-TEEFS") on specific instruments represent characters like Eve, Lucifer, and the archangel Michael in this series.

giasone.text: Some of this opera's recitatives employ awkward repeated notes and bizarre phrasing to depict the stuttering of the hunchback, Demo. For 10 points each: Name this 1649 opera by Francesco Cavalli ("kah-VAH-lee"), which is often claimed to be the most popular opera of the 17th century. In it, the title character praises the pleasures of love to Ercole in the aria "Delizie ("day-LEETS-yay"), contenti."

xerxes.text: Some of this opera's recitatives employ awkward repeated notes and bizarre phrasing to depict the stuttering of the hunchback, Demo. For 10 points each: Another opera by Cavalli uses a castrato to portray this real-life ruler. A different opera titled for this ruler begins with him admiring the shade of a plane tree in the aria "Ombra mai fu."

la sonnambula.text: A 1955 La Scala production of this opera, conducted by Leonard Bernstein, is sometimes credited with purging the excessively flashy soprano antics that had become traditional earlier in the century. For 10 points each: Name this opera by Vincenzo ("veen-CHEN-tso") Bellini, whose title character, Amina ("ah-MEE-nuh"), sings the aria "Ah! non credea mirarti" ("ah nawn kray-DAY-uh mee-RAHR-tee") after being rejected by Elvino, when she can't explain why she's in Rodolfo's room.

The soprano aria 'Ah! non credea mirarti' is sung by Amina in the last act of this opera as she crosses an unsafe bridge holding some withered flowers. For 10 points each: Name this opera in which the jealous Elviro's repeated suspicions of Amina's faithfulness are encouraged by the inn-owning Lisa, who wants to marry him.

luchino visconti.text: A 1955 La Scala production of this opera, conducted by Leonard Bernstein, is sometimes credited with purging the excessively flashy soprano antics that had become traditional earlier in the century. For 10 points each: The production was directed by this man, whose other collaborations with Callas at La Scala in the 1950s include productions of La vestale("lah vess-TAHL") and Iphigenie en Tauride("ee-fee-zheh-NEE awn taw-REED"). He also directed a controversial 1966 production of Falstaff starring Dietrich Fischer-Dieskau ("fisher-DEESS-cow").

rake s progress.text: Answer the following about W. H. Auden at the opera house, for 10 points each. With his former lover Chester Kallman, Auden wrote the libretto for this Stravinsky opera, whose title character weds the bearded lady Baba the Turk and is tempted by the Devil, who is disguised as Nick Shadow.

bassarids.text: Answer the following about W. H. Auden at the opera house, for 10 points each. Auden and Kallman wrote two libretti for Hans Werner Henze ("HEN-tsuh"), one for Elegy for Young Lovers, and one for this opera, inspired by a Euripides ("yoo-RIP-ih-deez") play set in Thebes. It is broken up into four movements like a symphony.

letters.text: In an aria often titled for one of these objects, a pattern of descending two-note phrases played by woodwinds followed by a chord from the harp musically depicts the act of creating one of them. For 10 points each: Name these objects in the colloquial name of the aria "Let me perish, but first...," which is sung by Tatyana in the first act of Tchaikovsky's opera Eugene Onegin.

werther.text: In an aria often titled for one of these objects, a pattern of descending two-note phrases played by woodwinds followed by a chord from the harp musically depicts the act of creating one of them. For 10 points each: This other opera's third act opens with a letter scene in which a woman reads letters from her love interest before singing the aria "Va! Laisse couler mes larmes" ("va, less koo-lay may larm"). Her love interest in this opera then sings the aria "Pourquoi me reveiller" ("poor-kwah muh ray-vay-yay").

mefistofele.text: This opera features the aria "Son lo spirito che nega sempre," at the end of which the title character puts his fingers in his mouth and loudly whistles along to the strings' concluding melody. For 10 points each: Name this Italian opera written after the success of a similar opera by Charles Gounod. A 2000 Robert Carsen production of this Arrigo Boito opera was revived by the Met in 2018.

leitmotifs.text: This opera's composer wrote that one character should sound like a "desperate animal on the way to the slaughterhouse" as she lets out a scream. For 10 points each: Like Wagner's other operas, Parsifal makes extensive use of these musical ideas that can represent a character, object, or emotion. The most famous of these themes, from Tristan und Isolde, contains the "Tristan chord" and represents desire.

summertime.text: African-American soprano Angel Blue headlined a Grammy Award-winning 2019 Met revival of this opera. For 10 points each: Blue's interpretation of this Porgy and Bess aria, which declares that "the livin' is easy" during its title season, has been hailed for its unique musical embellishments.

This aria's singer states that "Your daddy's rich, and your mamma's good looking." For 10 points each: Name this aria, with lyrics by DuBose Heyward, that is sung Clara in Catfish Row at the beginning of a 1935 opera.

price.text: African-American soprano Angel Blue headlined a Grammy Award-winning 2019 Met revival of this opera. For 10 points each: Blue has been compared to the Met's first African-American prima donna, who had this last name. A Black woman composer with this married surname premiered her pioneering First Symphony in Chicago in 1933.

twelve.text: One section of this opera is an invention on the single note B-natural, which is ominously crescendoed by the orchestra during a murder scene. For 10 points each: Berg and Schoenberg were both members of the Second Viennese School and thus were associated with a "technique" centering on this number of tones, which is also the number of distinct chromatic pitches.

ombra mai fu.text: Thanks to Reginald Fessenden, this aria was the first piece of music broadcast on AM radio. For 10 points each: Name this opening aria of the 1738 opera Serse, which is sung to a plane tree by the title king. It is also known as its composer's Largo.

This aria is addressed to the fronds of a plane tree by the title character of the opera in which it appears. For 10 points each: Name this first aria from the 1738 opera Serse. It sank without a trace in the 18th century, until its revival in the 19th century suddenly caused it to become one of its composer's best-known melodies.

georg fredrich handel.text: Thanks to Reginald Fessenden, this aria was the first piece of music broadcast on AM radio. For 10 points each: "Ombra mai fu" opens this composer's Serse, whose title role was premiered by the castrato Caffarelli. His other operas include Rinaldo and Giulio Cesare.

italian.text: Thanks to Reginald Fessenden, this aria was the first piece of music broadcast on AM radio. For 10 points each: Handel's operas were performed in this language, also the original language of Christoph Gluck's Orfeo ed Euridice as well as Claudio Monteverdi's operas.

ballets.text: An opera by this composer is divided into four parts focusing on love in the Ottoman Empire, Incan Peru, Persia, and North America. For 10 points each: Rameau's works are often categorized as a combination of opera and this genre of wordless dance accompanied by music. Later works in this art form include Swan Lake.

giovanni battista pergolesi.text: An opera by this composer is divided into four parts focusing on love in the Ottoman Empire, Incan Peru, Persia, and North America. For 10 points each: Rameau's lyric tragedies were slandered in the War of the Buffoons, which was sparked by a performance of this Italian composer's opera buffa La serva padrona.

storm.text: An interlude named for this kind of event begins with a wild fortissimo (fort-EE-see-moh) melody on the notes "E-flat F-flat E-flat up to C-flat." For 10 points each: Name this kind of event that occurs at the end of a certain opera's first act. Ned Keene calms a pub crowd by singing a round after a town outcast arrives during this kind of event.

george frederick handel.text: The aria "Delizie, contente" is sung by the title character of Francesco Cavalli's Giasone, who was this kind of singer. For 10 points each: Senesino and Farinelli were among the castrati who often premiered roles by this composer in operas like Giulio Cesare and Serse, the latter of which features the aria "Ombra mai fu."

jean sibelius.text: This composer wrote an opera set in the Middle Ages in which the Maiden and the Lover are reunited after the Chatelaine arrests the Bailiff. For 10 points each: Name this Finnish composer of The Maiden in the Tower. This composer's unfinished opera The Building of the Boat used his tone poem The Swan of Tuonela as its prelude.

kaija anneli saariaho.text: This composer wrote an opera set in the Middle Ages in which the Maiden and the Lover are reunited after the Chatelaine arrests the Bailiff. For 10 points each: This other Finnish composer who studied at the Sibelius Academy wrote an opera in which Jaufre Rudel dies in the arms of Clemence of Tripoli, her L'Amour de loin (lah-MORE duh LWAN).

olivier messiaen.text: This composer wrote an opera set in the Middle Ages in which the Maiden and the Lover are reunited after the Chatelaine arrests the Bailiff. For 10 points each: Kaija Saariaho got Peter Sellars to direct the premiere of L'Amour de Loin after seeing his staging of this composer's opera Saint Francois d'Assise. This French composer also wrote the Turangalila-Symphonie and Quartet for the End of Time.

oedipus rex.text: The arrival of a female character in this opera is heralded by a chorus singing "Gloria!" three times in C major chords, punctuated by short canons between the tenor and bass voices. For 10 points each: Name this opera-oratorio from Igor Stravinsky's neoclassical period with a libretto in Latin. Its title character confesses to a murder in the aria "Ego senem kekidi."

francis poulenc.text: The arrival of a female character in this opera is heralded by a chorus singing "Gloria!" three times in C major chords, punctuated by short canons between the tenor and bass voices. For 10 points each: The libretto for Oedipus Rex is by Jean Cocteau, whose play La voix humaine (lah vwah oo-MEN) was the basis for this composer's opera of the same name. This member of Les Six (lay see) wrote the opera Dialogues of the Carmelites.

louis hector berlioz.text: After being insulted by some guests, an old servant in this opera defiantly sings the battle song "Piff, paff, piff, paff." For 10 points each: This colleague of Meyerbeer praised the blessing of the daggers scene of Les Huguenots in his three articles analyzing it. This composer of the grand opera Les Troyens (luh trwa-YAN) also wrote Symphonie fantastique.

cesar.text: Answer the following about operas for literal children, for 10 points each. This 19th century composer wrote four operas based on fairytales: Little Red Riding Hood, The Snow-Bogatyr, Ivan the Fool, and Puss in Boots, which is the best known of the set.

cunning little vixen.text: Answer the following about operas for literal children, for 10 points each. Productions often emphasize the family friendly nature of this Leos Janacek opera about an animal that escapes from a forester who captures her and makes her his pet.

beethoven s 5th symphony.text: Answer some questions about the eclectic set of symphonic and operatic works played at the inaugural performance of the New York Philharmonic in 1842, for 10 points each. The concert opened with this C minor symphony by Beethoven, which begins with a short-short-short-long motif.

oberon.text: Answer some questions about the eclectic set of symphonic and operatic works played at the inaugural performance of the New York Philharmonic in 1842, for 10 points each. The first New York Philharmonic performance included both a duet and overture from this opera. Despite including characters from a Shakespeare play, this opera describes Sir Houn's journey to the court of Haroun al Rachid in Baghdad.
