iphigenie.text: This composer departed from opera seria by de-emphasizing embellishment to achieve "noble simplicity." For 10 points each: Gluck wrote two reform operas centered on this character in two places. Gluck's rival Niccolo Piccinni wrote a less successful version of the second of those two operas, where this woman serves the Scythian king Thoas.

mighty fortress is our god.text: In an opera by this composer, Catholic nobles laugh as the servant Marcel imitates the sound of cannons in the aria "Piff paff." For 10 points each: The overture to Les Huguenots quotes this song, which also provides the tune for a prayer sung by Marcel. The flute introduces this tune at the beginning of the fourth movement of another composer's fifth symphony.

seconda prattica.text: This composer invented the stile agitato ("STEE-lay ah-jee-TAH-toh") by including pizzicati and tremolos in The Combat of Tancredi and Clorinda, which is collected in his eighth book of madrigals. For 10 points each: Claudio Monteverdi coined the name for this style to distinguish his emphasis on the words of pieces, as opposed to the melodic emphasis of earlier composers. It is sometimes also called the stile moderno.

barbara strozzi.text: This composer invented the stile agitato ("STEE-lay ah-jee-TAH-toh") by including pizzicati and tremolos in The Combat of Tancredi and Clorinda, which is collected in his eighth book of madrigals. For 10 points each: Exemplares of the seconda prattica include this composer, who published eight collections of madrigals and other early Baroque songs before her death in 1677. She was adopted into a noble family by Giulio, a famed librettist of the time.

waltzes.text: Henryk Wieniawski's ("vee-nee-AHF-skee'z") Fantaisie Brillante ("fahn-tye-ZEE bree-YAHNT") on this opera's themes quotes the "Dance of the Golden Calf" sung in Act II. For 10 points each: A rendition of a piece in this genre that closes Act II of Faust, titled "Ainsi que la brise legere," ("ayn-SEE kuh lah BREEZ luh-ZHAIR") concludes Wieniawski's fantasy. The soundtrack to Eyes Wide Shut features one of these pieces which was once thought to be part of a Russian composer's jazz suite.

The invasion theme from Dmitri Shostakovich's Leningrad Symphony parodies the tune of this opera's aria "Da geh' ich zu Maxim" (dah GAY ikh tsoo mahk-SEEM). For 10 points each: Act 2 of The Merry Widow contains a famous one of these fast ballroom dances in 3/4 time that were extraordinarily popular in Vienna.

charles gounod.text: Henryk Wieniawski's ("vee-nee-AHF-skee'z") Fantaisie Brillante ("fahn-tye-ZEE bree-YAHNT") on this opera's themes quotes the "Dance of the Golden Calf" sung in Act II. For 10 points each: Faust is the best-known opera of this French Romantic composer, who also composed the Marche pontificale ("marsh pohn-tee-fee-KAHL") that became the Vatican's national anthem.

henryk gorecki.text: For 10 points each, answer the following about the career of the soprano Dawn Upshaw: Upshaw featured as a soloist on a recording of this Polish composer's Symphony of Sorrowful Songs, which unexpectedly went platinum a full fifteen years after the piece's premiere.

narrow sea.text: For 10 points each, answer the following about the career of the soprano Dawn Upshaw: Upshaw adjusted the pitches of ceramic bowls by adding and removing water at the premiere of this 2017 piece by Caroline Shaw. Composed for New York's So Percussion, this Shaw piece adapts four hymns about the River Jordan from the collection The Sacred Harp.

les troyens.text: The "Royal Hunt and Storm" that bridges two acts of this opera is often excerpted as an orchestral piece and features a chorus shouting "Italie! Italie! Italie!" For 10 points each: Name this opera in which Chorebe ("koh-REB") tells his fiance to not worry about an object at the center of a great parade during the first act. This grand opera ends after Didon ("dee-DOHN") stabs herself with a sword.

god save the queen.text: The "Royal Hunt and Storm" that bridges two acts of this opera is often excerpted as an orchestral piece and features a chorus shouting "Italie! Italie! Italie!" For 10 points each: Hector Berlioz acknowledged this piece as the influence for the "people's chorus" in Act III of Les Troyens. The American piece "My Country, 'Tis of Thee" uses the tune of this unofficial British national anthem.

regrets.text: The "Royal Hunt and Storm" that bridges two acts of this opera is often excerpted as an orchestral piece and features a chorus shouting "Italie! Italie! Italie!" For 10 points each: Performers of Aeneas's tenor role in Les Troyens often omit the high C from its aria titled for "useless" or "inutiles" examples of these notions. These notions also name a chanson by Josquin des Prez which was a favorite of emperor Charles V and based on a French love song.

olga neuwirth.text: This composer collaborated with Elfriede Jelinek ("el-FREE-duh YELL-ih-nek") on a multimedia opera adaptation of Leonora Carrington's play Baa-Lamb's Holiday. For 10 points each: Name this contemporary Austrian composer of several "video-operas" including Lost Highway, an adaptation of the David Lynch film of the same name. The premiere of this composer's 2019 opera Orlando featured Justin Vivian Bond yelling the line "fuck the patriarchy!"

luigi nono.text: This composer collaborated with Elfriede Jelinek ("el-FREE-duh YELL-ih-nek") on a multimedia opera adaptation of Leonora Carrington's play Baa-Lamb's Holiday. For 10 points each: Neuwirth's Les Encantadas is an homage to this musical teacher of hers. Works by this avant-garde Italian composer of the Darmstadt school include the anti-capitalist cantata Suspended Song and the opera Intolleranza.

rosamunde.text: The overture to this play was originally written for the opera Alfonso und Estrella. For 10 points each: Name this play written by the Hungarian librettist Helmina von Chezy ("KEH-zee"). The composer of its incidental music re-used the theme from its Entr'acte No. 3 for his String Quartet No. 13 and Impromptu in B-flat.

unfinished symphony.text: The overture to this play was originally written for the opera Alfonso und Estrella. For 10 points each: It is thought that the first Entr'acte from Franz Schubert's incidental music to Rosamunde was originally written as the finale of this Schubert piece, whose third-movement scherzo was only scored for piano.

hunters.text: The overture to this play was originally written for the opera Alfonso und Estrella. For 10 points each: The incidental music to Rosamunde includes a chorus of these people, who sing "Wie lebt sich's so frohlich im Grunen" ("vee LEBT seeshs zoh FROE-leesh am GRUE-nen"). These people sing of their "princely enjoyment and manly employment" in another chorus whose arrangement for solo violin features in the Suzuki books.

amilcare ponchielli.text: Venice, as one of Europe's leading cultural centres, has served as the setting for various operas. For 10 points each: In an opera by this composer set in Venice, Laura plans to elope with Enzo Grimaldo. A ballet depicting the passage of time is performed while Laura's vengeful husband believes she is succumbing to poison.

queen elizabeth i.text: In this opera, the young Tyrolean Tonio sings eight high C's in an aria nicknamed the "Mount Everest" for tenors titled "Ah! Mes amis, quel jour de fete" ("AH, mayz ah-MEE, kell ZHOOR de FETT"). For 10 points each: The title character of Donizetti's Roberto Devereux is a favorite of this historical queen. Donizetti invented a confrontation between this monarch and her cousin in Maria Stuarda and depicted her mother in Anna Bolena.

che gelida manina.text: Upon hearing Enrico Caruso sing this aria, its composer supposedly said "Who sent you to me-God?" For 10 points each: Give the original Italian name of this tenor aria sung by Rodolfo in an 1896 opera, in which he describes his life as a poet. The character Mimi responds to it by singing "Si, mi chiamano Mimi."

sneezes.text: A 2016 Royal Opera House production of a certain opera featured a chorus of strangely-costumed tap dancers whose interlude was punctuated by this involuntary action. For 10 points each: Name this involuntary action, a "musical" instance of which opens another opera, Hary Janos.

paul hindemith.text: A 2016 Royal Opera House production of a certain opera featured a chorus of strangely-costumed tap dancers whose interlude was punctuated by this involuntary action. For 10 points each: This composer's opera sketch Hin und zuruck begins and ends with a sneeze. This composer of the children's opera We Are Building a City outlined his distinctive theory of tonality in The Craft of Musical Composition.

love supreme.text: Answer the following about "meditations" in music, for 10 points each. The album Meditations is considered the "spiritual follow-up" to this modal jazz album. This album's first track opens with a gong clash and features the primary artist repeatedly chanting this album's four-syllable title.

deep listening.text: Answer the following about "meditations" in music, for 10 points each. Sonic Meditations is a series of exercises by Pauline Oliveros, who coined this two-word term for meditative music participation and improvisation. Oliveros founded a band by this name that specialized in performing in large, resonant spaces.

castor et pollux.text: In a dramatic scene in The Shawshank Redemption, an aria from this opera plays over the P.A. system at the prison yard. For 10 points each: Sofia Coppola directed a Kirsten Dunst-led Marie Antoinette in which the title character sees this Rameau opera. The film's excerpt shows Telaire mourning the death of one of the title characters of this tragedie en musique.

thomas ades.text: This composer's opera The Exterminating Angel requires a high A that was the highest note ever sung onstage at the Met when it premiered in 2017. For 10 points each: Name this composer of the violin concerto Concentric Paths whose orchestral work Asyla is inspired by techno music. His other operas include The Tempest.

Answer the following about extreme notes in the operatic repertoire, for 10 points each. The highest note ever sung at the Metropolitan Opera was an A above high C sung by soprano Audrey Luna in this 21st-century British composer's The Exterminating Angel. Luna played Ariel in this composer's The Tempest.

Answer some questions about some recently premiered operas, for 10 points each: This English composer premiered his acclaimed adaptation of Luis Bunuel's The Exterminating Angel in 2017. He is also known for his musical depiction of fellatio in Powder Her Face and an operatic version of The Tempest.

This composer worked with Australian playwright Meredith Oakes on an operatic adaptation of The Tempest that premiered in 2005. For 10 points each: Name this contemporary British composer, whose opera Powder Her Face contains a notorious musical depiction of fellatio. His other works include America: A Prophecy.

akhnaten.text: This composer's opera The Exterminating Angel requires a high A that was the highest note ever sung onstage at the Met when it premiered in 2017. For 10 points each: Another recent Met premiere was this Philip Glass opera, whose libretto features arias in Akkadian, Hebrew, and Ancient Egyptian. Along with Satyagraha and Einstein on the Beach, it forms Glass's Portrait Trilogy.

One work by this composer is divided into the sections "Train," "Trial," and "Field/Spaceship," and includes scenes entitled "Mr. Bojangles" and "Prematurely Air-Conditioned Supermarket." For 10 points each: In this final part of the Trilogy, the title character sings with his wife Nefertiti and his mother Tye at the "Window of Appearances." This opera's libretto is in several ancient languages, but the "Hymn to the Sun" is sung in the language of wherever it is performed.

falsetto.text: This composer's opera The Exterminating Angel requires a high A that was the highest note ever sung onstage at the Met when it premiered in 2017. For 10 points each: Like Trinculo in Ades' The Tempest, Akhnaten is played by a countertenor, who uses this register to sing unusually high pitches. This breathy and "feminine"-sounding style is often equated with a man's head voice.
Name these techniques used in singing classical music and opera, for 10 points each: In this technique, singers attempt to artificially reach notes higher than their normal vocal range.

tancredi.text: This character laughs at the statue-like appearance of his shocked companions in the Act 1 finale "Fredda ed immobile" [FREH dehd ee-MOH-bee-lay]. For 10 points each: This first opera seria by Rossini introduced innovations like the use of the chorus and shortening of the recitatives. Marilyn Horne helped revive this opera by championing its aria "Di tanti palpiti."

This opera experimented with a "good" and "bad" ending named after Venice and Ferrara respectively, in which the title character either lives or dies during a battle with Sicily. For 10 points each: Name this opera in which the Saracen Solamir falls in love with Amenaide, the daughter of a Syracuse noble family, who desires the title exiled general.

natalie dessay.text: A 1972 Met performance of this opera garnered Luciano Pavarotti seventeen curtain calls. For 10 points each: This green-eyed blond French coloratura soprano played Maria and Juan Diego Florez played Tonio in an acclaimed 2007 Met production of La fille du regiment. She retired from opera in 2013.

mark anthony turnage.text: An opera by this composer includes a "Drug Ballad," where the title character's son sings a long list of drugs, while laying in a body bag. For 10 points each: Name this Scottish composer who set Oedipus Rex in London's East End in his opera Greek. The bankrupt New York City Opera's last production was the premiere of his opera in which the title character rises from small-town waitress to Playboy Playmate, titled Anna Nicole.

i puritani or the puritans.text: The mad scene is a storied tradition in Italian opera. Name some operas featuring them for 10 points each: This Vincenzo Bellini opera contains several moments in which Elvira descends into madness after being separated from her lover Lord Arturo Talbo, such as during her Act II aria "Vien, diletto, e in ciel la luna."

l orfeo.text: A recurring ritornello in this opera is generally considered to be one of the earliest examples of a leitmotif, as it represents "the power of music." For 10 points each: Identify this opera that begins with a trumpet toccata and uses different groups of instruments to represent the two worlds it is set in, though musicians were given significant license to improvise.

chinese opera.text: Pieces in this art form include The Drunken Concubine, and revolutionary works of this kind were created during the Cultural Revolution. For 10 points each: Name this art form, often contrasted with its Western counterpart, that includes traditional ensembles accompanying onstage singers in intricate face paint.

erhu.text: Pieces in this art form include The Drunken Concubine, and revolutionary works of this kind were created during the Cultural Revolution. For 10 points each: This traditional instrument with two strings often accompanies Chinese operas. Solo repertoire for this instrument includes the short piece "Horse Race," in which the performer imitates a horse neighing.

white.text: Pieces in this art form include The Drunken Concubine, and revolutionary works of this kind were created during the Cultural Revolution. For 10 points each: Most characters in Chinese operas appear with face paint of this color, often with additional red accents to indicate bravery.

mask of orpheus.text: In this opera, three 'Passing Clouds' recount in turn the birth of Dionysus, the myth of Lycurgus, and the myth of Pentheus. For 10 points each: Name this opera, whose second act sees its title character travel through seventeen arches, beginning with Countryside, and ending with Fear.

orphee aux enfers.text: In this opera, three 'Passing Clouds' recount in turn the birth of Dionysus, the myth of Lycurgus, and the myth of Pentheus. For 10 points each: As well as featuring Birtwistle's The Mask of Orpheus, the English National Opera's 2019 season saw Emma Rice direct an adaptation of this comic operetta, in which Public Opinion tries, unasked, to salvage Orpheus and Euridice's unhappy marriage.

esclarmonde.text: Answer the following about some of the most difficult soprano roles in opera. For 10 points each: Sibyl Sanderson made her professional debut playing the title soprano of this opera by Jules Massenet. In this opera, the title Byzantine sorceress refuses the hand of Roland until he is able to win a tournament.

mad scene.text: Answer the following about some of the most difficult soprano roles in opera. For 10 points each: This operatic scene from Act 3 of Lucia di Lammermoor has historically been viewed as one of the most demanding for sopranos, which has prompted singers like Dame Joan Sutherland and Nellie Melba to appended cadenzas to end of its difficult aria Il dolce suono.

Toward the beginning of this scene, the opera's title character appears in a bloodstained dress and attempts to wipe a knife clean. For 10 points each: Name this scene which centers on the aria Il dolce suono in which the protagonist declares, "I surrender to you, oh my Edgardo! I have escaped from your enemies."

scotland.text: In this opera, two characters vow to remain true to each other in the aria "Au fond du temple saint" ("oh FON doo TOM-pluh SAN"). For 10 points each: A "Queen of the Gypsies" features in a Bizet opera titled for The Fair Maid of a city in this European country. The best-known aria from a different opera set in this country takes place after the title character murders her husband Arturo.

feodor ivanovich chaliapin.text: Sergei Rachmaninoff was one of this singer's mentors. For 10 points each: Identify this Russian basso profundo who achieved international prominence for his roles as Mephistopheles in operas by Gounod ("goo-NO") and Boito. This performer's realistic acting style influenced Stanislavsky and transformed acting in operatic productions.

modest mussorgsky.text: Sergei Rachmaninoff was one of this singer's mentors. For 10 points each: Chaliapin was particularly known for his performances as Boris Godunov in an opera by this composer. He wrote Night on Bald Mountain and Pictures at an Exhibition.

The title role of this opera was considered the signature role of both Mark Reizen and a singer surnamed Christoff who happened to share his first name with that character. For 10 points each: Boris Godunov was written by this Russian composer, though the version normally heard today was edited heavily by Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov

The title role of this opera was considered the signature role of both Mark Reizen and a singer surnamed Christoff who happened to share his first name with that character. For 10 points each: Boris Godunov was written by this Russian composer, though the version normally heard today was edited heavily by Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov

volga boatmen.text: Sergei Rachmaninoff was one of this singer's mentors. For 10 points each: Chaliapin also performed traditional Russian folk songs, most notably a song about men with this profession that was first arranged by Mily Balakirev ("buh-LAH-kee-riff"). That song includes the refrain "ey, ukhnem!", evoking the strain of men working in this profession.

dido s lament.text: The recitativo secco that precedes this aria opens with the lines "Thy hand, Belinda, darkness shades me / On thy bosom let me rest." For 10 points each: Name this aria that opens with the line "When I am laid in earth." At this aria's conclusion, the character singing it implores, "Remember me, but ah! Forget my fate."

chromatic fourth.text: The recitativo secco that precedes this aria opens with the lines "Thy hand, Belinda, darkness shades me / On thy bosom let me rest." For 10 points each: The opening measures of Dido's Lament are widely studied because it uses this melodic figure in the ground bass, also called a "lament bass." This melodic figure, which is related to the pianto, uses semitones to descend across a specific interval.

eugene onegin.text: Her career broke out after she played Violetta in the 2005 Salzburg Festival production of La traviata alongside the curly-haired Mexican-French tenor Ronaldo Villazon. For 10 points each: Netrebko opened a record third consecutive Met season as Tatiana, who sings the "Letter scene" alone in this dark drama that bookended Galina Vishnevskaya's career. Baritone Yuri Mazurok often sang the title role of this opera whose libretto was by K. S. Shilovsky and the composer, Tchaikovsky.

major general stanley.text: In his best known song, this character boasts that he can "whistle all the airs from that infernal nonsense Pinafore." For 10 points each: Name this character from The Pirates of Penzance who sings "I've information vegetable, animal, and mineral" in a patter song in which he states that he is the very model of modern holder of his namesake rank.

You know what this tournament needs? Some motherfuckin' Gilbert & Sullivan! For 10 points each: Perhaps the most famous patter song in the G&S canon is sung by this character from The Pirates of Penzance, who declares himself to have "information vegetable, animal, and mineral."

This man is the father of Mabel, who he offers in marriage to Frederick before the reprise of "Poor wand'ring ones" in the Act II finale of the work he appears in. For 10 points each: Name this man who patters that he is "very good at integral and differential calculus" and claims to have "information vegetable, animal, and mineral".

This character suffers a nightmare in which he rides a bicycle in Salisbury Plain with his eleven-year old attorney, who explains a scheme for planting tradesmen like trees so that they grow their products like fruit. FTPE: The Lord Chancellor is an example of a "patter baritone" role, the most famous of which is this character from The Pirates of Penzance who "knows the kings of England and can quote the fights historical / From Marathon to Waterloo in order categorical".

w s gilbert and arthur sullivan.text: In his best known song, this character boasts that he can "whistle all the airs from that infernal nonsense Pinafore." For 10 points each: After early successes including The Pirates of Penzance, this librettist-composer duo wrote the operas Patience and Iolanthe ("eye-oh-LAN-thee") for the Savoy Theatre. Name both.

Due to the vagaries of British copyright law, this work was premiered without any prior promotion for one night only at the tiny Royal Bijou Theatre in Paignton a few hours prior to its grander premiere in New York. For 10 points each: The Pirates of Penzance was one of several comic operas created by this duo, who also teamed up for H.M.S. Pinafore and a dozen other comic operas.

lord chancellor.text: In his best known song, this character boasts that he can "whistle all the airs from that infernal nonsense Pinafore." For 10 points each: This comic baritone sings about a time when "your brain is on fire" and "the bed-clothes conspire" in his "Nightmare Song," after he falls in unrequited love with his ward Phyllis in Iolanthe.

This character suffers a nightmare in which he rides a bicycle in Salisbury Plain with his eleven-year old attorney, who explains a scheme for planting tradesmen like trees so that they grow their products like fruit. FTPE: Name this character who declares "The Law is the true embodiment / Of everything that's excellent", before denying an Arcadian shepherd the right to marry his ward, whose hand is also sought by Tolloller and Mountararat.

rosina.text: Act 2 of one opera opens with this character lamenting her husband's infidelity in the aria "Porgi, amor, qualche ristoro." For 10 points each: Name this character who marries Count Almaviva at the end of Rossini's The Barber of Seville.

una voce poco fa.text: Act 2 of one opera opens with this character lamenting her husband's infidelity in the aria "Porgi, amor, qualche ristoro." For 10 points each: In Act 1 of The Barber of Seville, Rosina sings this aria, in which she expresses her desire to be with Lindoro. This aria is often transposed from its written E major to F major when sung by coloratura sopranos.

Anna Renzi, widely considered to be the first opera diva, created the role of Ottavia in this opera. For 10 points each: After Adelina Patti's florid performance of this aria, its composer remarked, "Very nice, my dear, and who wrote the piece you have just performed?" He then notated his own ornamentation on the high G fermata in this aria, which a character sings after receiving a letter.

scott joplin.text: This composer wrote the opera Treemonisha, in which a young woman leads her community against conjurors. For 10 points each: Name this ragtime composer of Maple Leaf Rag and The Entertainer.

The title character of this opera rejects the 'luck bags' sold by the conjurers Zodzetrick, Luddud, and Simon, arguing that it is such superstition which is holding her community back. For 10 points each: Not performed in full until 1972, Treemonisha [tree-mon-EE-sha] is an opera by this composer, who is most associated with ragtime, as in their piano works The Entertainer and the Maple Leaf Rag.

jelly roll morton.text: This composer wrote the opera Treemonisha, in which a young woman leads her community against conjurors. For 10 points each: In 1939, this New Orleans jazz musician released a recording of several Joplin compositions arranged for trumpet. This leader of the Red Hot Peppers claimed to have invented jazz.

king porter stomp.text: This composer wrote the opera Treemonisha, in which a young woman leads her community against conjurors. For 10 points each: Benny Goodman's 1935 recording of this Morton composition became a staple of the big band era. Goodman's recording of this song features a notable trumpet solo by Bunny Berigan.

le grand macabre.text: After an overture played on twelve car horns, this opera's first act opens with Piet the Pot drunkenly hiccuping his way through the Dies Irae. For 10 points each: Name this bizarre opera by Gyorgy Ligeti ("JURJ LIG-uh-tee") in which Nekrotzar destroys Breughelland.

salzburg festival.text: After an overture played on twelve car horns, this opera's first act opens with Piet the Pot drunkenly hiccuping his way through the Dies Irae. For 10 points each: An extensively revised version of Le Grand Macabre was premiered at this festival in 1997. A staging of Hugo von Hofmannsthal's Everyman is the centerpiece of this festival held annually in Mozart's hometown.

nazi germany.text: After an overture played on twelve car horns, this opera's first act opens with Piet the Pot drunkenly hiccuping his way through the Dies Irae. For 10 points each: In Le Grand Macabre, Ligeti satirizes this regime's secret police as the "Gepopo." This regime opened their Nuremberg Rallies with the overture from Wagner's opera Rienzi ("ree-ENT-see").

leporello.text: This character sings the words "Notte e giorno faticar" (No-tay ay jor-no FAH-tee-car) to quarter notes alternating between an F and a C below in the first aria of the opera in which he appears. For 10 points each: Name this character. Near the end of the opera, he is discovered by five others, scared out of his mind, usually hidden under the table. Then, the final sextet is performed.

This character accepts a peace offering of four doubloons from a man who attempted to frame and probably murder him. For 10 points each: Name this servant who repeatedly punctuates the opening aria "Notte e giorno faticar" ("NOTE-tay ay JOR-no fah-tee-CAR") with "No no no no no." This character also details the romantic conquests of his master in the "Catalogue Aria."

sydney.text: It's time to go to the opera! So for 10 points each, answer the following about opera houses. This city's opera house took Ove Arup's engineering firm 14 years to complete, including experiencing difficulties building the "shells" designed by Jorn Utzon.

zaha hadid.text: It's time to go to the opera! So for 10 points each, answer the following about opera houses. This architect's design for the Guangzhou Opera House was inspired by the banks of the Pearl River. This first woman to win the Pritzker Prize also designed the Broad (rhymes with "load") Art Museum at Michigan State University.

jean nouvel.text: It's time to go to the opera! So for 10 points each, answer the following about opera houses. Lyon's opera house was designed by this French architect, who it is named after. This 2008 Pritzker winner designed the bullet-shaped Torre Agbar in Barcelona and the Guthrie Theater in Minneapolis.

vittorio amedeo alfieri.text: Answer some questions about the appearance of Mary Stuart in European literature, for 10 points each. Lord Darnley is dubbed Arrigo in Maria Stuarda in a play by this Italian dramatist, better known for a play in which the title character kills himself after hearing about the approaching Philistine army, entitled Saul.

friedrich schiller.text: Answer some questions about the appearance of Mary Stuart in European literature, for 10 points each. Mortimer kills himself after his plan to rescue Mary Stuart from the prison is discovered in a play by this German playwright, better known for Die Rauber (ROY-ber).

joseph brodsky.text: Answer some questions about the appearance of Mary Stuart in European literature, for 10 points each. The lovesick speaker "tried shooting myself, but it is so complicated/with the weapons." in the sixth of this author's Twenty Sonnets to Mary, Queen of Scots. He may be better known for the collection A Part of Speech.

idomeneo re di creta ossia ilia e idamen.text: For 10 points each, answer some questions pertaining to operas by Mozart. This opera inspired by Greek mythology is about Ilia, the daughter of defeated Trojan King Priam. Ilia is kept as a prisoner on Crete and falls in love with Idamante, who is the son of the title character.

h m s pinafore.text: For 10 points each, answer some questions about the third of the Savoy Operas. This comic opera details the romance between Ralph Rackstraw and Josephine on the title boat, despite her father's intentions to have her married to another man.

gilbert sullivan.text: For 10 points each, answer some questions about the third of the Savoy Operas. H.M.S. Pinafore was the fourth operatic collaboration between these two men, whose other works include The Mikado and The Pirates of Penzance.

soubrette.text: For 10 points each, answer some questions about the third of the Savoy Operas. Cousin Hebe in H.M.S. Pinafore is a role for this kind of soprano, which have a sweet timbre and are often placed in comedic or flirtatious roles. Other roles for this type of soprano include Barbarina from The Marriage of Figaro and Dorine from Tartuffe.

One character in this opera instructs his cousin to pose as a notary for a marriage contract, and has to sing "Figliol non mi far scene" to get Ernesto to shut up as the title character is on the cusp of being hoodwinked. For 10 points each: Don Pasquale's Norina is a quintessential role of this kind, a stock coy and conceited female character. Sopranos with light voices and mid-range tessituras, who are just starting out, ofen sing these roles.

hermes.text: The main character in this musical is told to "not come on too strong" and responds by telling his love interest to "come home with [him]" and telling her about the song he's writing to fix the seasons. For 10 points each: This god serves as the narrator of the musical, beginning the musical by repeating "on the road to hell there was a railroad line" and then introducing the gods and men who make up the cast.

tennessee williams.text: The main character in this musical is told to "not come on too strong" and responds by telling his love interest to "come home with [him]" and telling her about the song he's writing to fix the seasons. For 10 points each: Although Hadestown is the only stage musical adaptation, the myth of Orpheus and Eurydice has been adapted into many other forms, including a play called Orpheus Descending by this author of A Streetcar Named Desire.

der ring des nibelungen.text: The second opera in this cycle ends with Wotan ("VO-tan") imprisoning Brunnhilde behind a curtain of magic fire. For 10 points each: Name this cycle of four operas by Richard Wagner, which tells the story of the deeds of Siegfried and the gods' attempts to win the title cursed object. Its first two entries are Das Rheingold and Die Walkure ("vahl-kuu-ruh").

dialogues des carmelites.text: At the end of this opera, an ensemble sings the "Salve Regina," and the number of singers gets reduced each time a character dies. For 10 points each: Name this opera in which Blanche de la Forge is forced to choose between her life and her religious oath. She chooses the latter after seeing her colleague Sister Constance walk to her death.

verismo.text: Give these Italian operatic terms, for 10 points each: This movement, exemplified by Mascagni's Cavalleria rusticana and Leoncavallo's Pagliacci, emphasized portraying life as it is, often with lower-class characters.

The one-act opera I Pagliacci was composed in this style by Ruggero Leoncavallo. For 10 points each: Identify this analogue of realism in Italian opera, whose key composers include Pietro Mascagni and Umberto Giordano.

In this opera, Santuzza details her seduction in "Voi le sapete", shortly after the singing of the Easter hymn. For 10 points each: Cavalleria Rusticana and Pagliacci are operas in this style, defined by its realistic and often violent depictions of unromanticized everyday life.

In this opera, set on Easter morning, the male lead has seduced Santuzza to get back at his old girlfriend, leading her to seek comfort with Lucia. For 10 points each: Like Pagliacci, with which it is often paired, Cavalleria rusticana belongs to this genre of Italian opera which focuses on depicting realistic emotions and characters. Verdi is often considered the supreme composer of it.

Georges Bizet reworked his incidental music to this novel into two orchestral suites, and it was first published a year after Le Petit Chose as part of Tales from My Windmill. For 10 points each: Cilea's works, like Umberto Giordano's Andrea Chenier, are notable examples of this operatic genre often marked by pronounced naturalism. It is most famously exemplified by Mascagni's Cavelleria Rusticana.

tessitura.text: Give these Italian operatic terms, for 10 points each: This term, which comes from the Italian for "weaving," refers to the range of a piece where the majority of the singer's notes lie or where the singer is most comfortable.

princess turandot.text: Joan Sutherland sang this role in a famous Decca recording opposite Luciano Pavarotti, with Zubin Mehta conducting. For 10 points each: Name this soprano role. Her first aria, "In questa reggia," ("een KWES-tah REJ-jah") explains the purpose of three riddles and culminates in a double high C with the tenor.

quando me n vo.text: Joan Sutherland sang this role in a famous Decca recording opposite Luciano Pavarotti, with Zubin Mehta conducting. For 10 points each: This other Puccini soprano aria features a diminuendo on a high B in the final words, "senti morir." A character sings this aria in Act II ("two") after encountering Marcello ("mar-CHEL-loh") in a cafe as she enters with Alcindoro ("ahl-chin-DOH-roh").

wolf s glen.text: The tonality of the music for the scene set in this location is built around a dissonant diminished seventh chord, which the composer also used in his opera Euryanthe. For 10 points each: Name this operatic location where a man has a vision of his beloved Agathe ("AH-gah-tuh") drowning. The scene set here features three upper voices repeating the eery phrase "Uhui! Uhui!" ("oo-HWEE oo-HWEE")

covent garden.text: In early 20th century England, the theater in this location was the only opera venue to perform operas that weren't in English. For 10 points each: Name this district of London which is home to, and sometimes names, the Royal Opera House.

subtitles.text: In early 20th century England, the theater in this location was the only opera venue to perform operas that weren't in English. For 10 points each: English opera houses experimented with these things in the 1920s, but they did not become widespread until the '80s. Foreign language films commonly use these things rather than dubbing over dialogue.

orfeo ed euridice.text: This opera's stagework is the best-known example of the azione teatrale ("ah-tzee-OH-nay tay-ah-TRAH-lay") genre of small-scale works on mythological subjects featuring dances. For 10 points each: Name this first "reform opera" by Christoph Willibald Gluck, from which "The Dance of the Blessed Spirits" is often excerpted.

kathleen battle.text: For 10 points each, answer the following about singers being dismissed from the Metropolitan Opera: This African-American singer was fired during rehearsals for The Daughter of the Regiment in 1994 after, among other things, reportedly demanding that the rest of the cast not look at them. After they performed with the San Francisco Opera, backstage workers reportedly started wearing T-shirts that read 'I survived the [their name]'.

anna bolena.text: The soprano aria 'Ah! non credea mirarti' is sung by Amina in the last act of this opera as she crosses an unsafe bridge holding some withered flowers. For 10 points each: Unlike Bellini's success at a young age, Donizetti wrote 30 operas before having success with this tragic opera, whose title character is still in love with Lord Richard Percy while her husband plots to replace her with her lady-in-waiting.

wasps nest.text: The title character of this opera rejects the 'luck bags' sold by the conjurers Zodzetrick, Luddud, and Simon, arguing that it is such superstition which is holding her community back. For 10 points each: In Treemonisha's second act, Remus, dressed as the Devil, saves Treemonisha from this hazard, into which the conjurers want to push her as retaliation for her refusal of their goods.

ruslan and lyudmila.text: This opera popularized the use of the whole-tone scale to represent evil in its home country; the scale has since been called "Chernomor's Scale." For 10 points each: Name this nationalistic 1842 opera based on a Pushkin poem.

nikolai andreyevich rimsky korsakov.text: This opera popularized the use of the whole-tone scale to represent evil in its home country; the scale has since been called "Chernomor's Scale." For 10 points each: Mikhail Glinka's music, particularly his operas, was an important precursor to this composer, whose Pushkin-based The Tale of Tsar Saltan contains an interlude titled "The Flight of the Bumblebee."

sadko.text: This opera popularized the use of the whole-tone scale to represent evil in its home country; the scale has since been called "Chernomor's Scale." For 10 points each: This Rimsky-Korsakov opera was based on his earlier "musical tableau" itself based on a bylina. It contains arias named for Varangian, Indian, and Venetian Guest and depicts a gusli-playing hero.

bel canto era.text: This singer prompted a namesake "debate" on account of her "great ugly voice." For 10 points each: Callas led a revival of the bravura operas of this era. This period of Italian opera, which includes works by Bellini and Donizetti, is named for the extensive use of messa di voce (MEH-sah di VOH-chay), many kinds of portamentos, and other "beautiful singing" techniques.

beijing opera.text: A variant of the huqin called the jinghu is often used as accompaniment in this theatrical genre. For 10 points each: Name this theatrical genre in which the Four Dan Actors and Four Dan Actresses specialized. This genre includes The Peony Pavilion and Farewell My Concubine.

red detachment of women.text: A variant of the huqin called the jinghu is often used as accompaniment in this theatrical genre. For 10 points each: This "model opera" is performed within John Adam's Nixon in China. This opera-ballet is interrupted by the First Lady, who thinks that a peasant girl is going to be whipped to death,

i am the wife of mao tse tung.text: A variant of the huqin called the jinghu is often used as accompaniment in this theatrical genre. For 10 points each: Pat Nixon's interruption of The Red Detachment of Women prompts this indignant coloratura aria that has become a signature of Kathleen Kim. It contains several repetitions of "the book" and brags about a man "who raised the weak above the strong."

habanera.text: This aria was based on a Sebastian Iradier piece that the composer believed to be a folk song. For 10 points each: Name this aria which opens with a spoken introduction answering the query "When will I love you?" It goes on to compare love to a "gypsy child" which has "never known the law."

The eternal friendship of two men is threatened when they fall in love with the same woman. For 10 points each: In Bizet's opera Carmen, the title character describes love as a "rebellious bird that none can tame" in this aria.

manon.text: This aria was based on a Sebastian Iradier piece that the composer believed to be a folk song. For 10 points each: Carmen is seen as a precursor to such characters as the title woman of this opera, who sings "Adieu, notre petite table." This French opera was based on a novel by the Abbe Prevost.

arthur seymour sullivan.text: This composer wrote a grand opera based on Ivanhoe and a cantata based on Longfellow's "The Golden Legend." For 10 points each: Name this composer who partnered with the librettist W.S. Gilbert. Richard D'Oyly Carte used the profits from his H.M.S Pinafore and The Pirates of Penzance to create the Savoy Theatre.

patter song.text: This composer wrote a grand opera based on Ivanhoe and a cantata based on Longfellow's "The Golden Legend." For 10 points each: Many of Sullivan's best known songs, such as his Major General's Song, are this type of rapid song in which each syllable is matched to one note. The name for this genre came from the Catholic habit of rushing through prayers.

You know what this tournament needs? Some motherfuckin' Gilbert & Sullivan! For 10 points each: Gilbert & Sullivan operettas are known for this type of song that involves a singer rattling off rapid-fire tongue-twisting lyrics. They include "My Name is John Wellington Wells" from The Sorcerer and the "My Eyes Are Fully Open" trio from Ruddigore.

Their first collaboration was a work called Thespis, or the Gods Grown Old. For 10 points each: Like the Major-General's song, "When I was a lad" is an example of this type of musical number common in Gilbert and Sullivan's works, characterized by comical lyrics sung very fast. Another example is the "Nightmare Song" from Iolanthe.

as some day it may happen.text: This composer wrote a grand opera based on Ivanhoe and a cantata based on Longfellow's "The Golden Legend." For 10 points each: This oft-parodied song from Gilbert and Sullivan's The Mikado includes "the lady novelist" and "all people who have flabby hands with irritating laughs" on a register of people who, if they were executed, would "none of them be missed".

elektra.text: In the first scene of this work, the fifth maid is taken to be flogged after she shows sympathy for the wild-looking title character. For 10 points each: Name this opera. The title character of this opera names a dissonant bitonal chord that implies both E major and C# major.

This system was first used at a 1983 production by the company of Lotfi Mansouri, who placed a video screen above the proscenium. For 10 points each: The first use of surtitles occurred during a performance of this Richard Strauss opera, whose title character is the daughter of Klytaemnestra and the sister of Orest.

head of john the baptist.text: In the first scene of this work, the fifth maid is taken to be flogged after she shows sympathy for the wild-looking title character. For 10 points each: The "most sickening chord in all opera," which is similarly polytonal, plays when this object is kissed by the lead of another opera by the composer of Elektra. An emerald, peacocks, and a veil from the Temple are rejected as gifts in favor of this object.

mystery plays.text: A piece before this opera's first act was published separately with the title "Dawn" as part of the Four Sea Interludes. For 10 points each: Britten's opera Noye's Fludde ("noise flood"), meant to be performed by young people and amateurs, is adapted from a cycle of these performances that originated in Chester. These performances were accompanied by antiphonal ("an-TIFF-uh-null") song, and many of them were created in the 16th century by the Wakefield Master.

jean baptiste lully.text: Sylvie Bouissou ("bwee-SOO"), a partisan of one of these composers, decried the other's opera Hippolyte et Aricie ("ee-po-LEET ay ah-ree-SEE") as having destroyed her preferred composer's style with a "single stroke." For 10 points each: Name these two opera composers. One of them developed tragedie en musique ("tra-zhay-DEE on myoo-ZEEK") in operas he worked on with Philippe Quinault ("kee-NO"), while the other came to prominence with a Treatise on Harmony.

baroque era.text: Sylvie Bouissou ("bwee-SOO"), a partisan of one of these composers, decried the other's opera Hippolyte et Aricie ("ee-po-LEET ay ah-ree-SEE") as having destroyed her preferred composer's style with a "single stroke." For 10 points each: Lully and Rameau are the two master French opera composers of this musical era of the 17th and early 18th centuries, which preceded the classical era.

pastoral.text: Sylvie Bouissou ("bwee-SOO"), a partisan of one of these composers, decried the other's opera Hippolyte et Aricie ("ee-po-LEET ay ah-ree-SEE") as having destroyed her preferred composer's style with a "single stroke." For 10 points each: Both Lully and Rameau wrote operas described by this term, such as Lully's Acis et Galatee ("ah-SEESE ay gah-lah-TAY") and Rameau's Zais ("zah-EESE"). An early Romantic symphony titled for this adjective depicts a thunderstorm in its fourth of five movements.

julie taymor.text: Productions of this opera frequently use Masonic themes to highlight its supposed underlying symbolism. For 10 points each: This director drew on Indonesian shadow puppet theater and bunraku in her acclaimed 2004 Met production of The Magic Flute. This director also used bunraku-influenced staging for the 1997 production of The Lion King.

shamisen.text: Productions of this opera frequently use Masonic themes to highlight its supposed underlying symbolism. For 10 points each: In bunraku and kabuki, the musical accompaniment almost always includes this banjo-like instrument. It is traditionally associated with more "popular" art forms than the lute-like biwa.

in questa reggia.text: Luciano Berio ("loo-CHAH-no BARY-oh") composed a new ending for this opera in 2002, attempting to correct the dramaturgical flaws of the standard ending written by Franco Alfano. For 10 points each: In this aria from Act 2, Turandot bemoans the death of Princess Lo-u-Ling and introduces Calaf to the challenge of the three riddles. The title of this aria translates as "In this palace."

This aria, sung by the opera's title character, explains that the penalty for answering any of three riddles incorrectly is death. For 10 points each: Name this aria sung to scare Calaf away from wanting to marry the singer, the princess and title character of the opera.

invites the statue to dinner.text: This character accepts a peace offering of four doubloons from a man who attempted to frame and probably murder him. For 10 points each: The Commendatore drags Don Giovanni to hell after Don Giovanni had earlier performed this action because Leporello was too frightened to do it himself. The statue bellows out an affirmative "Si" after this proposal.

l incoronazione di poppea.text: Anna Renzi, widely considered to be the first opera diva, created the role of Ottavia in this opera. For 10 points each: Name this opera by Monteverdi about the wives of the emperor Nero.

pants roles.text: Anna Renzi, widely considered to be the first opera diva, created the role of Ottavia in this opera. For 10 points each: Diva Maria Malibran performed Romeo in three different composers' adaptations of Romeo and Juliet, sometimes replacing music from one version with another. Give this term for operatic male roles played by women.

leitmotif.text: Heinrich Dorn coined the term for these musical particles to be similar to "guidebook" in German, because you'd need these themes to get through the composer's operas. For 10 points each: Name these recurring musical themes, which can represent people, objects, or abstract concepts.

sword.text: Heinrich Dorn coined the term for these musical particles to be similar to "guidebook" in German, because you'd need these themes to get through the composer's operas. For 10 points each: Another brassy leitmotif, first heard in the fourth scene of Das Rheingold, is for this object, which always starts with a descending octave leap before continuing with an arpeggio.

dance of the comedians.text: A character in this opera claims to value love over beer, but later betrays his lover by accepting 300 florins. For 10 points each: This is the third of the three dances in The Bartered Bride. It accompanies the arrival of a circus troupe in the village, and during it, Vasek dons a bear costume.

four saints in three acts.text: A song in this opera that nonsensically repeats the name "Lucy" may be a reference to a stock minstrel character. For 10 points each: Name this first opera with an all-black cast. It was directed by Eva Jessye and contains characters such as Plot, Chavez, and Teresa, as well as a character who sings the aria "Pigeons on the grass alas."

when i am laid in earth.text: This piece opens with a recitative section that is followed by a repeated chromatic descending fourth line in the bass that continues throughout the song. For 10 points each: Name this aria in which a Queen of Carthage asks Belinda to remember her, but to forget her fate as she kills herself.

This aria opens with the words "Thy hand, Belinda," and features chromatic word painting on many of its key words. For 10 points each: Name this aria from a seventeenth-century opera in which a character asks "Remember me, remember me, but ah! forget my fate."

abdelazer suite.text: This piece opens with a recitative section that is followed by a repeated chromatic descending fourth line in the bass that continues throughout the song. For 10 points each: Britten's Young Person's Guide to the Orchestra featured variations on the theme of this other composition by Henry Purcell, a collection of incidental music.

ludwig van beethoven.text: In a book about this opera's composer, Romain Rolland (ro-MAN ro-LAWN) analyzes how its "Prisoners' Chorus" was incorporated into the four different overtures its composer wrote for it. For 10 points each: Fidelio was the sole opera by this German composer of the Moonlight Sonata.

In an Umberto Giordano opera, Maddalena's pleading aria "La mamma morta" fails to stop Andrea Chenier (on-DRAY-uh shane-YAY) from going to one of these places, where he writes his last poem. For 10 points each: Fidelio was this deaf German composer's only opera.

gustav mahler.text: In a book about this opera's composer, Romain Rolland (ro-MAN ro-LAWN) analyzes how its "Prisoners' Chorus" was incorporated into the four different overtures its composer wrote for it. For 10 points each: This conductor and his stage director Alfred Roller are credited with the innovation of performing Leonore Overture No. 3 before the final scene, in which Don Fernando ends Don Pizarro's tyranny. This conductor left Vienna to lead the Metropolitan Opera from 1908 to 1910.

floria tosca jumping to her death.text: Before this action takes place, a character exclaims "Ecco un artista!" in response to a mock execution that she thought had gone flawlessly. For 10 points each: Name this operatic action that takes place in the Castel Sant'Angelo after Mario Cavaradossi dies, prefaced by the cry "O Scarpia, Avanti a Dio!"

e lucevan le stelle.text: Before this action takes place, a character exclaims "Ecco un artista!" in response to a mock execution that she thought had gone flawlessly. For 10 points each: When he is given an hour to live, Cavaradossi decides to write to Tosca but is ultimately unable to do so, as he recounts in this aria.

This slow aria begins with the quarter notes E-flat, D-flat, B-flat, A-flat, and is then repeated with a small flourish before the singer says she had never "harmed a living soul." For 10 points each: The clarinet introduces this tenor aria from act 3 of Tosca, where Cavaradossi is unable to hold in his emotions as he attempts to write Tosca a letter. That clarinet introduction of this aria is later played in a passage marked triple-forte by the brass as Tosca commits suicide.

The singer of this aria recalls "sweet kisses and languorous caresses" before sobbing, "I die in desperation / And I never before 6 loved life so much." For 10 points each: Name this aria sung by a painter as he awaits execution in a prison cell at the Castel Sant'Angelo.

christmas.text: A 1914 truce between French and Scottish soldiers and German troops on this holiday is the subject of Kevin Puts's (puts's) opera Silent Night. For 10 points each: Name this holiday. Tchaikovsky's ballet The Nutcracker is traditionally performed near this holiday.

amahl and the night visitors.text: A 1914 truce between French and Scottish soldiers and German troops on this holiday is the subject of Kevin Puts's (puts's) opera Silent Night. For 10 points each: This opera by Gian-Carlo Menotti is frequently televised around Christmas. In this opera, a disabled boy in Bethlehem is healed when he offers his crutch to the Magi as a gift for the newborn Christ child.

This aria is sung by the mother of the title character. For 10 points each: "All that Gold!" and "This is my box" are arias from this opera, in which the title character is cured of an ailment after giving a gift of his only possession to King Melchior.

A magician in this opera tries to gain access to the title character but ends up frightening a woman instead. For 10 points each: Menotti, the composer of The Consul, wrote this opera in which the main character offers his crutch to three kings, which gets his leg healed. This opera was composed for a Christmas television special.

nikolai.text: A 1914 truce between French and Scottish soldiers and German troops on this holiday is the subject of Kevin Puts's (puts's) opera Silent Night. For 10 points each: This composer of The Snow Maiden adapted a short story by one of his countrymen about a smith named Vakula into his opera Christmas Eve. A "Song of the Indian Guest" is often excerpted from his opera Sadko.

merry widow.text: The invasion theme from Dmitri Shostakovich's Leningrad Symphony parodies the tune of this opera's aria "Da geh' ich zu Maxim" (dah GAY ikh tsoo mahk-SEEM). For 10 points each: Name this comic operetta by Franz Lehar (leh-HAR) in which Baron Zeta plots for the wealthy woman Hanna Glawari (HAH-nah glah-VAH-ree) to marry Count Danilo Danilowitsch (dah-NEE-loh-vitch) to keep her money in the fictional Balkan state of Pontevedro.

He included the "Gold and Silver Waltz" in The Count of Luxembourg, but that piece is often included in another of his operettas. For 10 points each: This Lehar operetta includes "You'll Find Me at Maxim's." In it, Baron Zeta of Pontevedro tries to marry Count Danilovitsch to Hanna Glawari so that the fortune she inherited from her late husband stays in the country.

messiah.text: Answer some questions about the works that Handel composed whilst he was in the UK. For 10 points each: Name this oratorio which contains the Hallelujah chorus. Handel wrote this oratorio upon a 1741 invitation from the Lord Lieutenant of Ireland to help raise money for Dublin charities.

giulio cesare en egitto.text: Answer some questions about the works that Handel composed whilst he was in the UK. For 10 points each: This opera by Handel premiered in London in 1724, at the climax of his operatic career. Achila and Tolomeo woo Cornelia in this opera, which includes the arias 'Piangere la sorte mia' and Ah, sempre piangero'.

susan alexander kane.text: This character's performance in the opera Salammbo is panned. For 10 points each: Name this character whose husband finishes writing a review of her performance in the New York Inquirer.

george orson welles.text: This character's performance in the opera Salammbo is panned. For 10 points each: Susan's husband, Charles Foster Kane, is portrayed in Cit izen Kane by this actor who also directed the film.

snow globe.text: This character's performance in the opera Salammbo is panned. For 10 points each: When Charles Foster Kane dies, this object falls out of his hand; along with the sled Rosebud, it represents Kane's lost past life.

marie.text: A character with this name abandons her fiancee Stolzius for the dissolute nobleman Desportes during the first ricercari in Bernd Alois Zimmermann's opera Die Soldaten (dee zoll-DAH-tin), which is based on a play by Jakob Lenz (YAH-kop LENTS). For 10 points each: Identify this first name of the woman who is murdered under a blood-red moon in an "Invention on a Single Note" from another opera.

pietro mascagni.text: In the so-called "Octopus" aria, one of this composer's characters seems to describe the infamous artwork The Dream of The Fisherman's Wife. For 10 points each: Name the operatic composer, whose works include L'amico Fritz and the aforementioned Iris.

turiddu.text: In the so-called "Octopus" aria, one of this composer's characters seems to describe the infamous artwork The Dream of The Fisherman's Wife. For 10 points each: After returning from the army, this protagonist of Cavalleria Rusticana sleeps with Santuzza, which leads to him being killed by Alfio in a duel.

In this opera, set on Easter morning, the male lead has seduced Santuzza to get back at his old girlfriend, leading her to seek comfort with Lucia. For 10 points each: This man, Lucia's son, is the male lead of Cavalleria rusticana. He bites his rival Alfio's ear to instigate a fight to the death, which he then loses.

consul s office.text: In the final act of an opera, Magda leaves this location to go home and kill herself. For 10 points each: Identify this unnamed location where many people are waiting. A magician performs magic tricks to impress the secretary here, and Magda's husband, the dissident John Sorel, is eventually captured after showing up here.

gian carlo menotti.text: In the final act of an opera, Magda leaves this location to go home and kill herself. For 10 points each: The consul's office is a major setting in The Consul, an opera by this man. He composed the first opera specifically made for television.

This aria is sung by the mother of the title character. For 10 points each: Amahl and the Night Visitors was written by this composer, who also wrote an opera about a woman who has religious visions, The Saint of Bleecker Street.

come scoglio.text: In this aria, the singer exclaims "You audacious person, leave this place!" to reject a man disguised as an Albanian. For 10 points each: Name this difficult soprano aria in which Fiordiligi compares her faithfulness to a rock.

nedda.text: This character's cavatina "Qual fiamma aveva nel guardo" is interrupted by high trills in the violins and flutes to transition to her cabaletta, "Stridono lassu." For 10 points each: Name this character, who is stabbed by her husband before he declares "The comedy is finished!"

bisbigliando.text: This character's cavatina "Qual fiamma aveva nel guardo" is interrupted by high trills in the violins and flutes to transition to her cabaletta, "Stridono lassu." For 10 points each: Throughout the first half of Nedda's "Stridono lassu," the first harp is instructed to play arpeggios using this technique, which later became codified as a kind of two-handed trill. This technique is often used to play quiet enharmonic tremolos with two hands.

double dotting.text: The opening of this opera's prelude was plagiarized by Smetana for the dance of the mermaids in "The Moldau." For 10 points each: The first statement of the Grail Theme at the beginning of the Lohengrin Prelude uses this somewhat uncommon notational device on the first long note. In Baroque music, something like this notational device is thought to be implied in the slow sections of French overtures, which is why performers "sharpen" their rhythms.

viva vittorio emanuele re d italia.text: Gustavus Adolphus was assassinated at a court ball in 1789, and while Scribe and Auder based a grand opera on the plot, it needed to be disguised in Austrian-dominated Italy. For 10 points each: While the politics of the composer of Un ballo in maschera are ambiguous, this slogan was associated with Risorgimento. It supported the ascension of the first king of a united Italy since the 6th century.

alberto toscanini.text: Gustavus Adolphus was assassinated at a court ball in 1789, and while Scribe and Auder based a grand opera on the plot, it needed to be disguised in Austrian-dominated Italy. For 10 points each: The performance of "Va, pensiero" at Verdi's funeral was led by this Italian conductor. He gained fame as the first conductor of the NBC Symphony Orchestra, and was known for his brisk style.

gioachino antonio rossini.text: Marie-Antoine Careme may have created a dish consisting of filet mignon pan-fried in butter, topped with foie gras, and served on a crouton especially for this man. For 10 points each: This composer is better known for his operas and their overtures, including a notable use of snare drums in The Thieving Magpie and the "March of the Swiss Soldiers" in William Tell.

In its final stanza, its singer claims to be "swifter and swifter, like a thunderbolt", while earlier, he claims that "razors and combs, lancets and scissors, are at my command". For 10 points each: The Barber of Seville is an opera by this Italian, who also composed the music to La Cerentola and William Tell.
In one opera, this character sings "Contessa, perdono". For 10 points each: One of those two operas is The Barber of Seville by this Italian opera composer of William Tell.

This opera ends with shots firing that turn out to be rejoicing and not those of a firing squad. For 10 points each: The Thieving Magpie is a work by this Italian composer, who also composed the opera The Barber of Seville and William Tell.

la cenerentola ossia la bonta in trionfo.text: Marie-Antoine Careme may have created a dish consisting of filet mignon pan-fried in butter, topped with foie gras, and served on a crouton especially for this man. For 10 points each: Rossini composed the music for this opera in 24 days. In this opera, Angelina is kept hidden by Clorinda and Tisbe's father, but thanks to the philosopher Alidoro, she is saved when Prince Ramiro recognises her bracelet.

don magnifico.text: Marie-Antoine Careme may have created a dish consisting of filet mignon pan-fried in butter, topped with foie gras, and served on a crouton especially for this man. For 10 points each: Rossini gave Angelina's stepfather this name. This character interprets a dream about being turned into a donkey in Miei rampolli femminini, concluding that it predicts his family's rise to royalty.

The actions of this animal provoke the prayer Deh, tu reggi in tal momento. For 10 points each: In Rossini's opera La Cenerentola, Angelina works in the home of this count until she is set free by the fact that Prince Ramiro loves her, and not Clorinda or Tisbe.

knee play 1.text: This musical number features a droning electric organ throughout as one of the performers calls out random numbers. For 10 points each: Name this musical number. It precedes Act I of the opera it appears in and features two characters in plain white shirts and suspenders, with one reciting a Christopher Knowles ("nohlz") text describing "wind for the sailboat" and "these are the days my friends."

bed.text: This musical number features a droning electric organ throughout as one of the performers calls out random numbers. For 10 points each: This scene in Einstein on the Beach is followed by "Spaceship," scored for electric organ and soprano solo, and is divided into a cadenza, prelude, and aria. Robert Wilson's set design for this scene consists entirely of a rectangle of light that appears on the floor, floats up and rotates, and ascends offstage.

intermezzo.text: Emilio de Cavalieri and Jacopo Peri collaborated on a set of works in this genre to accompany Girolamo Bargagli's ("bar-GAHL-yee's") La Pellegrina for the 1589 marriage of Ferdinand I de Medici and Christine de Lorraine. For 10 points each: Name these precursors of opera buffa ("BOO-fuh") performed between the acts of opera seria. The "War of the Buffoons" was caused by Giovanni Pergolesi's La Serva Padrona, one of these works paired with Il Prigionier ("pree-joh-NYAIR") Superbo.

baldassare galuppi.text: Emilio de Cavalieri and Jacopo Peri collaborated on a set of works in this genre to accompany Girolamo Bargagli's ("bar-GAHL-yee's") La Pellegrina for the 1589 marriage of Ferdinand I de Medici and Christine de Lorraine. For 10 points each: Many of this composer's dramma giocoso written with Carlo Goldoni, such as L'Arcadia in Brenta and Arcifanfano Re dei matti, were performed as intermezzi in Rome. This native of Burano, who had earlier worked with Metastasio on many opera seria, also composed a number of harpsichord sonatas and concertos.

clarinet.text: The overture to Der Freischutz ("FRY-shoots") first introduces Agathe's ("ah-GAH-tuh's") Act II aria "Leise, leise ("LY-zuh LY-zuh"), fromme Weise ("FROH-muh VY-zuh")" using this instrument. For 10 points each: Name this instrument. The composer of Der Freischutz also wrote two concertos for this instrument, one in F minor and another in E-flat major, with both featuring Rondos for soloist Heinrich Baermann.

french horn.text: The overture to Der Freischutz ("FRY-shoots") first introduces Agathe's ("ah-GAH-tuh's") Act II aria "Leise, leise ("LY-zuh LY-zuh"), fromme Weise ("FROH-muh VY-zuh")" using this instrument. For 10 points each: The overture to von Weber's Oberon opens with a three note ascending theme for this instrument, with the first note double dotted. The Der Freischutz overture opens with an Adagio passage for a quartet of these instruments.

il pirata.text: This composer insisted to librettist Carlo Pepoli that "Opera must make people weep, feel horrified, die through singing" while working on an opera about the English Civil War. For 10 points each: This early Romantic opera marked Bellini's first collaboration with Felice Romani ("feh-LEE-chay roh-MAH-nee"). The tenor Giovanni Rubini was the intended performer of its title role, Gualtiero, an exiled count who loves Imogene ("ee-moh-JEH-nay").

nose.text: When its music was performed as a concert piece in 1929, this opera provoked an outcry from the Association of Proletarian Musicians, and was thus not put on again in Russia for 44 years. For 10 points each: Name this opera whose second act contains a ballad with lyrics from The Brothers Karamazov accompanied by music from the balalaika and flexatone.

dmitri shostakovich.text: When its music was performed as a concert piece in 1929, this opera provoked an outcry from the Association of Proletarian Musicians, and was thus not put on again in Russia for 44 years. For 10 points each: The Nose is by this Soviet composer of Lady Macbeth of the Mtsensk District. He composed fifteen symphonies including "Leningrad."

He included the "Gold and Silver Waltz" in The Count of Luxembourg, but that piece is often included in another of his operettas. For 10 points each: This Russian composer quoted "You'll Find Me at Maxim's" in his "Leningrad" Symphony. His operas include Katerina Ismailova and Lady Macbeth of Mtensk District.

ernani.text: At its premiere, this opera's composer insisted on casting the tenor Carlo Guasco in the role of its title bandit, which is intended for a contralto range. For 10 points each: Name this opera whose third act depicts a visit by Don Carlos to the tomb of Charlemagne. In 1904, it was the first opera to be recorded completely.

jerusalem.text: At its premiere, this opera's composer insisted on casting the tenor Carlo Guasco in the role of its title bandit, which is intended for a contralto range. For 10 points each: Verdi also wrote a grand opera titled for this city that centers on the lovers Gaston and Helene. Joseph Haydn's opera Armida is based on a witch from an Italian poem named after this city.

trial by jury.text: The curtain-raiser for this opera was the play Cryptoconchoidsyphonostomata, whose hit song "What An Afternoon!" includes such dopey lyrics as "he also moved his legs when he walked." For 10 points each: Name this 1875 one-act opera about a case concerning breach of promise of marriage. The judge in this opera sings a patter song about how he attained his position by falling for "a rich attorney's / elderly, ugly daughter."

gilbert and sullivan.text: The curtain-raiser for this opera was the play Cryptoconchoidsyphonostomata, whose hit song "What An Afternoon!" includes such dopey lyrics as "he also moved his legs when he walked." For 10 points each: Trial by Jury is the earliest surviving opera by this composer-librettist duo, whose later works included The Yeoman of the Guard and The Mikado.

jessye norman.text: In the prologue to this opera, two pieces of entertainment are ordered to be performed simultaneously to leave time for fireworks. For 10 points each: Among the most acclaimed interpreters of the role of Ariadne is this African American dramatic soprano. She also made a heralded recording of Strauss's Four Last Songs, performing with Kurt Masur and the Leipzig Gewandhaus (geh-VAHND-howss) Orchestra.

vissi d arte.text: This slow aria begins with the quarter notes E-flat, D-flat, B-flat, A-flat, and is then repeated with a small flourish before the singer says she had never "harmed a living soul." For 10 points each: Name this soprano aria in which the singer contemplates her fate after Scarpia tells her the only way she can be with her lover is if she sleeps with Scarpia.

The singer of this aria recalls "sweet kisses and languorous caresses" before sobbing, "I die in desperation / And I never before 6 loved life so much." For 10 points each: Tosca sings this aria in the second act while she deliberates whether she should sleep with the chief of police Scarpia to save her beloved Cavaradossi from execution.

la mamma morta.text: The one-act opera I Pagliacci was composed in this style by Ruggero Leoncavallo. For 10 points each: Madeleine offers her body in this soprano aria that includes the lines "A dying body is my body/ So take it./ I am already dead matter." The Maria Callas version of it was used in the film Philadelphia.

prophet.text: Act 3 of this opera contains a "skating" ballet in a forest near Westphalia. For 10 points each: Name this opera, in which Jean de Leyd is given a title by a trio of scheming Anabaptists, who intend on dethroning Count Oberthal and taking Munster for themselves. At the end of this opera Jean's lover Berthe sets fire to his palace and subsequently kills herself.

rienzi.text: Act 3 of this opera contains a "skating" ballet in a forest near Westphalia. For 10 points each: Somewhat ironically, the Jewish Meyerbeer was an early supporter of Richard Wagner, who in turn imitated Meyerbeer so much in this opera that it was called "Meyerbeer's best opera." Its title character fights Orsini and Colonna before dying in a fire inside of the Capitol of Rome.

The title character of this work is protected by chainmail from Orsini whilst watching a ballet about the Rape of Lucretia. For ten Points each: Name this opera, whose title Last of the Tribunes dies in a burning Capitol.

song to the moon.text: Its heroine replies to her father's remark that humans are full of sin by asserting that they are also full of love. For 10 points each: Before going to see Jezibaba, Rusalka sings this aria whose first stanza shifts from E flat major to E flat minor before linking to the second stanza with an ascending chromatic motif.

Jezibaba allows the title figure of this opera to become a beautiful woman, but only on the condition that she lose her voice and she and the prince be damned forever if he leaves her. For 10 points each: In this song from Act I of Rusalka, Rusalka begs the title object to carry the message of her love to the human prince for which she has fallen.

daggers.text: Its heroine replies to her father's remark that humans are full of sin by asserting that they are also full of love. For 10 points each: After being cursed by a foreign princess, Rusalka is told that she can save herself by murdering the prince with one of these weapons. In Verdi's Macbeth, Duncan is killed with one of these weapons.

rienzi the last of the tribunes.text: For 10 points each, name these things about Edward Bulwer-Lytton and the arts: This early Wagner opera in the style of a grand opera was adapted from a Bulwer-Lytton novel. Its title character leads a populist revolt in Rome, but, despite his prayer aria that opens the fifth act, he is killed under a collapsing building along with his sister Irene at its end.

The sustained A played by the trumpet which begins this opera's overture represents the war call of the Colonna family. For 10 points each: Name this opera in which the title Roman tribune sings the prayer "Allmacht'ger Vater" before being killed in the burning capitol along with his sister Irene and her lover Adriano.

lawrence alma tadema.text: For 10 points each, name these things about Edward Bulwer-Lytton and the arts: This artist's painting Glaucus and Nydia is housed at the Cleveland Museum of Art. This painter of The Roses of Heliogabalus frequently depicted scenes of ancient Roman life.

lady macbeth of the mstensk district.text: This opera's villain makes a grandiose evil speech proclaiming that "Heaven is an old wives' tale!" and "the honest man is a mockery." For 10 points each: In this Shakespeare-inspired Shostakovich opera, Katerina has an affair with Sergei, killing her husband Zinovy and his mushroom-obsessed father Boris. It ends with her dying on the way to Siberia.

babylon.text: At the end of Act I of an opera set in this city, the ghost of the murdered king Nino emerges from his tomb and terrifies the couple who conspired to murder him. For 10 points each: Name this city, the setting of Semiramide(seh-mee-RAH-mee-day). It is also the setting of an opera in which the high priest Zaccaria consoles a chorus that sings "Va, pensiero."

grand opera.text: Daniel Auber's The Mute Girl of Portici, which ends with an eruption of Mount Vesuvius, is usually considered to be the first opera in this genre. For 10 points each: Identify this primarily-19th-century genre of dramatic opera, which includes many works with libretti by Eugene Scribe (oo-ZHEN screeb). They are characterized by large casts and extravagant productions.

Born in the wake of the French Revolution, this style of opera was characterized by politically-charged topics, five lengthy acts, and an elaborate ballet scene towards the middle. For 10 points each: Name this lavish style of opera that arose in 19th-century France to appeal to the growing middle class in the wake of diminishing royal patronage of opera.

from marathon to waterloo.text: Due to the vagaries of British copyright law, this work was premiered without any prior promotion for one night only at the tiny Royal Bijou Theatre in Paignton a few hours prior to its grander premiere in New York. For 10 points each: In his eponymous song, the Major General brags that he can "quote the fights historical... in order categorical" ranging from one of these two battles to the other one.

cio cio san.text: Vladimir Rosing was the first director to cast Tamaki Miura in this role, doing so for the 1915 London season. For 10 points each: Name this soprano character who sings "Con onor muore" before stabbing herself and, earlier, dreams that "one fine day" her husband will return in the aria "Un bel di, vedremo".

la donna del lago.text: Quite a few nineteenth-century Italian composers chose to set their works in Scotland. For 10 points each: Gioacchino Rossini set a trend by setting this opera in Scotland. James V disguises himself as "Uberto" to seduce Douglas's daughter Elena, the title character of this work.

appomattox.text: One work by this composer is divided into the sections "Train," "Trial," and "Field/Spaceship," and includes scenes entitled "Mr. Bojangles" and "Prematurely Air-Conditioned Supermarket." For 10 points each: Christopher Hampton wrote the libretto for this Glass opera, in which both Ulysses S. Grant and Robert E. Lee are played by baritones. Its second act intersperses the signing of a document with scenes of racial violence in American history.

devil.text: In one appearance, this character is a huntsman who uses the name "Samiel" (ZOM-eel). For 10 points each: Name this character. In Stravinsky's opera The Rake's Progress, he uses the name "Nick Shadow," gives the protagonist a "fantastic Baroque machine," and loses to him at cards.

bullets.text: In one appearance, this character is a huntsman who uses the name "Samiel" (ZOM-eel). For 10 points each: In another operatic appearance, the Devil, as "Samiel," joins Kaspar in trying to persuade Max to create and use seven of these magical objects.

antonin dvorak.text: In one appearance, this character is a huntsman who uses the name "Samiel" (ZOM-eel). For 10 points each: In this man's opera The Devil and Kate, Kate and Jirka (YEER-kah) journey to Hell. This Czech composer's Ninth Symphony is subtitled "From the New World."

Jezibaba allows the title figure of this opera to become a beautiful woman, but only on the condition that she lose her voice and she and the prince be damned forever if he leaves her. For 10 points each: The score for Rusalka was written by this composer that used his time in Iowa to compose his ninth symphony, "From the New World".

stepdaughter.text: This character suddenly bursts out into a performance of the cabaret number, singing "Prenez garde a Tchou-Tchin-Tchou" (pruh-NAY gard ah choo-chin-choo). For 10 points each: Name this character who refuses a gift of a hat from a man in Madame Pace's shop because she is in mourning. This character then chastises him for saying "Let's take off this little frock" rather than "I understand."

six characters in search of an author.text: This character suddenly bursts out into a performance of the cabaret number, singing "Prenez garde a Tchou-Tchin-Tchou" (pruh-NAY gard ah choo-chin-choo). For 10 points each: The Stepdaughter's scene in Madame Pace's shop occurs in this 1921 play.

luigi pirandello.text: This character suddenly bursts out into a performance of the cabaret number, singing "Prenez garde a Tchou-Tchin-Tchou" (pruh-NAY gard ah choo-chin-choo). For 10 points each: This absurdist Italian playwright wrote Six Characters in Search of an Author and Enrico IV.
