arias.text: You are an opera singer. For 10 points each: The bulk of your showpieces are these sorts of expressive melodies for a single voice. Examples include "Nessun dorma" from Turandot and "O mio babbino caro" from Gianni Schicchi [JAH-nee SKEE-kee].

recitative.text: You are an opera singer. For 10 points each: The arias you perform are interspersed with passages of this type, which use the cadence and rhythm of ordinary speech. Its secco, or unaccompanied, type comprises the bulk of Claudio Monteverdi's operas.

coloratura.text: You are an opera singer. For 10 points each: You may perform complex arias written for this style of singing that uses virtuosic trills, tremolos, leaps, vibrato, and other enhancements. It is used by the title character of Lakme.

Name these techniques used in singing classical music and opera, for 10 points each: The performer who sings the "Queen of the Night" aria from The Magic Flute employs this virtuoso technique, characterized by wide leaps, trills, and other elaborate flourishes.

paul bunyan.text: Milton Warchoff voiced this character in the premiere of a 1941 operetta, in which he claims "I am the eternal guest, I am way, I am act" after Inkslinger asks him who he really is. For 10 points each: Name this character who-due to his enormous size-never actually appears on stage in a Benjamin Britten operetta titled for him.

minnesota.text: Milton Warchoff voiced this character in the premiere of a 1941 operetta, in which he claims "I am the eternal guest, I am way, I am act" after Inkslinger asks him who he really is. For 10 points each: Cyril Dickinson and James Payton built the enormous concrete and wood statues of Paul Bunyan and Babe the Blue Ox in this state's city of Bemidji, which claims to be Bunyan's birthplace.

les clark.text: Milton Warchoff voiced this character in the premiere of a 1941 operetta, in which he claims "I am the eternal guest, I am way, I am act" after Inkslinger asks him who he really is. For 10 points each: The Disney animated short Paul Bunyan was directed by this most-senior member of the "Nine Old Men," who-along with Ub Iwerks and Wilifred Jackson-animated Steamboat Willie.

coloratura soprano.text: Examples of this type of role include Madame Mao in Nixon in China and the title character of Handel's Agrippina. For 10 points each: Name this type of soprano singing style characterized by rapid, highly ornamented melodies typically scored in high octaves.

This character threatens to disown her daughter Pamina if she does not kill Sarastro in the aria "Der Holle Rache kocht in meinem Herzen." For 10 points each: The Queen of the Night is a soprano role described by this Italian word, indicating the presence of elaborate ornamentation of melodies and rapid runs, trills, and cadenzas, especially in the upper register.

The lead soprano roles of this opera, Anna Bolena, and Maria Stuarda are often known as the "Three Queens," and Beverly Sills performed all three as a series at the New York City Opera. For 10 points each: The lead role in Lucia di Lammermoor is an example of this type of soprano, who is able to sing elaborate vocal lines embellished with trills and arpeggios. Other dramatic roles for this type of soprano include the Queen of the Night in The Magic Flute.

zerbinetta.text: Examples of this type of role include Madame Mao in Nixon in China and the title character of Handel's Agrippina. For 10 points each: In Richard Strauss's Ariadne auf Naxos, this coloratura character tries to cheer up Ariadne in the difficult aria "High and Mighty Princess." In the prologue, she convinces the Composer to insert her and her comedy troupe into the opera seria he is scheduled to conduct.

Answer the following about these operas with crazy hard arias, for 10 points each. The aria "Grossmachtige Prinzzessin," which is sung by this operatic character to convince the title woman that she needs a new lover, ends with a string of high Es topped by a high F-sharp, putting "Der Holle Rache" to shame by the margin of one half-note.

renata tebaldi.text: This singer made her 1944 debut as Elena in Mefistofele, an opera she later recorded under the baton of Tullio Serafin in 1958, singing the role of Margherita. For 10 points each: Name this lyric soprano who was featured opposite Mario del Monaco in a series of classic recordings of Puccini and Verdi operas for Decca.

james levine.text: This singer made her 1944 debut as Elena in Mefistofele, an opera she later recorded under the baton of Tullio Serafin in 1958, singing the role of Margherita. For 10 points each: Tebaldi had an extremely productive relationship with the Met Opera. Her retirement roughly coincided with the appointment of this American conductor, who served as the Met's music director from 1976-2016.

amneris.text: Answer the following about the theme of jealousy in operas, for 10 points each. Verdi added a melody often called this character's "jealousy theme" to the prelude of Aida in 1872. This daughter of the Pharaoh is in love with Radames.

flower duet.text: This duet opens with lyrics about the "jasmine white" under a "leafy dome." For 10 points each: Name this duet for sopranos from Leo Delibes's Lakme, which is most commonly titled for the objects that Lakme and Malika have gone near a river to obtain.

nanki poo.text: This character makes bad singers sing to Madame Tussaud's waxwork and pool sharks play billiards with bent cues. For 10 points each: This character's rather racist name derives from British baby-talk for "handkerchief." This son of the Mikado disguises himself as a poor musician and falls in love with Yum-Yum.

speaking role.text: Most scholars now doubt that Emperor Josef II complained that there were "too many notes" upon hearing this opera. For 10 points each: Pasha Selim has this voice role in the opera. Other characters in this role include the devil Samiel from Der Freischutz and Moses from Schoenberg's Moses und Aron.

cristoph willibald gluck.text: In a letter about this opera, Mozart wrote to his father Leopold that ensembles must sing parlando, or expressively, rather than spianato, or smoothly. For 10 points each: The plot of Idomeneo was influenced by this composer whose Iphigenie en Aulide also depicts a person almost being sacrificed to the gods. This man also wrote Orfeo ed Euridice.

painter.text: A woman attracts the attention a man with this job by singing "Quando me'n vo" and leaves Alcindoro to be with him. For 10 points each: Name this profession of Marcello. Another character with this job sings "Recondita armonia" while comparing the title character to Mary Magdalene.

mario cavaradossi.text: A woman attracts the attention a man with this job by singing "Quando me'n vo" and leaves Alcindoro to be with him. For 10 points each: The singer of "Recondita armonia" is this character, who also sings "E lucevan le stelle" and the duet "O dolci mani" before he is executed by the firing squad of Baron Scarpia in Tosca.

This character sings the aria "E lucevan le stelle" while standing atop the Castel Sant'Angelo, waiting to be executed by Scarpia's men. For 10 points each: Name this painter, played by a tenor. He abets the convict Cesare Angelotti after comparing his painting of Mary Magdalene to his lover in the aria "Recondita armonia".

blonde.text: One character in this opera sings "I am an Englishwoman, born to freedom". For 10 points each: This character from The Abduction from the Seraglio is Konstanze's maid. She repels Osmin's lovemaking attempts, threatening to scratch out his eyes

die feen.text: The title character of this work is protected by chainmail from Orsini whilst watching a ballet about the Rape of Lucretia. For ten Points each: Gozzi's La donna serpente influenced this opera, Wagner's first, whose main characters are Ada and Arindal, the King of Tramond.

prison.text: In an Umberto Giordano opera, Maddalena's pleading aria "La mamma morta" fails to stop Andrea Chenier (on-DRAY-uh shane-YAY) from going to one of these places, where he writes his last poem. For 10 points each: Name this type of location where, in a different opera, a group of men celebrate fresh air in the chorus "O welche Lust" (oh VEL-shuh LOOST).

joan sutherland.text: With encouragement from her husband Richard Bonynge (BON-ing), who had conducted many of her performances, this singer rescued Jules Massenet's (mass-NAY's) incredibly difficult opera Esclarmonde from obscurity. For 10 points each: Name this Australian coloratura singer whom Pavarotti deemed the "Voice of the Century".

desdemona.text: In the fourth and final act of the opera, this character sings the prayer "Ave maria, piena di grazia, eletta fra le spose." For 10 points each: Name this character who repeats the word "salce" ("SAHL-chay") in a song she learnt from her mother's maid.

thunderstorm.text: In the fourth and final act of the opera, this character sings the prayer "Ave maria, piena di grazia, eletta fra le spose." For 10 points each: Desdemona appears in Otello, which begins with one of these weather events. The last act of Rigoletto is set during one of these events, using the piccolo to depict lightning.

arrigo boito.text: In the fourth and final act of the opera, this character sings the prayer "Ave maria, piena di grazia, eletta fra le spose." For 10 points each: Verdi came out of retirement to write Otello and Falstaff in large part because of the talent of this librettist. This librettist also wrote the opera Mefistofele.

In 1982, Carlo Bini was roundly booed at the Met upon singing this area after replacing Placido Domingo onstage. For 10 points each: This author wrote the libretto for La Gioconda, as well as the libretti for Verdi's operas Otello and Falstaff. He also composed the opera Mefistofele.

semitone.text: This character's final monologue is punctuated by an offstage chorus shouting his name, which leads him to frenziedly shout it himself. For 10 points each: In that monologue, Britten has the tuba imitate a foghorn by playing two notes separated by this musical interval.

carreras domingo pavarotti in concert.text: At one point in this album, one of the singers bungled part of di Capua's "O Sole Mio," but was mimicked by the others, saving the show and delighting the audience. For 10 points each: Name this album conducted in 1990 by Zubin Mehta with the orchestras of the Maggio Musicale Fiorentino and Teatro dell'Opera di Roma on the eve of the World Cup final. It became the best-selling classical album of all time.

sull aria.text: This character attempts to thwart the marriage of Masetto and Zerlina by seducing the latter in the duet "La ci darem la mano." For 10 points each: In this duet from another Mozart opera, Susanna writes down a letter dictated by Rosina as she attempts to unveil her husband's infidelity.

intolleranza 1960.text: This opera ends with a choral setting of parts of Brecht's poem "To Posterity," and its 1961 premiere caused a neo-Fascist riot where demonstrators yelled "Viva la polizia!". For 10 points each: Identify this opera by Luigi Nono for tape, orchestra, chorus, and loudspeakers. It set texts by such authors as Jean-Paul Sartre and Paul Eluard, denouncing racism and colonialism.

teatro la fenice.text: This opera ends with a choral setting of parts of Brecht's poem "To Posterity," and its 1961 premiere caused a neo-Fascist riot where demonstrators yelled "Viva la polizia!". For 10 points each: Intolleranza 1960 premiered at this Venice opera house, which received its nickname because it was repeatedly rebuilt after being burnt down.

cherubino.text: In an aria sung by this character, he describes suffering from the feeling of freezing and burning in a way that keeps him up all the time, but in a way that this character nonetheless enjoys. For 10 points each: Name this sex-starved young man who develops strong feelings for all women, including his godmother, which he describes in arias such as "Non so piu cosa son."

This character confesses that "I speak of love while awake, I speak of love while dreaming" in his aria agitata "Non so piu cosa son," and Susanna convinces him to sing "Voi che sapete." For 10 points each: Name this trouser role, a character who escapes through a window while wearing women's clothes. Another character sings "Non piu andrai" to him as he is sent off into the military.

aria parlante.text: In an aria sung by this character, he describes suffering from the feeling of freezing and burning in a way that keeps him up all the time, but in a way that this character nonetheless enjoys. For 10 points each: Cherubino is so overcome with emotion as he sings "Non so piu cosa son" that it comes out as this type of declamatory aria with an Italian name, which is sung breathlessly and rapidly and with little ornamentation.

niccolo piccinni.text: Berlioz suggested that Mozart plagiarized some of the Commendatore's music in Don Giovanni from this opera, in which the title character sings "Divinites du Styx." For 10 points each: In the 1770's-80's, Parisian operagoers were divided between "Gluckists" and fans of this Neapolitan composer of La Cecchina, a favorite of Marie Antoinette. The Paris Opera commissioned him and Gluck to write rival settings of Iphigenie en Tauride.

nightmare song.text: You know what this tournament needs? Some motherfuckin' Gilbert & Sullivan! For 10 points each: In Iolanthe, the Lord Chancellor sings this patter song, which comes after the recitative "Love unrequited." This song describes riding on a bicycle with an eleven-year-old lawyer, and planting a pastry-cook like a tree so that he grows apple puffs.

castrati.text: This aria is addressed to the fronds of a plane tree by the title character of the opera in which it appears. For 10 points each: "Ombra mai fu" is often sung in modern productions by female mezzo-sopranos. But it was originally sung by Caffarelli, who was one of these male singers prized in the 18th century, the most famous of which was Farinelli.

les oiseaux dans la charmille.text: This opera's prologue sets the frame story of the title character telling about his three great loves: Olympia, Antonia, and Giulietta. For 10 points each: Olympia sings this coloratura soprano aria from The Tales of Hoffmann, during which she repeatedly runs down and needs to be re-wound. Its French title roughly translates to "The birds in the arbor."

la forza del destino.text: In this opera, the titular characters sing an a capella rataplan chorus praising their leader Admiral Coligny. For 10 points each: A rataplan chorus is led by the gypsy Preziosilla in this other 19th century opera, which features the prayer "Pace, pace mio Dio", and whose overture begins with a three-note "Fate" motif in the brasses.

jenny lind.text: Operas have a lot of characters, but those only come to life because real people sing the roles. For 10 points each: This 19th-century soprano, known as the "Swedish Nightingale," first gained fame for her 1838 performance in Der Freischutz, but spent the later part of her career as a travelling attraction for P.T. Barnum.

jonas kaufmann.text: Operas have a lot of characters, but those only come to life because real people sing the roles. For 10 points each: This contemporary German tenor is known for his performance of Massenet operas, including a 2014 production of Werther at the Met, and for his recent tour singing Schubert's Winterreise.

princess eboli.text: At the opening of this opera, its title character travels incognito to a forest in Fontainebleau, where he meets his future wife Elisabetta and determines that they both love each other. For 10 points each: In Act II, this princess declares her love for Don Carlo. Most performances of Don Carlo feature this woman wearing an eyepatch, like the real-life woman she was based on.

ghosts of versailles.text: The undercover spy Patrick Honore Begearss appears in this opera, which features a libretto by William Hoffman and was described by its composer as a "grand opera buffa". For 10 points each: Name this John Corigliano opera. It includes an opera-within-an-opera based on La Mere coupable, staged to cheer up the spirit of Marie Antoinette, who is sad about getting guillotined.

swearing an oath of eternal peace betwee.text: Act 4 of this opera includes the Grand Duo's "immortal melody" "Tu l'as dit" and the Blessing of the Daggers, while Act 1 contains the puritanical servant Marcel's brash battle song "Pif! paf!" For 10 points each: At the end of Act 2, Raoul, Nevers, and St.-Bris take this action in a slow section beginning "Par l'honneur" in C minor octaves, as Marcel wishes death on his enemies. A minute after this action, Raoul rejects Valentine, thinking she is Nevers' immoral mistress, and chaos ensues.

humming chorus.text: The leitmotif accompanying this character resembles the opening phrase of "The Star-Spangled Banner." For 10 points each: The Second and Third Acts of Madame Butterfly are bridged by this chorus, which depicts Cio-Cio San waiting up all night for Pinkerton to visit her.

dame nellie melba.text: Enrico Caruso's 1902 recording of this aria was the first record to sell over 1 million copies. For 10 points each: This soprano performed as Nedda in the well-received 1893 Covent Garden staging of Pagliacci. She was Covent Garden's chief soprano for most of the late 19th century and famously refused to sing in German-language operas.

zazie.text: After the "chap" buys this character a "cacocalo," she tells him about testifying at the trial of Jeanne Lalochere, her mother, who had split open her alcoholic father's head to stop him from abusing this character. For 10 points each: Name this title character of a novel who visits her uncle Gabriel, who crossdresses and dances The Dying Swan at a gay nightclub. When a workers' strike ends, she finally gets to ride on public transportation in Paris.

raymond queneau.text: After the "chap" buys this character a "cacocalo," she tells him about testifying at the trial of Jeanne Lalochere, her mother, who had split open her alcoholic father's head to stop him from abusing this character. For 10 points each: Zazie in the Metro is a novel by this French member of Oulipo, who retold a story about an experience on the S Bus 99 times in his Exercises in Style.

ottone.text: This character cross-dresses to kill his ex-girlfriend under orders from the empress. For 10 points each: Name this man who wants to get back together with that ex-girlfriend but has to marry Drusilla instead.

coronation of poppaea.text: This character cross-dresses to kill his ex-girlfriend under orders from the empress. For 10 points each: Ottone is a character in this opera, whose most notable number is the duet "Pur ti miro, pur ti godo." That number sees the title character replace Empress Ottavia as wife of Nero after everyone involved in the murder plot is exiled.

love for three oranges.text: Princesses emerge from this work's title objects; two die of thirst and one is turned into a giant rat. For 10 points each: Name this satirical opera in which Leandro and Smeraldina help Fata Morgana try to kill the Prince because he laughed when she fell over.

Tchelio repeatedly loses a card game in this opera, which begins with a frame story involving the ridicules intervening in a play. For 10 points each: Name this opera based on a play of the same name by Carlo Gozzi. It tells the story of the title curse made on the Prince by Fata Morgana.

A prince in this opera does not laugh at anything until Fata Morgana falls down and exposes her underwear. For 10 points each: Name this opera, in which a fairy princess named Ninette emerges from one of the title fruit, is turned into a giant rat by Morgana, and almost watches the Prince marry the disguised servant Smeraldina.

sergei prokofiev.text: Princesses emerge from this work's title objects; two die of thirst and one is turned into a giant rat. For 10 points each: This Russian composer of The Love for Three Oranges and Peter and the Wolf also created a suite for a film in which a bureaucratic typo causes clerks to fake a military officer's life, Lieutenant Kije.

fiery angel.text: Princesses emerge from this work's title objects; two die of thirst and one is turned into a giant rat. For 10 points each: Prokofiev also composed this opera, in which Ruprecht falls in love with Renata, who believes that Otterheim is an heavenly creature with whom she fell in love.

la donna e mobile.text: When the melody of this aria is introduced, the last bar is conspicuously silent. For 10 points each: Name this aria in which the Duke of Mantua compares the title figure to a feather in the wind.

This scene is followed by Azucena's singing of "Stride la vampa," which declares her desire to exact revenge on the Count. For 10 points each: This song from Rigoletto is sung by the Duke of Mantua. Its title is commonly translated as "woman is fickle."

A reprise of this aria is heard when Rigoletto opens a sack to discover his daughter's dead body. For 10 points each: Name this aria sung by the Duke of Mantua, whose title translates as "woman is fickle."

sparafucile.text: When the melody of this aria is introduced, the last bar is conspicuously silent. For 10 points each: Rigoletto pays this assassin to murder his master the Duke, but he instead kills Gilda after the latter agrees to take the Duke's place.

A reprise of this aria is heard when Rigoletto opens a sack to discover his daughter's dead body. For 10 points each: Rigoletto sings the duet "Pari Siamo" with this incompetent assassin, who is hired to kill the Duke of Mantua, but is convinced by his sister Maddalena to kill the next person who enters the room. Unfortunately for Rigoletto, that person is his daughter Gilda.

libiamo ne lieti calici.text: This opera's first act closes with the title character rejecting a potential lover in the aria "Sempre libera." For 10 points each: La traviata opens with a party in which Violetta and Alfredo join in a duet to sing this drinking song beckoning others to enjoy wine, song, laughter, and the beautiful night.

camellia.text: This opera's first act closes with the title character rejecting a potential lover in the aria "Sempre libera." For 10 points each: At the end of the first act of La Traviata, Violetta gives Alfredo this specific flower and tells him to return it to her when it has wilted. The original novel by Alexandre Dumas, fils that the opera is based on contains this flower in the title.

eugene scribe.text: At the start of Act Two in this opera, two characters entertain guests at a wedding feast by singing a barca-rolle that starts, "I am rich and you are beautiful." For 10 points each: The libretto of The Elixir of Love is an Italian translation of a French libretto by this dramatist, who closely collaborated with Giacomo Meyerbeer on projects such as Le Prophete and Les Huguenots.

furtive tear.text: At the start of Act Two in this opera, two characters entertain guests at a wedding feast by singing a barca-rolle that starts, "I am rich and you are beautiful." For 10 points each: Nemorino finally knows that Adina cares for him when he notices this object, about which he sings an aria. This object is caused by Adina seeing Nemorino surrounded by girls.

suzuki.text: The singer of this aria claims that she will not respond when her name is called out, both to tease her hus-band and to keep herself from being overwhelmed by her excitement. For 10 points each: Butterfly sings "Un bel di" to this servant, who tries to tell Butterfly not to expect Pinkerton to return. When he does, Butterfly and this character sing a duet while spreading flowers around.

la rondine.text: The male lead of this opera writes his name on a tablecloth below the name of his lover, who is given the title moniker by the poet Prunier during a palm reading. For 10 points each: Name this incomplete opera in which Magda breaks off her relationship with Ruggero because she is too much of a free spirit, represented by the title creature.

In this opera, Minnie cheats against a sheriff in a poker game in order to win the life of her lover. For ten points each: Puccini also wrote this opera about the courtesan Magda, who disguises herself as the working girl Pauletta in order to win the love of Ruggero but eventually leaves him because she fears that her past would hurt his family.

musetta s waltz.text: The male lead of this opera writes his name on a tablecloth below the name of his lover, who is given the title moniker by the poet Prunier during a palm reading. For 10 points each: A woman from another Puccini opera pretends that her shoe is too tight and sends off Alcindoro to the shoemaker immediately after singing this aria, in which she states that everyone stares at her beauty when she walks down the street.

good friday music.text: Engelbert Humperdinck composed extra music for the transition between the first two scenes in early performances of this opera, whose main soprano role first appears carrying a vial of balsam from Arabia. For 10 points each: The Prelude to Act 1 of Parsifal is sometimes performed in conjunction with this musical excerpt from Act III, which plays when Parsifal returns to the castle of the Grail bearing the Holy Spear.

ballet of the nuns.text: This operatic excerpt made use of the recently-installed gas lighting at the Paris Opera, and secured the fame of Marie Taglioni. For 10 points each: Name this episode from the third act of a 1831 opera, in which Bertram goes to a cloister and summons the title vow-breaking ghosts from their tombs, before the Abbess orders them to dance.

ballet tutu.text: This operatic excerpt made use of the recently-installed gas lighting at the Paris Opera, and secured the fame of Marie Taglioni. For 10 points each: The "Ballet of the Nuns" is widely held to be the first work in the romantic ballet genre, which introduced this innovation to the art form.A The pancake and the bell styles are common variants of this aspect of ballet.

cielo e mar.text: In 1982, Carlo Bini was roundly booed at the Met upon singing this area after replacing Placido Domingo onstage. For 10 points each: Name this tenor aria which is sung in Act II by Enzo, who awaits the arrival of Laura aboard his ship.

new moon.text: The protagonist of this operetta instills the spirit of the French Revolution into his comrades with the marching song "Stouthearted Men." For 10 points each: Name this operetta with music by Sigmund Romberg, in which Robert Misson founds a republic near New Orleans after being rescued from the title ship. Its songs "Lover Come Back to Me" and "Softly as in a Morning Sunrise" became jazz standards.

jerome kern.text: The protagonist of this operetta instills the spirit of the French Revolution into his comrades with the marching song "Stouthearted Men." For 10 points each: The songs "Ol' Man River" and "Can't Help Lovin' Dat Man" appear in Show Boat, a 1927 musical with words by Hammerstein and music by this composer, who wrote "The Way You Look Tonight" for the Fred Astaire and Ginger Rogers film Swing Time.

ebben ne andro lontana.text: The title character of this opera falls in love with Giuseppe Hagenbach, the enemy of her father Stromminger. For 10 points each: Near the end of the first act, Wally sings this famous aria after she decides to leave her home rather than marry a man she does not love. She follows it up by departing for the mountains.

agrippina.text: This character threatens to disown her daughter Pamina if she does not kill Sarastro in the aria "Der Holle Rache kocht in meinem Herzen." For 10 points each: This coloratura soprano role, the title character of an early Handel opera, sings "Se vuoi pace" and "L'alma mia fra le tempeste." She plots against Ottone, and her son sings "Come nube che fugge."

bird.text: In the second act of this opera, a group of women sing the tavern song "Les tringles des sistres tintaient." For 10 points each: When Carmen appears in the first act, she sings an aria commonly known as the Habanera. In a descending chromatic figure, she compares love to a rebellious one of these creatures in the aria's first line.

temistocle solera.text: His opera Nabucco is based on stories from the Book of Jeremiah and the Book of Daniel. For 10 points each: Giuseppe Verdi often collaborated with this librettist for his historical operas, such as Nabucco, Giovanna d'Arco, and Atilia. This man claimed that Verdi was a "tyrant" and would often lock him up in a room to write.

powder her face.text: Philip Hensher wrote the libretto to this opera, which ends with an electrician and a maid destroying its setting, a lavish hotel room. 10 points each: Name this chamber opera by Thomas Ades, which musically depicts fellatio in its treatment of the "Dirty Duchess of Argyll."

covent garden opera house.text: Philip Hensher wrote the libretto to this opera, which ends with an electrician and a maid destroying its setting, a lavish hotel room. 10 points each: Another opera by Thomas Ades, The Tempest, premiered at this opera house in London. Its current music director is Sir Antonio Pappano.

tobias picker.text: Answer some questions about controversies involving opera composers, for 10 points each. The San Francisco Opera premiered this composer's Dolores Claiborne, which contains a scene where the title character is molested on stage. In 2005, he composed an opera based on An American Tragedy.

hans werner henze.text: Answer some questions about controversies involving opera composers, for 10 points each. As riots broke out when his oratorio The Raft of the Medusa premiered, this composer led a chant of "Ho, Ho, Ho Chi Minh!" His other works include the one-act opera The Bassarids.

l enfant et les sortileges fantaisie lyr.text: This opera ends with the protagonist singing "Maman!" succeeding the fugue "Il est bon, l'enfant, il est sage" and a bunch of parallel fourths in the oboes. For 10 points each: Name this opera, in which a kid finds that his toys come to life after he throws them around during a tantrum. It amusingly features an Chinese cup and Wedgwood teapot that speak a bizarre English pidgin, and a duet sung entirely in cat sounds.

joseph maurice ravel.text: This opera ends with the protagonist singing "Maman!" succeeding the fugue "Il est bon, l'enfant, il est sage" and a bunch of parallel fourths in the oboes. For 10 points each: This French composer set Colette's libretto in L'Enfant et les Sortileges, though he may be better known for ballets such as Daphnis et Chloe.

l heure espagnole.text: This opera ends with the protagonist singing "Maman!" succeeding the fugue "Il est bon, l'enfant, il est sage" and a bunch of parallel fourths in the oboes. For 10 points each: Ravel's only other finished opera was this work, in which Torquemada's wife Concepcion falls for the muleteer Ramiro after he carries two clocks, in which the poet Gonzalve and the banker Don Inigo are hiding.

bohemian girl.text: In this opera, Arline, kidnapped sweetheart of the Polish noble Thaddeus, dreams "that you loved me still the same" while reminiscing about her childhood in the court of her father, Count Arnhelm. For 10 points each: Name this opera by Michael William Balfe most famous for its aria "I Dreamt I Dwelt in Marble Halls."

ireland.text: In this opera, Arline, kidnapped sweetheart of the Polish noble Thaddeus, dreams "that you loved me still the same" while reminiscing about her childhood in the court of her father, Count Arnhelm. For 10 points each: Balfe was from this island, the home of John Field, who wrote the first nocturnes. Victor Herbert was born on this island, which is also home to flautist James Galway.

barbarina.text: This character confesses that "I speak of love while awake, I speak of love while dreaming" in his aria agitata "Non so piu cosa son," and Susanna convinces him to sing "Voi che sapete." For 10 points each: Cherubino initially incurs Count Almaviva's wrath by flirting with this daughter of the gardener Antonio, who sings "L'ho perduta, me meschina" after losing a pin she was supposed to deliver to Susanna.

thieving magpie.text: The actions of this animal provoke the prayer Deh, tu reggi in tal momento. For 10 points each: Name this animal, whose nest houses a silver spoon and a silver coin. Until it is found, Ninetta is imprisoned for suspected theft.

perfect american.text: This opera begins with women writing equations in the air and later features a man delivering a soliloquy on love from a bus. For ten points each: This other opera by the same composer as Einstein on the Beach depicts Walt Disney on his death bed; it discusses the conspiracy theory that his body was cryogenically stored.

anvil chorus.text: An opera by this composer opens with Ferrando narrating how a gypsy was falsely burned at the stake, on accusations of causing the illness of the Count di Luna. For 10 points each: The second act of Il Trovatore opens with a chorus of gypsies singing this number, in which they praise wine and gypsy women.

This song is sometimes known by its first line, "Vedi! Le fosche notturne", and it describes how women turn days from gloom to bright sunshine. For 10 points each: Identify this piece from act 2, scene 1 of Il Trovatore, in which a group of Spanish Gypsies set to work.

This scene is followed by Azucena's singing of "Stride la vampa," which declares her desire to exact revenge on the Count. For 10 points each: Name this song from an opera. It is officially called "Vedi! Le fosche notturne spoglie."

This cabaletta is sung after Ruiz brings the news that a gypsy woman is going to be burned at the stake. For 10 points each: The opera containing "Di quella pira" also features this popular piece, which depicts gypsies striking the title objects. It begins "Vedi! Le fosche notturne."

angela gheorghiu.text: An opera by this composer opens with Ferrando narrating how a gypsy was falsely burned at the stake, on accusations of causing the illness of the Count di Luna. For 10 points each: This Romanian soprano made her premiere recording as Violetta in La Traviata in 1994. Her 2002 recording of Il Trovatore was made with her on-again-off-again husband, Spanish tenor Roberto Alagna.

taran tara taran tara.text: This man is the father of Mabel, who he offers in marriage to Frederick before the reprise of "Poor wand'ring ones" in the Act II finale of the work he appears in. For 10 points each: In The Pirates of Penzance, this four-syllable chant is sung by the chorus of police throughout Act II, undergirding such songs as "When the foeman bears his steel" and "With cat-like tread".

largo al factotum.text: In its final stanza, its singer claims to be "swifter and swifter, like a thunderbolt", while earlier, he claims that "razors and combs, lancets and scissors, are at my command". For 10 points each: Name this difficult, triplet-filled baritone aria, sung by a character who repeats his own name several times before the final patter section.

der holle rache kocht in meinem herzen.text: Identify the following things about Mozart's operas, for 10 points each. This aria from The Magic Flute requires the soprano playing the Queen of the Night to hit a high F6. In it, the Queen orders Pamina to assassinate her rival Sarastro.

At the beginning of this opera, Tamino is saved from a serpent by three servants of the Queen of the Night. For 10 points each: The Queen of the Night sings this technically difficult aria when Pamina refuses her order to murder Sarastro. Reaching F6 several times, it tells of disowning Pamina if she disobeys.

being burned at the stake.text: This song is sometimes known by its first line, "Vedi! Le fosche notturne", and it describes how women turn days from gloom to bright sunshine. For 10 points each: Il Trovatore also features the aria "Di quella pira", in which Manrico vows to save his mother Azucena from this fate. This fate also befalls Pollione and Norma at the end of a Vincenzo Bellini opera.

l elisir d amore or the elixir of love.text: In this work, the peasant Nemorino is in love with Adina, but she only reciprocates when she finds out that he joined the army in order to pay a charlatan for wine he believes to be a magic potion. For 10 points each: Nemorino sings the aria "Una furtiva lacrima" in this opera, after believing Adina has finally fallen in love with him.

magnet.text: Antonio Salieri set part of this opera's libretto to music, but gave up in a hissy fit. For 10 points each: After pretending to poison themselves, the Albanians are "revived" by one of these objects brought in by the maid Despina, disguised as a doctor, in a ludicrous scene that unfolds at the end of Act I.

micaela.text: This character prays to God to protect her in her Act 3 aria "Je dis que rien ne m'epouvante." For 10 points each: Name this soprano role that was not present in the original novella by Prosper Merimee. As an innocent village maiden, she serves as a foil to the brash and indecent title character.

escamillo.text: In Act Three of the opera in which he appears, this character has his hat shot off of his head by his rival, Don Jose. For 10 points each: Name this bullfighter, who sings "Toreador, en garde!" in his aria, "Votre toast, je peux vous le rendre".

un ballo in maschera.text: In this opera, Renato changes his mind about killing Amelia and changes his ire towards Riccardo in the aria "It was you who stained this soul." For 10 points each: Name this Giuseppe Verdi opera which ends at the title event, when Oscar reveals after "You want to know how he is dressed" that Riccardo is wearing a black cloak and red ribbon to the title event.

gustav iii.text: In this opera, Renato changes his mind about killing Amelia and changes his ire towards Riccardo in the aria "It was you who stained this soul." For 10 points each: A Masked Ball was originally about the 1792 assassination of this king of Sweden, who was in fact killed by Jacob Johan Anckarstrom at a masked ball.

boston.text: In this opera, Renato changes his mind about killing Amelia and changes his ire towards Riccardo in the aria "It was you who stained this soul." For 10 points each: To escape Italian censorship, the first decades of performances of A Masked Ball changed Gustav III to the Earl of Warwick and the setting from Stockholm to this city, where Charles Ives's movement "St.-Gaudens" from Three Places in New England is set.

mother of us all.text: Characters in this opera include Lillian Russell and Daniel Webster. For 10 points each: Identify this Virgil Thomson opera, whose title character tells of her life of "effort and strife" in its finale, "My long life".

gertrude stein.text: Characters in this opera include Lillian Russell and Daniel Webster. For 10 points each: The Mother of Us All was a collaboration between Virgil Thomson and this writer. Goings on at her Paris salon were described in Hemmingway's A Moveable Feast.

juilliard school of music.text: Characters in this opera include Lillian Russell and Daniel Webster. For 10 points each: Thomson's later opera Lord Byron was first performed at this institution. This institution is named for a textile merchant and is currently headed by Joseph Polisi, the author of The Artist as Citizen.

goyescas los majos enamorados opus 11.text: This suite was later adapted into an opera about the lovers Rosario and Fernando. For 10 points each: Name this piano suite that includes "Compliments" and a "Ballad of Love and Death." It was written by Enrique Granados.

new york city.text: This suite was later adapted into an opera about the lovers Rosario and Fernando. For 10 points each: Goyescas premiered at an opera in this city with tenor Giovanni Martinelli as Fernando and baritone Giuseppi De Luca as Paquiro. Three years later, De Luca premiered Puccini's Gianni Schicchi at an opera in this city.

surtitles.text: This system was first used at a 1983 production by the company of Lotfi Mansouri, who placed a video screen above the proscenium. For 10 points each: Name this system used to keep operatic audiences aware of what the lyrics mean.

james.text: This system was first used at a 1983 production by the company of Lotfi Mansouri, who placed a video screen above the proscenium. For 10 points each: This music director of the Met said of surtitles, "Over my dead body will they show those things at this house," but he was mollified by Met Titles, which have individual display screens on the back of the seats. He has led more than 2,000 performances at the Met since 1971.

adolphe.text: He wrote an opera about the romance between the poor fisherman Zephoris and the Princess of Goa, Nemea; that is his Si j'etais roi. For 10 points each: Name this French composer. The ghost of the title peasant maid saves Prince Albrecht from the revenge of spirits called the Wilis in his Giselle.

le corsaire.text: He wrote an opera about the romance between the poor fisherman Zephoris and the Princess of Goa, Nemea; that is his Si j'etais roi. For 10 points each: This ballet by Adam features Conrad's rescue of his beloved from the Seid Pasha. Its pas de deux between Medora and the slave Ali is perhaps the most popular pas de deux in all of French classical ballet.

riccardo drigo.text: He wrote an opera about the romance between the poor fisherman Zephoris and the Princess of Goa, Nemea; that is his Si j'etais roi. For 10 points each: The pas de deux for the Petipa version of Le Corsaire was arranged by this Italian composer, who re-arranged the music for the Petipa version of Swan Lake, and who himself composed The Awakening of Flora.

o mio babbino caro.text: This composer had a collaborative friendship with Arturo Toscanini, who conducted the premiere of his opera, La Boheme. For 10 points each: In this famous aria from Gianni Schicchi, Lauretta threatens to throw herself into the Arno river if her father will not consent to her marriage with Rinuccio.

luigi illica.text: This composer had a collaborative friendship with Arturo Toscanini, who conducted the premiere of his opera, La Boheme. For 10 points each: This playwright, who wrote the libretto for Giordano's Andrea Chenier, collaborated with Giuseppe Giacosa in writing the libretti for most of Puccini's greatest hits, including La Boheme, Tosca, and Madama Butterfly.

alcindoro.text: This character receives a pink bonnet, which she reminisces over while lamenting her lover's abandonment in the aria "Donde Lieta Usci." For 10 points each: Musetta performs "Quando M'en Vo" while at Cafe Momus with this man, her sugar daddy. The characters eventually run out of the cafe, leaving this man to pay their bill.

daughter of the regiment.text: Answer the following about these operas with crazy hard arias, for 10 points each. This opera's aria "Ah mes amis" is nicknamed the "Mount Everest for tenors" because the tenor sings a total of 9 Cs an octave above middle C. The title character of this opera, Marie, saves the life of her Tyrolean lover Tonio.

magic bells.text: At the beginning of this opera, Tamino is saved from a serpent by three servants of the Queen of the Night. For 10 points each: The Queen of the Night gives Papageno this item, which he uses to cause the pursuing Monostatos to happily dance away. He also uses this to summon Papagena after being persuaded not to commit suicide.

alberich.text: In this opera, the orchestra plays the "death-curse" leitmotif when Fafner kills Fasolt. For 10 points each: This dwarf forswears love after being mocked by the Rhine maidens, and forges the ring from the Rheingold.

loge.text: In this opera, the orchestra plays the "death-curse" leitmotif when Fafner kills Fasolt. For 10 points each: Alberich is tricked by this character to transform into a toad using the powers of the Tarnhelm. Alberich is promptly captured by this character and his companion Wotan.

all that gold.text: This aria is sung by the mother of the title character. For 10 points each: Name this aria, in which that woman wonders how a group of gift givers cannot understand the troubles of the poor and proceeds to be caught while stealing some of the namesake substance.

force of destiny.text: Several characters in this opera are chided into enlisting in the army by a gypsy in the aria "Al suon del tamburo." For 10 points each: Name this opera centering on the forbidden love between Don Alvaro and Leonora. It ends after Alvaro mortally wounds Don Carlos, who sought revenge for the accidental death of his father at Alvaro's hands.

francesco maria piave.text: Several characters in this opera are chided into enlisting in the army by a gypsy in the aria "Al suon del tamburo." For 10 points each: The libretto for The Force of Destiny was written by this man, who also provided the librettos for Rigoletto and La Traviata.

tonio.text: This aria is sung after one character discovers that his love agrees to run away with Silvio. For 10 points each: This other character confesses his love to Nedda, but it is ultimately rejected. At the beginning of the opera, he addresses the audience in the aria "Si puo?," reminding them that the actors are actually human.

masked ball.text: This scene is followed by Azucena's singing of "Stride la vampa," which declares her desire to exact revenge on the Count. For 10 points each: Riccardo's "Laughing Aria" appears in this other Verdi opera. In this opera, Riccardo is destined to be killed by Renato, the husband of Riccardo's beloved Amelia, after shaking Renato's hand.

Marian Anderson broke the color barrier at the Met by singing the fortuneteller Ulrica in this opera. For 10 points each: Name this opera in which Riccardo declares his love for Amelia before being fatally stabbed by Amelia's husband Renato. Its original subject, the assassination of Gustav III, was censored, caused its composer to change the setting to colonial Boston.

consul.text: A magician in this opera tries to gain access to the title character but ends up frightening a woman instead. For 10 points each: Name this opera about Magda's struggle to get permission to leave the country with her political dissident husband, John. The opera ends with Magda committing suicide using her gas oven.

medium.text: A magician in this opera tries to gain access to the title character but ends up frightening a woman instead. For 10 points each: This other Menotti opera is about Madame Flora who pretends to be the title spiritualist but ends up hearing voices that cause her to shoot and kill Toby.

sergei sergeyevich prokofiev.text: Tchelio repeatedly loses a card game in this opera, which begins with a frame story involving the ridicules intervening in a play. For 10 points each: The Love for Three Oranges is an opera by this composer of Peter and the Wolf.

A prince in this opera does not laugh at anything until Fata Morgana falls down and exposes her underwear. For 10 points each: The Love for Three Oranges is a work by this composer, who also created a ballet version of Romeo and Juliet and Peter and the Wolf.

ninette.text: Tchelio repeatedly loses a card game in this opera, which begins with a frame story involving the ridicules intervening in a play. For 10 points each: This character in The Love for Three Oranges is loved by the Prince, but she gets turned into a rat by Fata Morgana, so the Prince has to instead marry Smeraldina.

ottorino respighi.text: Although he wrote such operas as The Sunken Bell, this composer is better known for his orchestral works such as Gli uccelli, or The Birds, as well as a work that contains such sections as "Circuses" and "October Festival." For 10 points each: Name this Italian composer who is best remembered for a "trilogy" of works that attempt to visually depict a certain city. One of those works depicts various fountains, while another depicts trees near the Villa Borghesse.

lute.text: Although he wrote such operas as The Sunken Bell, this composer is better known for his orchestral works such as Gli uccelli, or The Birds, as well as a work that contains such sections as "Circuses" and "October Festival." For 10 points each: A set of suites by Respighi titled Ancient Airs and Dances is largely based off of ancient music for this stringed instrument, whose European variety features a pegbox bent away from the neck and rounded body. The mandolin is a descendant of this instrument.

oh what a beautiful mornin.text: The female protagonist of this work tries to forget her love interest while showering in "I'm Gonna Wash That Man Right Out of My Hair". For 10 points each: Oklahoma unusually opens with this solo song for Curly McClain that describes corn "as high as an elephant's eye".

giuseppe.text: One character in this opera rejoices in the name of her beloved in "Caro Nome". For 10 points each: Rigoletto is by this Italian opera composer of Aida.

it ain t necessarily so.text: This aria's singer states that "Your daddy's rich, and your mamma's good looking." For 10 points each: Sportin' Life earlier casts aspersions on the stories of David and Goliath, Jonah and the whale, and Moses's discovery by Pharoah's daughter in this song from Porgy and Bess.

sotto voce.text: Name these techniques used in singing classical music and opera, for 10 points each: The Lacrimosa from Mozart's Requiem calls for this technique, a dramatic lowering of one's singing voice for emphasis.

sharpless.text: This character sings the aria "One Beautiful Day," in which she foretells the coming of a ship, and she later asks another character about her beloved's behavior with the analogy of a nesting robin. For 10 points each: In Madame Butterfly, this is the character whom Butterfly asks about robins' nesting behaviors in America. He is an American consul who accompanies Pinkerton to Butterfly's house in the third act.

madamina il catologo e questo.text: The title character of this opera tries to woo a maid while accompanying himself on the mandolin in "Deh vieni alla finestra." For 10 points each: Leporello sings this aria, which claims that his master slept with 1,003 Spanish women, along with significant numbers from other countries.

bryn terfel.text: The title character of this opera tries to woo a maid while accompanying himself on the mandolin in "Deh vieni alla finestra." For 10 points each: Figaro and Don Giovanni were both signature roles of this Welsh bass-baritone singer for a time. He recently starred as Wotan in the Met's new production of the Ring Cycle.

cabaletta.text: Examples of this type of piece include "Non piu mesta" from Rossini's La Cenerentola and "Di quella pira" from Verdi's Il trovatore. For 10 points each: Give this term for the second half of a double aria in Italian opera. It was usually faster and more virtuosic than the preceding cantabile, making it useful for representing intense emotion and generating applause.

sempre libera.text: Examples of this type of piece include "Non piu mesta" from Rossini's La Cenerentola and "Di quella pira" from Verdi's Il trovatore. For 10 points each: Another cabaletta is this one in which Violetta sings of her resolve to "frolic from joy to joy," free from Alfredo's constraining love.

dalibor.text: In this opera, Milada brings a fiddle to the title character and falls in love with him, though she had earlier demanded his death. For 10 points: Name this opera whose title character is thrown in jail after killing an enemy Burgrave in revenge for the death of his friend Zdenek.

brandenburgers in bohemia.text: In this opera, Milada brings a fiddle to the title character and falls in love with him, though she had earlier demanded his death. For 10 points: In this other opera by the composer of Dalibor, Varneman and his warriors are called in to fight against the Habsburgs but are driven out by the work's end.

porgi amor qualche ristoro.text: In one opera, this character sings "Contessa, perdono". For 10 points each: The other opera is Mozart's The Marriage of Figaro in which the Countess bemoans Almaviva's intended infidelity in this aria, which opens the second act.

adriana lecouvreur.text: The title character of this opera dies after sniffing poisoned flowers sent by her rival, who also loves Maurizio. For 10 points each: Name this opera whose central conflict stems from a love triangle involving the Princess of Bouillon, the Count of Saxony, and the title actress.

eri tu che macchiavi.text: Marian Anderson broke the color barrier at the Met by singing the fortuneteller Ulrica in this opera. For 10 points each: At the beginning of the third act of A Masked Ball, Renato sings this aria in which he resolves to seek vengeance on Riccardo before joining the conspirators.

guiseppe verdi.text: Marian Anderson broke the color barrier at the Met by singing the fortuneteller Ulrica in this opera. For 10 points each: This Italian composer wrote A Masked Ball, as well as operas such as Otello and La Traviata.

sleepwalker.text: In 1949, this soprano shocked the opera world by singing Elvira in I Puritani just days after performing as Brunnhilde in Die Walkure. For 10 points each: Callas was renowned for performing as Amina, the title character of this Vincenzo Bellini opera. It includes the aria "Ah! non credea," in which Amina sings about her love for Elvino.

dame joan sutherland.text: In 1949, this soprano shocked the opera world by singing Elvira in I Puritani just days after performing as Brunnhilde in Die Walkure. For 10 points each: This Australian coloratura soprano was also known for her bel canto repertoire, and was nicknamed "La Stupenda" after singing the title role of Handel's Alcina at Le Fenice. In 1959, she became famous for performing the title role of Zeffirelli's production of Lucia di Lammermoor.

roberto devereux.text: The lead soprano roles of this opera, Anna Bolena, and Maria Stuarda are often known as the "Three Queens," and Beverly Sills performed all three as a series at the New York City Opera. For 10 points each: Name this opera in which a blue scarf convinces Elizabeth I that the title character is in love with another woman. Elizabeth sings the mad scene "Vivi ingrato...Quel sangue versato" after ordering the title character's execution.

di quella pira.text: This cabaletta is sung after Ruiz brings the news that a gypsy woman is going to be burned at the stake. For 10 points each: Name this piece, whose singer exhorts the people around him to help him put out the blaze.

cavalleria rusticana or rustic chivalry.text: In this opera, set on Easter morning, the male lead has seduced Santuzza to get back at his old girlfriend, leading her to seek comfort with Lucia. For 10 points each: Name this opera by Pietro Mascagni which narrates the story of Lola, Santuzza, and the men who duel over them. It is based on a short story by Giovanni Verga.

khovanschina or the khovansky affair.text: The title role of this opera was considered the signature role of both Mark Reizen and a singer surnamed Christoff who happened to share his first name with that character. For 10 points each: This other Godunov opera is mainly known today for its Prelude depicting dawn on the Moskova River, as well as the "Dance of the Persian Slaves." The plot concerns a rebellion by the Straltsy and Old Believers against Peter the Great.

The title role of this opera was considered the signature role of both Mark Reizen and a singer surnamed Christoff who happened to share his first name with that character. For 10 points each: This other Godunov opera is mainly known today for its Prelude depicting dawn on the Moskova River, as well as the "Dance of the Persian Slaves." The plot concerns a rebellion by the Straltsy and Old Believers against Peter the Great.

lucio silla.text: For 10 points each, name these operas by Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart. This 1772 Mozart opera tells the story of the namesake Roman dictator and his lust for Giunia, daughter of his archrival Gaius Marius.

la gazza ladra.text: This opera ends with shots firing that turn out to be rejoicing and not those of a firing squad. For 10 points each: Name this opera in which the servant Ninetta is sentenced to death by the villainous Mayor after the title creature is responsible for taking a silver spoon. It features the aria "Deh, tu reggi in tal momento."

la cenerentola ossia la bontia in trionf.text: This opera ends with shots firing that turn out to be rejoicing and not those of a firing squad. For 10 points each: Rossini wrote this opera in which the title character is the stepdaughter of Don Magnifico and forced to work as a maid, but finds happiness in the hands of Prince Ramiro. The title character's real name is Angelina.

il trittico.text: One opera by this composer has Scarpia betray the title character and allow Cavaradossi to be executed; in another, the title princess presents potential suitors with three riddles, having those who fail to correctly answer them executed. For 10 points each: These three one-act Puccini operas are often performed together. They include Gianni Schicchi, Suor Angelica, and Il Tabarro.

reinhold gliere.text: A prince in this opera does not laugh at anything until Fata Morgana falls down and exposes her underwear. For 10 points each: Prokofiev took private lessons from this Soviet composer, who did the music for the ballet The Red Poppy.

w.text: Their first collaboration was a work called Thespis, or the Gods Grown Old. For 10 points each: Name these two men, the composer and librettist of operettas including Iolanthe, The Yeomen of the Guard, and The Pirates of Penzance.

der kaiser von atlantis or the emperor o.text: Viktor Ullmann composed this opera while imprisoned at the Terezin concentration camp. FTPE: Name this one-act opera in which Death is so disgusted by modern, mechanized war that he goes on strike, and consents to return only if the title character, who has ordered all-out war, becomes his first victim.

savitri.text: Viktor Ullmann composed this opera while imprisoned at the Terezin concentration camp. FTPE: In this English-language opera inspired by the Mahabharata, Death comes to claim the title character's husband Satyavan, but she wins him back through clever bargaining. Its composer previously drew on Hindu sources for his opera Sita.

iolanthe.text: This character suffers a nightmare in which he rides a bicycle in Salisbury Plain with his eleven-year old attorney, who explains a scheme for planting tradesmen like trees so that they grow their products like fruit. FTPE: The Lord Chancellor appears in this Gilbert & Sullivan operetta, in which Strephon becomes a member of parliament while trying to win Phyllis from the House of Lords and disguise the fact that his mother is the title fairy.

l arlesienne.text: Georges Bizet reworked his incidental music to this novel into two orchestral suites, and it was first published a year after Le Petit Chose as part of Tales from My Windmill. For 10 points each: Identify this work of Alphonse Daudet whose most famous operatic treatment, which sees Frederico's self-defenestration over the cheating title character, was composed by Francesco Cilea.

flower.text: Georges Bizet reworked his incidental music to this novel into two orchestral suites, and it was first published a year after Le Petit Chose as part of Tales from My Windmill. For 10 points each: The title character of Cilea's Adriana Lecouvreur dies after kissing poisoned objects of this type. The title character performs a duet named for these objects with Malika in Delibes's Lakme, while a specific type of this general object makes Octavian the title character in Der Rosenkavalier.

franz lehar.text: He included the "Gold and Silver Waltz" in The Count of Luxembourg, but that piece is often included in another of his operettas. For 10 points each: Name this Austrian composer who wrote the song "You'll Find Me at Maxim's," as well as an operetta about Paganini's fictional affair with the Duchess of Lucha entitled Paganini.

love.text: In this opera's third scene, Wotan and Loge travel to Nibelheim and kidnap Alberich in order to pay Fasolt and Fafnir for constructing Valhalla. For 10 points each: Das Rheingold begins with Alberich renouncing this emotion in order to steal the Rhine gold. The Ring cycle ends with a leitmotif usually called "redemption through" this emotion.

erda.text: In this opera's third scene, Wotan and Loge travel to Nibelheim and kidnap Alberich in order to pay Fasolt and Fafnir for constructing Valhalla. For 10 points each: In Das Rheingold, Wotan reluctantly gives the ring to Fasolt and Fafnir after this primeval goddess rises from the ground and warns him about the ring's curse, predicting the downfall of the gods.

louise.text: In this opera's third act, the title character sings "Depuis le jour" and is crowned Queen of Montmartre by a group of Bohemians. For 10 points each: Name this opera by Gustave Charpentier about the title seamstress's love for the artist Julien.

verismo opera.text: In this opera's third act, the title character sings "Depuis le jour" and is crowned Queen of Montmartre by a group of Bohemians. For 10 points each: Louise is often considered a French example of this type of opera, which focuses on realistic subjects and became popular in the late 19th century. In Italy, examples of this type of opera include Pagliacci and Cavalleria Rusticana.

caro nome.text: A reprise of this aria is heard when Rigoletto opens a sack to discover his daughter's dead body. For 10 points each: In the first act of Rigoletto, Gilda sings this aria, in which she declares her love for a student named Gualtier Malde, who is actually the Duke of Mantua in disguise. As foreshadowing, most of its coloratura passages occur on the word "die."

giaochino antonio rossini.text: This opera experimented with a "good" and "bad" ending named after Venice and Ferrara respectively, in which the title character either lives or dies during a battle with Sicily. For 10 points each: Stendhal praised Tancredi as the masterpiece of this Italian composer of The Thieving Magpie and William Tell.

turk in italy.text: This opera experimented with a "good" and "bad" ending named after Venice and Ferrara respectively, in which the title character either lives or dies during a battle with Sicily. For 10 points each: This Rossini opera opens with the poet Prosdocimo searching for a topic to write about, and subsequently meeting Zaida, a former member of the title character's harem. It ends happily with the reuniting of Fiorilla and Geronio.
